metadata.canto_summary: (Ayodhyakanda)
(Ayodhyakanda)

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, having sent his sons Bharata and Satrughna to their maternal grandfather's kingdom, began to consider appointing Rama as his successor.  He consulted with a council of princes on this matter. Meanwhile, Bharata and Satrughna, though enjoying their stay, still fondly remembered their father.  Dasaratha, in turn, missed his sons, all four of whom he loved equally.  However, Rama, a virtuous and glorious prince, brought him particular joy. Rama was described as a divine incarnation, born to vanquish the evil Ravana.  His mother, Queen Kausalya, was as proud of him as Aditi was of Indra.  Rama possessed numerous admirable qualities:  He was handsome, brave, and kind.  He remained calm and spoke gently, even when provoked.  He appreciated even small acts of kindness and readily forgave offenses.  He sought the company of wise and virtuous elders. He was eloquent, yet humble despite his strength. He was honest, respectful, and beloved by the people. Compassionate and self-controlled, Rama was a devout follower of righteous conduct.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: (Ayodhyakanda)
(Ayodhyakanda)

__data__.text: Rama continued to grow in virtue and wisdom.  He spent his free time conversing with those older than him in age, wisdom, and piety.  Talented and well-spoken, he initiated conversations with others in a pleasant manner.  Though strong and valiant, he remained humble.  Truthful and learned, Rama respected his elders and was beloved by the people, whom he also loved in return.  Compassionate and in control of his anger, he was devoted to Brahmins and empathetic towards the suffering.  He was knowledgeable in righteousness, practiced self-control, and lived a life of purity.  Rama diligently fulfilled his Kshatriya duties, viewing them as a path to a heavenly reward. He avoided harmful activities and distasteful conversations, preferring instead to engage in thoughtful discussions and debates, demonstrating his eloquence.  Healthy, youthful, and articulate, Rama had a keen awareness of appropriate conduct and a deep understanding of people.  His exceptional qualities made him the favorite of the people, cherished like their very lifeblood.  Mastering all areas of knowledge, including the Vedas, he surpassed even his renowned father in archery and mystical weaponry.  Blessed and benevolent, Rama remained steadfast in truth and integrity. He was guided by the wisdom of learned Brahmins, developing a strong understanding of righteousness, prosperity, and worldly affairs.  Modest and discreet, he attracted many followers.  He was judicious in both rewarding and punishing, and discerning in his assessment of character.  Rama was adept at assembling and supporting virtuous individuals, strategic in the use of force, and knowledgeable in financial matters, both in generating revenue and appropriate expenditure.  He was well-versed in both traditional scriptures and more contemporary works, balancing worldly enjoyment with righteous conduct and financial responsibility, all while remaining actively engaged in his duties.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: (Ayodhyakanda)
(Ayodhyakanda)

__data__.text: Rama possessed unwavering devotion and a stable mind. He surrounded himself with worthy individuals and spoke with integrity.  He was diligent and ever watchful, discerning the strengths and weaknesses of both his own people and others.  Skilled in sacred texts, he recognized and appreciated the contributions of others.  He was a keen judge of character and dispensed justice and favor fairly.  Rama excelled at assembling and supporting virtuous individuals, strategically applying force when necessary.  He was adept at generating revenue without undue burden on the people and managing finances responsibly, adhering to established principles.  Proficient in both traditional scriptures and contemporary works, he balanced worldly enjoyment with righteous conduct and financial responsibility, remaining actively engaged in his duties.  He appreciated the arts and allocated resources wisely. Skilled in horsemanship and managing elephants, Rama was a master archer, respected even by the most formidable warriors. He was adept at both offensive and defensive warfare, strategically deploying his forces. Respected in all realms, Rama was likened to the earth in forbearance and to the preceptor of the gods in wisdom, while his valor rivaled that of Indra. The earth desired him as her protector, and his father, King Dasaratha, contemplated how to crown him while still alive, recognizing Rama's superior qualities and the people's affection for him.  Dasaratha longed to witness this event, understanding Rama's compassion for all beings and his dedication to the welfare of the people.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: (Ayodhyakanda)
(Ayodhyakanda)

__data__.text: King Dasaratha contemplated crowning his son, Rama, while he was still alive.  He yearned to witness this joyous occasion, recognizing Rama's exceptional qualities, which surpassed even his own. Rama was beloved by the people, compassionate to all beings, and dedicated to their welfare.  He was compared to Yama and Indra in valor, Brihaspati in wisdom, and a mountain in steadfastness.  The king desired to see Rama ruling the world before he himself passed away.  Considering Rama's numerous virtues, Dasaratha consulted with his ministers and decided to appoint Rama as Prince Regent.  He informed them of the ominous portents he had observed and the effects of his advancing age.  However, the ministers assured him of Rama's immense popularity, which eased the king's anxieties.  Driven by his love for his people and their well-being, Dasaratha urged his ministers to expedite the coronation.  He summoned prominent citizens and rulers from across the kingdom, welcoming them with honors and gifts.  In his haste, he decided to inform King Kekaya and King Janaka later.  As Dasaratha took his seat in the assembly, the invited kings entered and sat respectfully facing him.  Surrounded by these dignitaries, King Dasaratha shone like Indra among the gods.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, eager to proceed with the coronation, hadn't yet informed King Kekaya or King Janaka, assuming they would learn of the joyous news later.  With Dasaratha seated in the assembly hall, the invited kings entered and respectfully took their designated places facing him.  Surrounded by these dignitaries, both local and from across the kingdom, King Dasaratha radiated a majestic presence, reminiscent of Indra among the gods.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha addressed the assembled council, his voice resonant and deep like a kettledrum. He declared his intention to retire and appoint Rama as his successor.  He spoke of his long reign, during which he diligently protected the kingdom, following the path of his ancestors.  Now, feeling the weight of his age and labors, he desired rest.  He praised Rama's virtues, comparing him to Indra in valor, and expressed his confidence in Rama's ability to rule, believing that under Rama's leadership, the kingdom would prosper even further. He sought the council's approval for this transition of power.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: King Dasaratha announced his plan to appoint Rama as Prince Regent the following morning, under the auspicious asterism of Pusya.  He described Rama as a righteous individual, comparing his splendor to the moon. He emphasized Rama’s exceptional qualities and expressed confidence that Rama's leadership would further enhance the kingdom’s prosperity.  Dasaratha stated his intention to relinquish the burden of rule and enjoy a peaceful retirement.  He requested the council’s approval, inviting alternative suggestions if his proposal was deemed unsuitable.  The assembly enthusiastically applauded the king's decision. Amidst joyous exclamations, the Brahmins, military leaders, and citizens of Ayodhya, along with representatives from other territories, convened.  They affirmed their support for Rama's appointment, expressing their eagerness to see him assume the role. King Dasaratha, feigning ignorance of their sentiments, questioned why they desired Rama as ruler while he himself still reigned righteously. The assembly responded by praising Rama's numerous virtues, offering to elaborate on his exceptional qualities.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: King Dasaratha asked the assembly why they desired Rama as ruler while he himself still reigned. The assembly responded by extolling Rama's virtues. They described Rama as possessing superhuman virtues comparable to Indra, surpassing all previous kings of the Ikshvaku dynasty. He was depicted as truthful and the embodiment of virtue, bringing prosperity to the kingdom.  The assembly compared Rama to the moon for his comforting presence, to the earth for his forbearance, to Brihaspati for his wisdom, and to Indra for his valor.  He was praised for his self-control, amiability, gratitude, and mastery over his senses.  Furthermore, he was described as gentle, kind, and a devout follower of learned Brahmins and elders, which further enhanced his reputation. Skilled in all forms of combat and knowledgeable in the Vedas, Rama excelled in music. He was also lauded for his concern for the citizens' welfare, inquiring about their families and students upon his return from expeditions. He shared in their joys and sorrows, demonstrating empathy and compassion.  The assembly highlighted Rama's ability to deliver just punishments while remaining composed and generous. They likened his radiance to the sun and expressed their good fortune in having such a virtuous prince. They wished for Rama’s strength, health, and long life, emphasizing his amiable nature.

__data__.text: The assembly continues their praise of Rama, highlighting his battlefield prowess and compassionate nature.  Returning victorious from expeditions, he inquires about the well-being of Ayodhya's citizens, demonstrating genuine concern for their families, occupations, and students.  He shares in their joys and sorrows, like a father to his children. Rama is described as just, showing empathy for those in distress, and celebrating the successes of others. Truthful and disciplined, he respects elders and controls his senses. He speaks with a smile, embraces righteousness, and avoids contentious arguments.  A skilled debater, he is compared to Brihaspati in his pursuit of truth. His physical appearance is likened to Lord Vishnu.  The assembly emphasizes Rama's ability to rule, noting his strength, valor, and discerning judgment.  They reiterate their desire for him to be installed as Prince Regent, highlighting his resemblance to a divine being, destroyer of enemies, and dedication to the welfare of all.

__data__.text: The assembly continues to extol Rama's virtues, comparing him to the guardians of the realms and emphasizing his unwavering strength.  They express their deep desire for him to rule, likening him to Kasyapa, a divine sage, and highlighting his virtuous nature.  The people of Kosala, both within the city and throughout the countryside, along with celestial beings, pray for Rama's well-being and long life.  Women of all ages offer devout prayers for his sake.  The assembly reiterates their fervent wish to see Rama, the destroyer of enemies and a figure of divine beauty, installed as Prince Regent, believing his leadership will bring prosperity and benefit to all.  They implore King Dasaratha to grant this request, emphasizing Rama's resemblance to Vishnu and his dedication to the welfare of the world.

__data__.text: King Daśaratha, pleased with the proposal to appoint Rama as Prince Regent, addressed the gathering, expressing his joy and good fortune. He then instructed Vasiṣṭha and other Brahmins to prepare for the upcoming ceremony in the month of Caitra.  Following a joyful uproar from the crowd, the king specified the necessary arrangements for the installation, including gold, herbs, flowers, food offerings, cloth, weapons, an army with all its divisions, an elephant, whisks, fans, a flag, a white umbrella, golden pitchers, a decorated bull, a lion skin, and sandal paste. He further ordered the decoration of the city and palace with garlands and incense, and instructed that ample food and gifts be prepared for the Brahmins attending the ceremony the following morning.

__data__.text: King Daśaratha, after addressing the assembly regarding Rama's appointment as Prince Regent, instructed Sage Vasiṣṭha to oversee the necessary preparations.  Vasiṣṭha, in turn, directed the ministers to gather materials for the ceremony, including gold, herbs, flowers, food, cloth, weapons, an army, an elephant, whisks, fans, a flag, a white umbrella, golden pitchers, a bull, a lion skin, and sandal paste. The king further ordered the decoration of the city and palace with garlands and incense. He also commanded that a feast be prepared for the Brahmins, along with generous gifts. Preparations were to be made for a ritual invocation of blessings the next morning, and the main road was to be sprinkled with water.  Dancers were to be stationed in the palace courtyard, ready to perform.  Shrines throughout the city were to be honored with offerings of food and money.  Armed guards, dressed in clean uniforms, were to secure the palace courtyard.  Vasiṣṭha and Vamadeva oversaw these preparations and informed the king of their completion.  Daśaratha then dispatched Sumantra to bring Rama to him.

__data__.text: King Daśaratha finalized the arrangements for Rama's coronation, instructing that the streets be decorated, and dancers and musicians be stationed within the palace.  He ordered offerings be made at temples and shrines.  The King's advisors, Vasiṣṭha and Vamadeva, oversaw the preparations and informed the King of their completion.  Daśaratha then summoned Rama.  As Rama arrived, the assembled kings and dignitaries paid him respect.  Daśaratha, filled with pride, offered Rama a magnificent seat.  He then formally announced Rama's appointment as Prince Regent, praising his virtues and expressing his love for him.  Daśaratha offered Rama fatherly advice, counseling him to cultivate humility, control his senses, and rule wisely, caring for his subjects and accumulating resources for the kingdom's prosperity. The citizens rejoiced at the news of Rama's imminent appointment.

__data__.text: King Daśaratha, deeply moved by Rama's respectful demeanor, embraced his son and offered him a magnificent gem-encrusted seat.  As Rama sat, his presence illuminated the court.  The King, filled with joy, formally addressed Rama, comparing their relationship to that of the sage Kasyapa and Indra. He declared Rama his beloved son and heir, born of his worthiest wife, Kausalya, and praised Rama's virtues, which had won the hearts of the people.  Daśaratha then advised Rama to accept the role of Prince Regent. The King, though recognizing Rama's inherent goodness, offered fatherly counsel, urging him to cultivate humility, control his desires and anger, and avoid vices. He emphasized the importance of ruling justly, both directly and through advisors, and of ensuring the kingdom's prosperity by accumulating resources and caring for his subjects. He reminded Rama that a benevolent ruler, who cherishes his people, earns the loyalty and joy of his friends and allies.  Rama's friends, eager to share the good news, rushed to Queen Kausalya and informed her of Rama's appointment.  The Queen, overjoyed, rewarded the messengers generously. Rama, after paying his respects to the King, returned to his palace, greeted by cheering crowds along the way.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha advised Rama to discipline his mind and conduct himself wisely.  Rama's friends, eager to share the news of his imminent appointment as Prince Regent, hurried to Queen Kausalya.  The queen, overjoyed, generously rewarded them with gold, cows, and jewels.  Rama, after paying his respects to the king, returned to his palace by chariot, greeted by cheering crowds. The citizens, thrilled by the king's announcement, returned to their homes and offered prayers for the successful completion of Rama's installation ceremony.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, after consulting his advisors, decided to install Rama as Prince Regent the next day, as the auspicious constellation Pusya would be ascendant. He summoned Rama again through Sumantra.  Rama, apprehensive upon Sumantra's second arrival, inquired about the reason. Sumantra conveyed the king's wish to see him.  Rama immediately went to the palace.  Dasaratha welcomed Rama warmly, offering him a seat. The king expressed his contentment, having lived a long life, enjoyed prosperity, performed many sacrifices, and been blessed with a son like Rama.  He stated his only remaining desire was to install Rama as Prince Regent, requesting Rama's compliance with his wishes.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: King Dasaratha continues explaining his rationale for Rama's immediate installation as Prince Regent. He recounts his long and prosperous life, filled with religious sacrifices and the fulfillment of desires. He expresses joy at having a son like Rama and having fulfilled his duties to gods, sages, ancestors, and Brahmins. Now, he desires only to see Rama installed as regent. He emphasizes the urgency due to ominous dreams, unfavorable astrological signs, and the people's desire for Rama as their leader.  Dasaratha wishes to complete the coronation before his mind changes or unforeseen circumstances arise. He instructs Rama to observe a fast with Sita, spending the night on a bed of grass with a stone pillow, guarded by friends against potential obstacles.  The king insists the installation must happen while Bharata is away, acknowledging Bharata's virtuous nature but emphasizing the fickleness of human minds, even those dedicated to righteousness. He stresses the auspicious timing of the next day, with the moon aligned with the constellation Pusya.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: King Dasaratha advises Rama to have his friends guard him vigilantly through the night, foreseeing potential obstacles to the upcoming coronation. He reiterates the importance of the ceremony occurring while Bharata is away, acknowledging Bharata's good character but emphasizing the unpredictable nature of the human mind.  Following his father's instructions and blessings, Rama departs to inform Sita of the impending coronation.  Not finding Sita in their quarters, Rama proceeds to his mother Kausalya's chambers where she is praying for his well-being.  Sumitra, Lakshmana, and Sita have already joined Kausalya, who sits with her eyes closed, contemplating the divine after hearing of Rama's imminent appointment. Rama approaches his mother, informs her of his upcoming coronation and the required overnight fast with Sita, and requests her to arrange for the necessary rituals.  Overwhelmed with joy, Kausalya blesses Rama with a long life, the defeat of his enemies, and the prosperity of their families.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: Rama informed his mother Kausalya that he had been appointed to rule the people and his coronation was scheduled for the following day. He explained that he and Sita were required to fast overnight, as instructed by his teachers and father. He requested Kausalya to arrange the necessary rituals for the upcoming ceremony.  Kausalya, overjoyed, blessed Rama with a long life, victory over his adversaries, and prosperity for their families.  She expressed her gratitude for his birth and the honor he brought to their lineage.  Rama then turned to Lakshmana, offering him a share in the kingship and its rewards, expressing his deep affection for his brother.  Finally, after taking leave of his mothers and Sita, Rama returned to his own residence.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, having informed Rama about his upcoming coronation, summoned his priest, Vasistha.  He instructed Vasistha to go to Rama’s palace and request both Rama and Sita to fast that night for a successful coronation.  Vasistha agreed and traveled to Rama’s palace in a chariot.  Upon arriving, Rama greeted Vasistha with respect and helped him from the chariot. Vasistha relayed the king’s message about the upcoming coronation and the need for the couple to fast. Rama and Sita respectfully accepted. After being honored by Rama, Vasistha departed. Rama then took leave of his friends and retired to his inner apartments. His palace, filled with happy people, resembled a vibrant lake teeming with birds and blooming lotuses.  Leaving Rama's palace, Vasistha found the streets crowded with people.

__data__.text: After being honored by Rama, Vasistha left Rama's palace. Rama then took leave of his friends and retired to his private quarters.  The palace, bustling with joyful people, resembled a vibrant lake filled with birds and blooming lotuses.  Exiting Rama's palace, Vasistha found the streets crowded.  The main roads of Ayodhya were overflowing with people eager to witness the upcoming coronation festivities.  The noise of the excited crowds resembled the roar of the sea.  Ayodhya’s streets were clean and decorated with flowers and flags. The city's residents, including women and children, eagerly awaited sunrise and Rama's coronation.  Vasistha slowly made his way through the crowded streets back to the royal palace, where he met with King Dasaratha.  Dasaratha rose from his throne to greet Vasistha and inquired about his visit with Rama. Vasistha confirmed that he had relayed the king’s message.  The king’s advisors also rose to honor Vasistha.  With Vasistha's permission, Dasaratha dismissed the assembly and retired to his private chambers, which were filled with elegantly dressed women.

__data__.text: After priestly duties, Rama, focused in mind, worshipped Lord Narayana with Sita.  He reverently offered ghee into the sacred fire and partook of the remaining offering.  Rama then rested on a bed of kusa grass in the temple, contemplating the divine presence.  Awakening before dawn, he oversaw the temple's decoration.  Amidst the melodious recitations of storytellers, genealogists, and bards, Rama completed his morning prayers with deep concentration. Dressed in fine silk, he honored Lord Vishnu and requested blessings from the Brahmins.  The sound of these blessings, accompanied by music, filled Ayodhya.  The citizens rejoiced upon hearing of Rama's fasting and, with the arrival of dawn, began decorating the city in anticipation of his installation.  Flags and banners adorned temples, crossroads, shops, homes, and prominent trees.  The people enjoyed performances by actors, dancers, and musicians.  Conversations throughout the city centered on the upcoming installation of Rama.

__data__.text: Ayodhya was adorned with flags and banners in every corner, from temples and crossroads to shops and homes, even the trees were decorated.  Actors, dancers, and musicians entertained the joyous citizens.  Talk of Rama's impending installation filled the air, from the city's squares to private homes, even children at play discussed it.  The main road was made beautiful with flowers and the fragrant smoke of incense.  Lamp posts, shaped like trees with lights on each branch, were erected throughout the city in anticipation of the evening's procession.  The citizens, eager for Rama's installation, gathered and praised King Dasaratha for his decision.  They spoke of Dasaratha's wisdom and Rama's virtues: his humility, knowledge, piety, and love for his brothers and the people.  They expressed gratitude that Rama would be their protector. People from the countryside, drawn by the news, flocked to Ayodhya to witness the event, adding to the city's swelling population.  The noise of the crowd echoed like the roar of the ocean at high tide.

__data__.text: The citizens of Ayodhya rejoiced, proclaiming, "Long live our noble King Dasaratha! By his grace, we shall witness Rama's installation as Prince Regent."  News of the upcoming ceremony spread, reaching people in the surrounding countryside who then journeyed to Ayodhya to witness the event.  Arriving from all directions, these eager crowds filled the city, creating a cacophony that resembled the roar of a turbulent ocean at high tide.  Ayodhya, already bustling with its own citizens, swelled with the influx of visitors, its grandeur and vibrant atmosphere likened to that of Indra's celestial city, Amaravati. The city, filled with anticipation, buzzed with the excitement of the crowds.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto VII

Beholding the festivities and finding Kausalya bestowing large gifts of
money on the Brahmanas, Manthara, a hunchback hereditary maid-
servant of Queen Kaikeyi, who had accidentally ascended the roof
of the palace, inquires of Sri Rama’s erstwhile nurse the occasion

for the festivities and, on being told of Sri Rama’s forthcoming
installation, feels enraged and, approaching Kaikeyi,
instigates her to stop the installation. Kaikeyi,
on the other hand, feels rejoiced over the
news and gifts her a jewel as a
token of her pleasure

__data__.text: Manthara, a maid-servant originally from Kaikeyi’s parents' household, happened to climb onto the palace roof. From this vantage point, she observed the bustling city of Ayodhya.  The main streets were watered and adorned with flowers.  Banners and flags decorated the buildings.  The city, sprinkled with sandalwood water, teemed with people who had bathed for the special occasion.  Brahmins carrying flowers and sweets added to the festive atmosphere.  Temples with gleaming white doorways stood amongst the sounds of music and Vedic chants.  Joyful citizens, elephants, horses, and even the calls of bulls and cows contributed to the vibrant scene.  Overwhelmed by the joyous spectacle, Manthara spotted Kausalya, Rama's mother, dressed in white silk and radiating happiness.  She asked Kausalya’s old nurse why the queen, usually careful with her wealth, was so generously distributing riches. She also inquired about the cause of the widespread celebration and the king’s plans.  The nurse excitedly explained that King Dasharatha was going to install Rama as Prince Regent the following day, under the auspicious asterism of Pusya.  Upon hearing this news, Manthara became instantly enraged.  She descended from the roof and, seething with anger, approached Kaikeyi, who was resting.  Manthara urgently roused Kaikeyi, warning her of impending danger and the fleeting nature of her perceived good fortune.

__data__.text: Manthara, angered by the news of Rama's impending coronation, descended from the palace roof.  She found Kaikeyi resting and immediately began to agitate her, warning of a supposed impending disaster. Manthara accused Kaikeyi of being naive and blind to the danger she faced, claiming her good fortune was fleeting.  Kaikeyi, bewildered by Manthara's outburst, asked what was wrong. Manthara, feigning concern, declared that King Dasharatha was plotting Kaikeyi's ruin by installing Rama as Prince Regent. She claimed that the king was deceitful and manipulative, using kind words to mask his true intentions.  Manthara warned that after sending Bharata away, Dasharatha would place Rama on the throne, having removed all obstacles. She compared Dasharatha to a venomous serpent nurtured by Kaikeyi, implying that he was a hidden enemy.

__data__.text: Manthara continued her manipulative campaign against Kaikeyi, declaring Dasharatha a deceitful and dangerous enemy disguised as a loving husband. She claimed that Dasharatha, despite his pleasant words, was planning Kaikeyi's downfall by installing Rama as the heir and sending Bharata away. Manthara compared Dasharatha to a poisonous snake, nurtured unknowingly by Kaikeyi, suggesting that he would strike when least expected.  She urged Kaikeyi to act swiftly to protect herself, her son, and even Manthara herself from the supposed impending doom.  Instead of heeding the manipulative words, however, Kaikeyi, filled with joy at the news of Rama's coronation, rose from her bed and rewarded Manthara with a beautiful jewel.  Expressing her delight at the announcement, she stated she saw no difference between Rama and Bharata and was pleased with Dasharatha's decision.  Overwhelmed with joy, she offered Manthara yet another gift, urging her to ask for anything she desired.

While Manthara was thus trying to impress on Kaikeyi’s mind that the

__data__.text: Manthara, upset by Kaikeyi's joy at Rama's impending coronation, rebukes her for failing to see the danger it poses to Bharata. She argues that Kaikeyi should be mourning, not celebrating, this event, which she sees as a calamity.  Manthara questions Kaikeyi's wisdom in rejoicing over the elevation of Rama, whom she characterizes as a potential enemy. She expresses concern for Bharata, highlighting the equal claim he has to the throne, and noting the loyalty of Lakshmana to Rama and Shatrughna to Bharata. Manthara points out that Bharata is the rightful heir based on seniority.  She fears Rama's capabilities as a Kshatriya, predicting his potential threat to Bharata. Manthara foresees Kausalya's rise to prominence and Kaikeyi's subservience to her, along with Bharata becoming Rama's servant. She concludes by painting a bleak picture of the future, where Rama's household will rejoice while Bharata's family suffers.  Despite Manthara's harsh words, Kaikeyi continues to praise Rama's virtues, asserting that his qualities make him deserving of the position of Prince Regent, and expressing confidence in his ability to protect his family.

__data__.text: Manthara continues her attempts to sway Kaikeyi, predicting joy for Rama's household and sorrow for Bharata's if Rama becomes king.  Kaikeyi, however, remains unmoved, praising Rama's virtues and asserting his worthiness as heir. She argues that Rama will care for his brothers like a father, questioning Manthara's distress. Kaikeyi believes Bharata will eventually inherit the throne after Rama's reign, and she expresses confusion over Manthara's apparent jealousy.  She emphasizes her equal affection for both Rama and Bharata, highlighting Rama's respect for his brothers. Manthara, frustrated by Kaikeyi's continued defense of Rama, laments her inability to see the impending danger. She predicts Bharata's exclusion from the royal lineage and argues that not all sons of a king can rule. Manthara reiterates the tradition of the eldest son inheriting the throne, regardless of the other sons' merits. She foresees Bharata losing his comforts and royal status, emphasizing her efforts to protect Kaikeyi and Bharata's interests.  Manthara also accuses Kaikeyi of failing to appreciate her advice, implying a misplaced loyalty to Kausalya, and warns that Rama will likely banish Bharata once he secures power.

__data__.text: Manthara argues that kings traditionally appoint their eldest son as heir, regardless of the other sons' merits. She foresees a bleak future for Bharata, deprived of comforts and royal lineage, if Rama becomes king. Manthara claims she's acting in Kaikeyi and Bharata's best interests, accusing Kaikeyi of misplaced loyalty to Kausalya and warning that Rama might banish Bharata. She cites the example of a tree saved by nearby thorns to illustrate the protective bond between Rama and Lakshmana, asserting that Rama would never harm Lakshmana but would likely act against Bharata.  Manthara urges Kaikeyi to advocate for Rama's exile and Bharata's coronation, believing this will ensure Bharata's rightful inheritance and the well-being of Kaikeyi's family. She questions how Bharata could live under Rama's rule and encourages Kaikeyi to protect her son, recalling past conflicts with Kausalya. Manthara paints a grim picture of future humiliation for Kaikeyi and Bharata if Rama ascends the throne and urges her to secure the kingdom for Bharata and find a pretext to exile Rama.

She suggests that Bharata, like an elephant overwhelmed by a lion, needs protection from Rama's dominance.  Manthara then sows seeds of distrust, insinuating that Kausalya, Rama's mother, harbors resentment towards Kaikeyi and will seek revenge once Rama becomes king.  She paints a bleak future where Kaikeyi and Bharata will face humiliation and servitude under Rama's rule. Manthara concludes by advising Kaikeyi to secure the throne for Bharata and find a reason to exile Rama, characterizing Rama as Bharata's enemy.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto IX

Kaikeyi, whose heart was poisoned by the malicious gossip of Manthara as
aforesaid, takes a vow to see that Rama is sent into exile and Bharata
installed as Prince Regent, and asks Manthara herself how to
secure that consummation. Manthara tells her how in the
course of a conflict between gods and demons, in which
Dasaratha’s help was enlisted by the gods, the queen,
who had accompanied her husband to the field of
operations and had rendered valuable assistance
to him at a critical juncture, was offered a couple
of boons, which she had kept in abeyance.

Manthara now asks Kaikeyi to demand the
banishment of Rama and the installation of
Bharata against the two promised boons.

Kaikeyi accordingly throws away her
ornaments and lies down on the bare
floor in the sulking-room in
a sullen mood

__data__.text: Kaikeyi, enraged by Manthara's words, declares her intent to banish Rama to the forest and instate Bharata as Prince Regent.  She then questions Manthara on how to achieve this. Manthara, with malicious intent, reminds Kaikeyi of two boons promised to her by Dasaratha.  She advises Kaikeyi to use these boons to secure Bharata's kingship.  Intrigued, Kaikeyi urges Manthara to elaborate on this plan. Manthara recounts the story of Dasaratha's involvement in a war between the gods and demons, where he was accompanied by Kaikeyi.  During this war, Dasaratha was severely wounded while fighting the demons, who attacked sleeping, injured soldiers at night.

__data__.text: Dasaratha, accompanied by Kaikeyi and other royal sages, journeyed south to the Dandaka forest to aid Indra in a war against the demons.  Their target was the city of Vaijayanta, ruled by the formidable demon Timidhwaja, also known as Sambara.  Sambara, a master of illusion, proved a challenging adversary, even for the gods.  The demons employed a ruthless tactic, attacking wounded and sleeping soldiers at night. During the fierce battle, Dasaratha was severely injured.  Kaikeyi bravely rescued the unconscious king from the battlefield, moving him to safety.  Grateful for her courage and quick action, Dasaratha offered Kaikeyi two boons.  Manthara reminds Kaikeyi of these unclaimed boons, urging her to use them to secure Bharata's ascension to the throne and Rama's exile. Manthara advises Kaikeyi to feign anger and refuse all other offerings from the king, emphasizing Dasaratha's deep affection for her and his willingness to fulfill her desires.

__data__.text: Manthara continues to manipulate Kaikeyi, assuring her of Dasaratha's unwavering love and emphasizing his inability to refuse her any request.  She advises Kaikeyi to exploit this devotion, rejecting any offers of jewels or gold.  Instead, Manthara urges Kaikeyi to demand the two boons promised to her by Dasaratha during the war with the demons.  These boons, Manthara insists, should be used to secure Bharata's coronation and Rama's fourteen-year exile. This exile, Manthara argues, will allow Bharata to consolidate his power, ensuring his reign remains unchallenged upon Rama's return.  Kaikeyi, swayed by Manthara's cunning words, agrees to this plan, believing it to be in Bharata's best interest. She acknowledges Manthara's shrewdness and expresses gratitude for her advice.

floor in the sulking-room in
a sullen mood

Canto IX

Kaikeyi, whose heart was poisoned by the malicious gossip of Manthara as
aforesaid, takes a vow to see that Rama is sent into exile and Bharata
installed as Prince Regent, and asks Manthara herself how to
secure that consummation. Manthara tells her how in the
course of a conflict between gods and demons, in which
Dasaratha’s help was enlisted by the gods, the queen,
who had accompanied her husband to the field of
operations and had rendered valuable assistance
to him at a critical juncture, was offered a couple
of boons, which she had kept in abeyance.

Manthara now asks Kaikeyi to demand the
banishment of Rama and the installation of
Bharata against the two promised boons.

Kaikeyi accordingly throws away her
ornaments and lies down on the bare
floor in the sulking-room in
a sullen mood

__data__.text: Kaikeyi, heavily influenced by Manthara, sees Bharata's ascendance as a timely opportunity. She envisions Bharata solidifying his rule while Rama is away, garnering support and establishing control.  Kaikeyi, accepting Manthara's counsel, plans to compel Dasaratha to change his mind about Rama's appointment.  Expressing gratitude and admiration, Kaikeyi praises Manthara's intelligence and loyalty. She compliments Manthara's appearance, focusing on specific physical attributes and minimizing the impact of her deformity.  Kaikeyi promises lavish rewards, including gold and fine adornments, once her plan succeeds.  She envisions Manthara, adorned and respected, holding a prominent position, even among Kaikeyi's rivals.

__data__.text: Kaikeyi, fueled by Manthara's manipulative words, promises extravagant rewards, envisioning Manthara adorned with gold and jewels, holding a prominent position.  She declares that Manthara will be treated with the utmost respect, attended by other servants.  Manthara urges Kaikeyi to act swiftly, emphasizing the urgency of the situation before Rama's position is solidified.  Kaikeyi, driven by pride and anger, casts off her jewelry and lies on the floor of the sulking chamber. She demands that either Rama be exiled and Bharata installed as king, or she will die. She reiterates her disinterest in material possessions, stating that her life is meaningless if Rama becomes king. Manthara, continuing her manipulative tactics, reminds Kaikeyi of the potential suffering she and Bharata will endure under Rama's rule.  Overwhelmed with anger and astonishment, Kaikeyi again insists on Rama's exile or her own death. She declares that only Bharata's ascension or her demise will satisfy her.

__data__.text: Kaikeyi, driven by Manthara's relentless and manipulative words, is consumed by anger and jealousy. Manthara continues to goad Kaikeyi, emphasizing that she and Bharata will suffer under Rama's rule.  Overcome with emotion, Kaikeyi dramatically throws off her jewelry and lies on the bare floor. She declares that either Rama must be exiled and Bharata installed as his successor, or she will choose death.  She renounces all comforts and pleasures, asserting that her life holds no value if Rama becomes king.  Kaikeyi's despair is likened to a Kinnara fallen from heaven and her rage-filled countenance is compared to a darkened sky, its stars obscured by shadows.

__data__.text: Dasaratha, unaware of Kaikeyi's manipulation by Manthara, found her lying on the floor, distressed.  Kaikeyi, having formulated her plan with Manthara, revealed her intentions.  She lay down, discarding her ornaments, appearing distraught.  The king, having ordered Rama's installation and dismissed his council, returned to his private chambers.  He entered Kaikeyi's opulent apartment, filled with the sounds of music and birdsong, but did not find her on her bed. Concerned, as this was unusual, he questioned the portress about her whereabouts.

__data__.text: Dasaratha, having instructed preparations for Rama's coronation, sought out Kaikeyi to share the joyous news.  He found her chamber opulent, filled with the sounds of music and the presence of birds and servants. However, Kaikeyi was not in her usual place. Instead, she lay on the floor of her sulking-chamber, dressed in soiled clothes, her hair in a single braid, and stripped of her ornaments, which lay scattered around her.  Distressed by her unusual state, Dasaratha questioned the portress, who informed him of Kaikeyi's retreat to the sulking-chamber in anger. The king, deeply troubled, went to her and, seeing her distraught condition, tried to comfort her, inquiring about the cause of her distress and offering to fulfill any wish she might have, reminding her of his devotion and the vastness of his kingdom as proof of his ability to grant her desires. He urged her to rise and tell him what was troubling her, promising to dispel her fears as the sun dispels mist.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Distressed by the portress's words, Dasaratha found Kaikeyi lying on the floor of her sulking-chamber, her appearance disheveled and her ornaments scattered around her.  The king, deeply saddened by her state, compared her to a broken vine, a fallen deity, a banished Kinnara, a fallen celestial nymph, a failed illusion, and a trapped doe.  He gently stroked her and, with a worried heart, asked her what troubled her.  He denied any knowledge of having angered her and questioned who had dared to rebuke or disrespect her. He implored her to tell him the cause of her distress, offering the services of his skilled physicians and promising to fulfill any wish she had, whether it involved rewarding someone, punishing someone, enriching someone, or impoverishing someone.  He emphasized his complete obedience to her will and urged her to speak her mind, assuring her of his unwavering love and his ability to grant her desires. He swore by his own merit that he would do whatever pleased her, reminding her of his vast kingdom.

__data__.text: Dasaratha, continuing his attempts to comfort Kaikeyi, swears by his accumulated merit that he will grant her any wish. He emphasizes the vastness of his kingdom, listing prosperous regions like Dravida, Sindhusauvira, Saurastra, Dakshinapatha, Vanga, Anga, Magadha, Matsya, Kasi, and Kosala, along with their abundant resources of wealth, grains, and livestock, all of which he offers to her. He urges her to rise from her distress, inquiring about the source of her fear and promising to dispel it like the sun dispelling mist. Reassured by his words, but still harboring her cruel intentions, Kaikeyi prepares to unveil her unpleasant request, ready to inflict further pain upon her husband.

Egged on by Kaikeyi to grant her desire, DaSaratha gives his word of
honour to her to that effect. Invoking the presence of gods as

during the conflict of gods and demons, Kaikeyi asks of

and installing Bharata as Prince Regent

exiling Sri Rama for a period of fourteen years
and installing Bharata as Prince Regent

__data__.text: Kaikeyi cruelly addressed the lovestruck King Dasharatha.  She claimed neither insult nor disrespect, but declared a desire she wished him to fulfill. She demanded a vow from him before revealing her request.  The king, affectionately arranging her hair, swore by Rama, dearest to him above all men, that he would grant her wish. He reiterated his vow, emphasizing Rama's importance, even above his own life, and implored Kaikeyi to reveal her desire.  He urged her to speak, assuring her of his unwavering love and commitment to fulfilling her request. Kaikeyi, realizing the king had taken the oaths she sought, declared her intention, which was as ominous as impending death.  She demanded that the gods, the celestial bodies, the earth, and all beings bear witness to his oaths and the boon she was about to request.

__data__.text: Kaikeyi, emboldened by Dasharatha's oaths, revealed her dreadful request.  She demanded the gods, the celestial bodies, the earth, and all beings bear witness to the king's promise.  She reminded Dasharatha of his virtuous nature and his commitment to truthfulness as she prepared to ask her boon.  Flattering him and reminding him of a past battle where she aided him, she declared he had offered her two boons, which she now intended to claim.  She pressed him, stating that if he refused, she would take her own life.  Overwhelmed by Kaikeyi's words and his affection for her, Dasharatha fell into her trap.  Kaikeyi then revealed her cruel demands: Bharata, her son, should be crowned king instead of Rama, and Rama should be exiled to the Dandaka forest for fourteen years, living as a hermit.

__data__.text: Kaikeyi, continuing her manipulative plea, reveals the details of her two boons.  First, she demands that Bharata be crowned immediately with the same elaborate preparations intended for Rama's coronation.  Second, she insists that Rama be exiled to the Dandaka forest for fourteen years, dressed in bark and deerskin, living the austere life of a hermit.  She emphasizes her desire to see Rama depart for the forest that very day.  Further leveraging Dasharatha's reputation, she urges him to uphold his promise, emphasizing that his honor, lineage, and very virtue are at stake.  She reminds him that truthful speech is the highest good, particularly for those seeking spiritual merit.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XII

Extolling the virtues of Sri Rama and showing him undeserving of exile, the
Emperor endeavours to dissuade Kaikeyi from her pertinacity in sending
Sri Rama into exile. Quoting the examples of Harigcandra and others,
Kaikeyi, however, redoubles her insistence. The Emperor for his
part rebukes her in harsh words and goes the length of falling
at her feet in order to bring her round but in vain

Canto XII

Emperor endeavours to dissuade Kaikeyi from her pertinacity in sending
Sri Rama into exile. Quoting the examples of Harigcandra and others,
Kaikeyi, however, redoubles her insistence. The Emperor for his
part rebukes her in harsh words and goes the length of falling
at her feet in order to bring her round but in vain

__data__.text: Overcome with grief and confusion upon hearing Kaikeyi's cruel request to exile Rama, the king questioned whether he was dreaming or if his mind was playing tricks on him.  He fainted from the shock, only to be tormented anew by her words upon regaining consciousness. Distressed and restless, he sighed deeply.  Expressing his disbelief and indignation, the king fainted once more.  When he finally awoke, he angrily confronted Kaikeyi, questioning her wicked intentions and reminding her of Rama's unwavering devotion and virtuous nature.  He declared he would rather give up his other wives, his kingdom, and even his life than part with his beloved son.  The king pleaded with Kaikeyi, even touching her feet, begging her to abandon her cruel plan.

__data__.text: King Dasharatha continues his desperate plea to Kaikeyi, questioning her motives for wanting to exile Rama. He accuses her of being like a venomous serpent, unknowingly brought into his home for his destruction, while the world sings Rama's praises.  He emphasizes his willingness to sacrifice his other wives, his kingdom, and even his life, but not Rama, the source of his greatest joy.  The king declares he cannot live without Rama, comparing his absence to a world without the sun or crops without water. He implores her to abandon her cruel plan, even touching her feet in supplication.  He reminds Kaikeyi of her past praise for Rama and questions her sudden change of heart, suggesting she is possessed by an evil spirit.  The king laments this misfortune befalling their lineage due to her perverted mind. He expresses disbelief in her actions, contrasting them with her past behavior. Dasharatha points out Rama’s equality to Bharata in her eyes, recalling her own stories illustrating this. He questions how she can favor exiling such a devoted and delicate son to the harsh forest.  He reiterates Rama’s dedicated service, surpassing even Bharata’s, asking why she would reward such devotion with exile. The king concludes by highlighting Rama’s impeccable reputation, free from any censure or calumny among the royal household.

__data__.text: Dasharatha continues to implore Kaikeyi, questioning her desire to exile Rama, who is obedient and pleasing. He emphasizes Rama’s superior service compared to Bharata, highlighting his respect, diligence, and the inability to find any fault in him.  The king points out Rama’s popularity and positive influence on the kingdom, achieved through his virtuous qualities like truthfulness, charity, and asceticism.  Dasharatha questions how Kaikeyi can contemplate harming such a guileless and godlike individual. He expresses his inability to cause Rama distress, reminding her of Rama’s kindness. The king again declares his dependence on Rama for his own well-being and begs for mercy, offering Kaikeyi anything she desires in exchange for sparing Rama. He even touches her feet in supplication, pleading with her to protect Rama and prevent unrighteousness from consuming him.  Despite Dasharatha’s desperate pleas, a distressed and grieving state, Kaikeyi remains unmoved and prepares to respond.

__data__.text: Kaikeyi remains firm in her resolve to see Rama exiled.  Dasharatha, in deep anguish, pleads with her, touching her feet and begging her to protect Rama from the unrighteousness that would befall the king if he breaks his promise. Kaikeyi coldly questions Dasharatha’s piety, asking how he can now go back on his word. She challenges him, questioning how he will face the royal sages after breaking his promise to her. Kaikeyi reminds him of her past service and loyalty, and accuses him of betraying her.  She cites examples of kings who upheld their promises, even at great personal cost, urging Dasharatha to honor his word.  Kaikeyi sees through what she believes is Dasharatha’s true motive: to enjoy a peaceful life with Kausalya with Rama as king.  She insists on the fulfillment of her promise, regardless of whether it's right or wrong. Kaikeyi threatens to drink poison and die if Rama is installed as Prince Regent and vows never to accept Kausalya as the mother of the future king. She swears by Bharata and herself that only Rama's exile will satisfy her.  Having delivered this ultimatum, Kaikeyi falls silent, ignoring the king's continued lamentations.

The king was stunned by her harsh words, remaining speechless for a time as he stared at her.  Overwhelmed by sorrow and realizing Kaikeyi's unwavering resolve, Dasharatha collapsed like a felled tree, crying Rama's name.  Distraught and disoriented, he questioned who had influenced Kaikeyi to pursue such a harmful path.  He lamented the change in her character, expressing his confusion over her sudden fear and her desire to see Bharata on the throne and Rama banished. He urged her to abandon her hostility and fear, appealing to her to consider the well-being of her husband, the world, and Bharata himself, and condemning her cruel and sinful plan.

Dasharatha expresses concern for Sumitra and Sita, who will be devastated by Rama's exile and his own potential demise from grief. He envisions Sita's despair, likening her to a Kinnara separated from her mate. He predicts his own death due to the unbearable pain and foresees Kaikeyi ruling the kingdom with Bharata.

Emperor endeavours to dissuade Kaikeyi from her pertinacity in sending
Sri Rama into exile. Quoting the examples of Harigcandra and others,
Kaikeyi, however, redoubles her insistence. The Emperor for his
part rebukes her in harsh words and goes the length of falling
at her feet in order to bring her round but in vain

__data__.text: Dasharatha continues to lament the consequences of Kaikeyi's demand. He agonizes over how he will explain Rama's exile to Kausalya, recalling her devoted service and his past neglect of her.  He worries about Sumitra's reaction and the pain it will cause her and her sons.  He envisions Sita's despair at the double blow of Rama's banishment and his own impending death from grief.  Dasharatha berates himself for his blindness to Kaikeyi's true nature, comparing her to poisoned wine. He feels betrayed and manipulated, likening her soothing words to a hunter's deceptive melody. The king anticipates the public shame and condemnation he will face for exiling his beloved son. He accepts responsibility for his predicament, viewing it as karmic retribution.  Dasharatha reflects on his naive trust in Kaikeyi, comparing it to a child unknowingly playing with a venomous snake.  He believes the world will rightly criticize him for failing to protect Rama. He laments that Rama, despite his noble nature and obedience, will meekly accept his unjust exile.  Finally, Dasharatha foresees his own death from the unbearable grief, predicting his demise will be met with public scorn.

__data__.text: King Dasharatha continues his lament, focusing on the hardships Rama will endure in exile.  Accustomed to a life of comfort and scholarly pursuits, Rama will now face a harsh, unfamiliar wilderness.  Dasharatha knows his obedient son will not protest this injustice, increasing his own anguish. The king predicts his imminent death from grief upon Rama's departure, anticipating public condemnation for his failure as a father. He expresses concern for his other wives and sons, envisioning their shared sorrow and despair.  He accuses Kaikeyi of condemning them all to a living hell, leaving her to rule a kingdom tainted by her cruelty. Dasharatha questions how Rama, used to royal luxuries, will cope with the physical deprivations of the forest, walking on foot, wearing rough clothing, and eating wild, unpalatable food. He ends by demanding to know the source of this cruel plan that demands both Rama's exile and Bharata's coronation.

__data__.text: Dasharatha continues his desperate plea to Kaikeyi, questioning how Rama, accustomed to royal chefs and fine dining, could survive on the meager, wild food of the forest.  He laments the thought of Rama, used to luxurious robes, dressed in rough, ochre garments. The king demands to know the origin of this cruel plan that exiles Rama while simultaneously elevating Bharata.  He condemns Kaikeyi, accusing her of selfishness and malice, though clarifying that his criticism is directed at her alone, not all women. Dasharatha accuses Kaikeyi of bringing ruin upon him and questions what wrong he or the ever-dutiful Rama has done to deserve such treatment.  He predicts a mass exodus from Ayodhya, as people follow Rama into exile, leaving the kingdom desolate.  He expresses the joy Rama's presence brings him, contrasting it with the despair he feels at the prospect of Rama's departure. Dasharatha declares that life in Ayodhya would be impossible without Rama, even if natural order itself were disrupted. He likens Kaikeyi to a deadly serpent he unknowingly harbored, a hostile enemy disguised as a loved one. He bitterly suggests she rule the kingdom with Bharata after he and his favored sons are gone, leaving her to gloat over her victory.  Dasharatha expresses surprise that Kaikeyi’s cruel words haven't caused her teeth to shatter, given the injustice of her demands. He points out Rama's gentle nature, incapable of harsh words, asking how she could find fault with such a virtuous son. He declares he will never agree to her demands, even if she resorts to self-harm, calling her a disgrace to her family.  Finally, Dasharatha begs Kaikeyi for mercy, touching her feet, pleading that his life is meaningless without Rama.

__data__.text: Dasharatha continues to rebuke Kaikeyi, questioning how she could accuse the always agreeable Rama of speaking harshly, especially since he is incapable of such behavior.  He vehemently rejects her demands, declaring he would rather die than agree to exile Rama. Dasharatha then unleashes a torrent of accusations against Kaikeyi, calling her deceitful, malicious, and destructive, stating he no longer desires her to live.  Overcome with grief, he reiterates that life without Rama is impossible and begs her for mercy, even stooping to touch her feet.  Distraught, Dasharatha collapses, wailing like a child, unable to fully reach Kaikeyi’s outstretched feet.

__data__.text: King Dasharatha, deeply distressed by Kaikeyi's relentless demands, laments his fate and reproaches her. He questions how he can banish Rama, a virtuous and deserving son, to the forest.  He expresses his anguish at the thought of the gods' disapproval and his own inability to face them knowing he exiled his righteous son. Dasharatha further emphasizes Rama's exceptional qualities – his valor, learning, control over anger, and forgiving nature – questioning how he could possibly send such a son into exile. The king grieves at the thought of Rama, deserving of comfort, enduring hardship in the Dandaka forest. He even expresses a wish for his own death if it could prevent Rama's suffering, brought about by Kaikeyi's cruel and sinful resolve.

__data__.text: King Dasharatha, heartbroken and desperate, pleaded with Kaikeyi to reconsider her cruel demand, offering her reverence and highlighting the injustice of exiling the virtuous Rama. He lamented his own impending death, wishing it could come without causing Rama pain.  As the night wore on, Dasharatha's sorrow deepened.  He begged the night not to turn to dawn, dreading the moment Rama's exile would begin.  The king continued to implore Kaikeyi, appealing to her compassion and reminding her of Rama's noble qualities and the devastating impact his exile would have on everyone, including Bharata.  Despite the king's heartfelt pleas and evident distress, Kaikeyi remained unmoved and refused to speak.  Overwhelmed by grief and Kaikeyi's unwavering stance, Dasharatha fainted and collapsed to the floor.  As the agonizing night passed, attempts to rouse the king with customary music were silenced by the heartbroken monarch.

__data__.text: The heartbroken King Dasharatha, his eyes reddened and wet with tears, continued to plead with Kaikeyi, mixing pleas with desperate threats.  Despite his anguished cries, the queen, unmoved by his suffering and focused on her malicious intent, remained silent and refused his pleas.  Seeing his beloved wife so hostile and insistent on Rama's exile, the king fainted again, collapsing to the floor in grief.  As the agonizing night wore on, Dasharatha sighed heavily in his misery.  When musicians attempted to rouse him with traditional celebratory music, the grieving king silenced them.

__data__.text: Kaikeyi, witnessing King Dasaratha’s distress at the thought of Rama’s exile, heartlessly questioned his inaction, reminding him of his promise.  She emphasized the importance of truthfulness, citing examples of King Saibya, King Alarka, and the ocean, all of whom adhered strictly to their vows.  She then pressed Dasaratha to fulfill his promise and exile Rama, invoking his righteousness and her own request. Kaikeyi threatened to take her own life if he refused, further intensifying the pressure on the distraught king, who found himself trapped by his word like Bali bound by Indra. The king’s face paled, his heart agitated, his eyes dimmed with distress.  With great effort, he finally spoke, disowning Kaikeyi, her son Bharata, and the hand he had once held in marriage, blaming her for this agonizing situation.  He lamented the approaching dawn, knowing the people eagerly awaited Rama's installation as Prince Regent.

__data__.text: As King Dasaratha grappled with Kaikeyi’s cruel demand, he felt trapped and agitated, like a bullock caught between a cart’s wheels.  His face paled, and his vision blurred with distress.  With immense effort, he rebuked Kaikeyi, disowning her, Bharata, and their marriage, placing the blame for this torment squarely on her.  He lamented the approaching dawn, knowing the populace eagerly anticipated Rama’s installation.  He proposed a morbid plan: if he couldn't bear this grief, Rama should perform his last rites using the very items meant for his coronation. Furthermore, if Rama’s installation was indeed canceled as per her wish, neither Kaikeyi nor Bharata should participate in his last rites. Dasaratha expressed his anguish at seeing his people’s joy turn to despair if Rama were not crowned.  As the night ended and dawn broke, the king continued his lament.  Kaikeyi, however, remained unmoved, coldly urging him to summon Rama and install Bharata as king, exiling Rama to the forest, thus fulfilling his promise and freeing her from perceived threats.  At that moment, as the auspicious hour for the installation arrived, Sage Vasistha entered Ayodhya with his disciples, carrying the ceremonial items, unaware of the unfolding tragedy.

__data__.text: As dawn broke, the sun rising and the auspicious hour arriving, Sage Vasistha entered Ayodhya with his disciples, bearing the items for Rama's coronation. The city was decorated with colorful banners, its streets clean and filled with joyful people. Markets overflowed with goods, and the air was fragrant with incense. Ayodhya, bustling with anticipation for Rama's installation, rivaled the splendor of Indra's celestial city.  Vasistha observed the palace, adorned with flags and teeming with citizens and rural folk, graced by Brahmins and attendants. Reaching the palace, the joyful sage and his companions bypassed the crowd at the gate, where he encountered Sumantra, Dasaratha's charioteer and minister.  Vasistha informed Sumantra of his arrival and presented the ceremonial items: sacred waters, a wooden throne, various seeds and herbs, gemstones, honey, curds, ghee, grains, grass, flowers, milk, eight maidens, an elephant, a chariot, a sword, a bow, a palanquin, an umbrella, chowries, a bull, a lion, a horse, another throne, a tiger skin, fire-starting sticks, musical instruments, adorned women, Brahmins, cows, animals, birds, prominent citizens, merchants, and their followers. All these, along with numerous well-wishers, eagerly awaited Rama's installation.  Urging the king to act swiftly, Vasistha instructed Sumantra to inform Dasaratha that the auspicious moment had arrived for Rama's coronation.  Sumantra, having received Vasistha's message, re-entered the palace, praising King Dasaratha. The king's attendants, respectful and eager to please, did not hinder Sumantra, recognizing his seniority and prior permission to see the king. Unaware of Dasaratha's distress, Sumantra approached him, intending to offer words of praise.  He began by comparing the king's joy to the ocean illuminated by the rising sun and urged him to share his happiness.  He then likened his role to that of the Vedas and other branches of knowledge guiding Brahma in creation, and to the sun and moon awakening the earth, encouraging Dasaratha to fulfill his duty.  Finally, Sumantra implored the king to rise, adorned and radiant, like the sun ascending Mount Meru.

__data__.text: Sumantra, preparing for Rama's coronation, presented the assembled items to King Dasaratha, including various provisions, animals, dignitaries, and well-wishers.  He urged the king to proceed with the ceremony, emphasizing its importance. However, finding Dasaratha grief-stricken and unable to speak, Sumantra attempted to comfort him, comparing the king's absence to a shepherdless flock or an army without a general.  Kaikeyi intervened, falsely claiming the king was asleep due to excitement and exhaustion over Rama's imminent coronation.  She then instructed Sumantra to fetch Rama without delay.

__data__.text: Sumantra, praising King Dasaratha, likened his absence to situations lacking leadership: a shepherdless flock, an army without a general, a moonless night, and cows without a bull.  Despite Sumantra's attempts to comfort him, the king remained grief-stricken. He felt Sumantra's well-intentioned words only deepened his pain.  Seeing the king's distress, Sumantra respectfully withdrew. Kaikeyi, however, insisted Sumantra bring Rama quickly, seemingly eager for the coronation.  Sumantra, believing the king's fatigue was preventing him from attending the ceremony, hurried to fetch Rama.  He left the palace and found the entrance crowded with people bearing gifts, ready to celebrate the coronation.

__data__.text: Sumantra, observing King Dasaratha's distress, respectfully withdrew.  Kaikeyi then addressed Sumantra, falsely explaining that the king was asleep, exhausted from the excitement of Rama's imminent coronation. She urged Sumantra to quickly bring Rama to the king.  Dasaratha, overhearing this, expressed his own desire to see Rama.  Sumantra, believing he was facilitating the coronation, happily and swiftly departed to fetch Rama.  Kaikeyi again emphasized the need for haste.  Sumantra, convinced he was acting on the king's wishes and motivated by the impending coronation, left the palace. He emerged to find the entrance thronged with people bearing gifts in anticipation of the celebration.

__data__.text: Brahmins learned in the Vedas, along with royal priests, gathered at the palace gate for Rama's installation ceremony.  Ministers, military leaders, and merchants also assembled joyfully for the event.  With the sun in Cancer and the Pusya star visible, as at Rama's birth, preparations were complete.  Golden pitchers filled with water, a decorated seat, a chariot with a tiger-skin cover, and water from the Ganga and Yamuna's confluence were ready.  Water from other sacred rivers, pools, and seas was also collected.  Honey, curds, ghee, grains, kusha grass, flowers, eight young women, a majestic elephant, gold and silver pitchers with lotuses and lilies, white chowries, and a white umbrella were all prepared.  A white bull and horse stood ready. Musical instruments, panegyrists, and bards were present.  The assembled Brahmins and others, having brought the necessary items for the installation, wondered who would inform the king of their arrival, as he was not present despite the sun having risen.

__data__.text: Sumantra returned to the palace gate and informed the assembled Brahmins, officials, and kings that he was sent by the king to fetch Rama for the ceremony.  He assured them he would inquire about the king's well-being and the reason for his delayed appearance.  Re-entering the palace, Sumantra praised the king's lineage and approached the royal bedchamber.  He offered blessings to Dasaratha, wishing him victory and acknowledging the arrival of the new day. He urged the king to awaken and attend to the waiting dignitaries, emphasizing their eagerness to see Rama.  Dasaratha, recognizing Sumantra's voice, questioned why his earlier command to bring Rama had not been obeyed, stating he was not asleep. He again instructed Sumantra to bring Rama quickly.

__data__.text: Sumantra, following the king's renewed command, left the king's chambers and proceeded towards Rama's palace.  He traveled along the main road, adorned with festive banners and flags.  The anticipation of Rama's imminent installation filled Sumantra with joy, a sentiment echoed by the conversations he overheard among the people.  He soon arrived at Rama's magnificent palace.  Its splendor was comparable to Mount Kailasa and Indra's celestial abode.  The palace, secured by imposing doors and embellished with numerous balconies, featured a grand facade adorned with gold and a gateway of gems and coral. Its brilliance resembled a luminous autumn cloud.  The palace was richly decorated with gems, pearls, sandalwood, and aloe, emitting a delightful fragrance like the aromatic slopes of Mount Dardura.  The palace grounds were graced by the presence of cranes and peacocks.  Intricate carvings of wolves and other artistic details further enhanced its beauty, captivating the eyes and minds of all who beheld it.  The palace, radiant as the sun and moon, rivaled the opulence of Kubera's palace and teemed with a variety of birds. Sumantra observed the palace, a majestic structure akin to Mount Meru, surrounded by a crowd of people with hands respectfully clasped, many of whom had brought gifts in anticipation of Rama's installation.

__data__.text: Sumantra arrived at Rama's palace, a grand structure shining like the sun and moon, as magnificent as Kubera's palace and bustling with various birds.  It resembled Mount Meru, surrounded by people with clasped hands, many bearing gifts for Rama's upcoming installation as prince regent. The palace, resplendent and cloud-like, was adorned with gems and attended by servants.  Sumantra, driving his chariot, approached the palace, bringing joy to the city's inhabitants. He was thrilled to arrive at the opulent palace, teeming with deer and peacocks, reminiscent of Indra's abode.  Passing through the ornate gates, which rivaled Mount Kailasa and the celestial realm, Sumantra navigated past Rama's followers and reached the inner chambers. There, he heard the joyful pronouncements of those preparing for Rama's installation and the people's enthusiastic blessings. The palace shone brightly, akin to Mount Meru's peak, and housed various creatures.  The entrance was crowded with people carrying gifts, their vehicles parked nearby. Sumantra then observed the impressive elephant, Satrughnaya, as tall as a mountain, which would carry Rama. He also noted the presence of adorned ministers, arriving on horseback, elephant, or chariot. Making his way past them, Sumantra entered the richly appointed inner chambers of the palace, which resembled a cloud-capped mountain with mansions like aerial cars, containing a wealth of gems.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

Canto XV
While going out of the gynaeceum to bring Sri Rama, Sumantra sees Vasistha
and others as well as a number of kings waiting at the gate and hastens

back to announce their presence to the king. He is, however, sent back
to fetch Sri Rama and forthwith enters the latter’s apartments

__data__.text: Sumantra, unhindered, entered Rama's private chambers. The palace interior, with its towering mansions resembling celestial vehicles, was as radiant as a cloud-wreathed mountain peak, filled with a wealth of precious gems.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: Sumantra, entering a gate guarded by loyal men, proceeded to another secured by devoted guards.  He requested them to inform Rama of his arrival. The guards promptly relayed the message to Rama, who was with Sita.  Rama graciously welcomed Sumantra into his private chamber.  Sumantra found Rama, radiant and adorned, seated on a golden couch.  Sita stood beside him. Sumantra respectfully greeted Rama. He then conveyed Dasaratha's wish, along with Kaikeyi's, for Rama to see them. Rama, pleased,  conjectured to Sita that his parents' summons likely pertained to his imminent appointment as Prince Regent.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: Sumantra informed Rama of his father Dasaratha's and Queen Kaikeyi's desire to see him.  Overjoyed, Rama shared with Sita his belief that this summons likely concerned his appointment as Prince Regent. He expressed his confidence that Kaikeyi, perceptive and eager to please Dasaratha, was likely advocating for him.  Rama saw Sumantra's arrival as a positive sign, further solidifying his expectation of imminent good news.  After bidding a loving farewell to Sita, who expressed her wishes to see him installed and offered prayers for his protection, Rama left his residence with Sumantra.  As he departed, he encountered Lakshmana waiting respectfully at the gate.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: As Rama departed the palace, blessings were invoked for his protection.  He then emerged like a lion from its mountain lair and saw Lakshmana waiting respectfully. After exchanging greetings with friends and relatives gathered at the middle gate, Rama boarded his magnificent chariot, radiant as fire.  The chariot, adorned with gold and jewels, was drawn by powerful horses and sped forth like Indra's own.  Lakshmana followed closely, holding a ceremonial fan.  The crowd erupted in cheers as Rama's procession, including elephants and warriors, moved through the city.  Musicians played, bards sang praises, and women showered flowers from balconies, expressing their joy and offering blessings.  They anticipated Kausalya's happiness at seeing Rama's impending coronation. They praised Sita's virtue, comparing her fortunate union with Rama to Rohini's with the moon god.  Amidst the celebratory clamor, Rama heard the people's enthusiastic discussions about his lineage and their hopes for his reign. They believed his rule would bring prosperity and happiness to the kingdom.  The procession continued, led by those proclaiming blessings and reciting Rama's lineage, accompanied by musicians.  Rama observed the bustling streets, filled with people, animals, chariots, and shops overflowing with goods.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: As Rama's chariot, resplendent and swift, departed the palace, Lakshmana attended him with a ceremonial fan.  The crowd's joyous clamor filled the air as the procession, complete with horses, elephants, and warriors, moved through the city. Musicians and bards contributed to the celebratory atmosphere, while women showered flowers upon Rama from the balconies.  They expressed their anticipation of Kausalya's joy and praised Sita's virtuous character, comparing her union with Rama to that of Rohini and the moon god.  The people enthusiastically discussed Rama's lineage and the prosperity they expected from his reign.  The procession advanced, led by those offering blessings and reciting Rama's lineage, accompanied by musicians.  Rama observed the bustling streets filled with people, animals, chariots, and shops brimming with goods.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}
