__data__.text: Rama, joyous amongst his friends and family, rode his chariot through Ayodhya.  The city was decorated with flags and banners, fragrant with incense, and filled with people.  He proceeded down the main road, lined with white houses that resembled clouds.  Like Indra, king of the gods, Rama surveyed the route, clear of any danger and bustling with shops overflowing with sandalwood, aloe, silks, pearls, crystals, flowers, and food.  The crossroads were adorned with offerings.  As he passed, people called out blessings and praises, which Rama received graciously.  They spoke of the righteous path of his ancestors and expressed their hopes for even greater prosperity under his rule, surpassing even the golden age of his father, Dasaratha.  They declared that nothing would bring them more joy than Rama's coronation.  Though the crowds lauded him, Rama remained humble, yet no one could tear their gaze away from him as he passed.  Those who missed seeing him felt a pang of regret.  Rama, compassionate to all, regardless of their social standing, earned the devotion of the people.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: Rama, continuing his procession, heard the praises of his friends and family, though he remained unaffected by the adulation.  Even as he passed, people couldn't look away, captivated by his presence.  Those who didn't see him felt a sense of loss. Rama, compassionate to all people, earned their devotion. He respectfully drove past crossroads, temples, and sacred places.  He then entered the magnificent royal palace, which was adorned with beautiful attics and pleasure-houses, a structure comparable to Indra's celestial abode.  After passing through the first three gates in his chariot, Rama proceeded on foot through the remaining two, dismissing his entourage before entering the private chambers to meet his father. The waiting crowd joyfully anticipated his return, like the ocean awaiting the moonrise.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi
tells him all that had happened in the meantime and sternly
urges him to depart for the woods

Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi
tells him all that had happened in the meantime and sternly
urges him to depart for the woods

__data__.text: Rama approached his father, King Dasaratha, who sat dejectedly with Kaikeyi.  Rama bowed respectfully to both. Dasaratha, tearful and distressed, could barely acknowledge Rama's presence.  Observing his father's unusual state, Rama became apprehensive, like one who accidentally steps on a snake. He saw the king emaciated with grief, sighing, and looking like a turbulent ocean, a sun in eclipse, or a sage who has uttered falsehood.  Concerned, Rama wondered why his father, usually so affectionate, was unresponsive. He questioned Kaikeyi, asking if he had unknowingly offended his father and pleading with her to intervene on his behalf. He expressed his deep concern for Dasaratha's well-being, hoping he was not suffering from physical or mental anguish, and anxiously inquired if he had offended Bharata, Shatrughna, or any of his mothers. Rama declared that he could not bear to live if he had displeased his father in any way.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi
tells him all that had happened in the meantime and sternly
urges him to depart for the woods

Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi
tells him all that had happened in the meantime and sternly
urges him to depart for the woods

__data__.text: Rama, deeply concerned by his father's silence and distress, questioned Kaikeyi about the cause.  He expressed his worry, hoping no physical or mental ailment troubled the king, and wondered if he had unknowingly offended anyone, including Bharata, Shatrughna, or any of his mothers.  Rama affirmed his devotion to his father, emphasizing the impossibility of living if he had caused displeasure. He wondered if Kaikeyi had perhaps spoken harshly to Dasaratha, causing him distress.  In response, Kaikeyi claimed the king was neither angry nor in anguish but held something back for fear of hurting Rama. She insisted that Rama must fulfill a promise Dasaratha made to her, a boon the king now regretted granting.  Kaikeyi implied this undisclosed promise was difficult for Dasaratha to honor and warned that the king might forsake truth due to his anger towards her for Rama's sake. She agreed to reveal the matter only if Rama pledged to obey the king's command, whatever it may be.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi
tells him all that had happened in the meantime and sternly
urges him to depart for the woods

Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi
tells him all that had happened in the meantime and sternly
urges him to depart for the woods

__data__.text: Kaikeyi continued to manipulate Rama, asserting that Dasaratha, bound by truth and piety, was tormented by the promise he made to her. She pressed Rama to swear obedience to his father's command before she would reveal it, insinuating that the king's distress stemmed from his reluctance to burden Rama with this difficult request.  Rama, distraught by the implication of his father's unhappiness, readily agreed, declaring his unwavering devotion and willingness to obey any command, even if it meant self-sacrifice. He implored Kaikeyi to disclose the king's wish.  Kaikeyi then revealed her demand: that Rama be exiled to the Dandaka forest for fourteen years while Bharata be crowned king. She claimed these were the boons Dasaratha had granted her long ago, for saving his life in battle.  She insisted that honoring this promise was the only way for both Dasaratha and Rama to uphold their integrity. She portrayed the king as heartbroken over his predicament, unable even to look at Rama due to his grief over this forced exile.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi

Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi
tells him all that had happened in the meantime and sternly
urges him to depart for the woods

__data__.text: Kaikeyi commands Rama to relinquish his claim to the throne and instead allow Bharata to be crowned king, using the resources gathered for Rama's own coronation. She dictates that Rama must spend fourteen years in the Dandaka forest, wearing matted hair and bark clothing, while Bharata rules the prosperous kingdom.  Kaikeyi manipulates Rama by claiming that Dasaratha is overwhelmed with grief over this situation, brought about by boons he had previously granted her. She asserts that Dasaratha is unable to face Rama due to his sorrow and that fulfilling her request is the only way to uphold both his and Rama's honor.  Kaikeyi urges Rama to obey his father's supposed wish and release him from this supposed suffering.  Despite Kaikeyi's harsh words, Rama remains composed, while Dasaratha, though strong-willed, is deeply distressed by the impending separation from his son.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XIX

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya

Canto XIX

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya

__data__.text: Rama, unfazed by the harsh decree, calmly accepted his exile to the forest, honoring his father's promise. He questioned Kaikeyi, seeking to understand the king's sudden change in demeanor.  Rama assured her that he bore no ill will and would willingly obey, even relinquishing Sita and his own life if commanded. He emphasized his eagerness to fulfill his father's wishes, especially if it pleased Kaikeyi.  Concerned by the king's sadness, Rama urged that messengers be sent to retrieve Bharata.  He affirmed his resolve to depart swiftly for the Dandaka forest, adhering to his father's command without question.  Pleased with his compliance, Kaikeyi hastened his departure, promising to summon Bharata. She dismissed Rama's concerns about the king's silence, attributing it to shyness and urging him to disregard it.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XIX

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya

Canto XIX

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya

__data__.text: Rama, accepting his exile, reassured Kaikeyi of his obedience.  He urged her to quickly dispatch messengers to retrieve Bharata. Kaikeyi, pleased with his compliance, pressed him to leave immediately, attributing the king's silence to shyness.  Dasaratha, heartbroken by Rama's words, collapsed onto his couch, overcome with grief.  Rama, urged by Kaikeyi, prepared to leave for the forest.  He responded to Kaikeyi's harsh words by stating his disinterest in worldly possessions and his commitment to righteousness.  He affirmed his duty to his father and agreed to his fourteen-year exile. He then requested permission to bid farewell to his mother, Kausalya, and Sita before departing for the Dandaka forest.  He reminded Kaikeyi of her duty to ensure Bharata's proper governance of the kingdom and care for their father. Dasaratha, unable to speak, wept aloud in his anguish.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XIX

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya

Canto XIX

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya

__data__.text: Rama, addressing Kaikeyi, questioned her perception of his virtues, given her seemingly trivial request to his father, despite her significant influence over him.  He asked for her patience while he informed his mother, Kausalya, and Sita of his departure for the Dandaka forest.  He reminded Kaikeyi of her responsibility to ensure Bharata's proper governance and care for their father.  Overwhelmed by grief, Dasaratha wept uncontrollably.  Rama, after paying his respects to his father and Kaikeyi, departed.  Leaving the inner quarters, he met his friends.  A visibly upset Lakshmana, followed close behind.  Rama, bypassing the consecration preparations, maintained his composure despite the loss of his kingship.  He dismissed his attendants and, controlling his emotions, entered his mother's chambers to convey the unwelcome news. Despite the circumstances, Rama's outward appearance remained unchanged, showing no visible sign of distress.  He maintained his usual joyful demeanor and respectful manner as he entered his mother's presence, followed by Lakshmana, who also struggled to contain his sorrow.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya

Canto XIX

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya

__data__.text: Rama, maintaining his composure and usual joyful demeanor, entered his mother Kausalya's palace.  His appearance remained unchanged, betraying no sign of the inner turmoil he felt about his imminent exile.  He greeted everyone with his usual respectful and pleasant speech. Lakshmana, equally distressed by the turn of events but mirroring Rama's outward calm, followed closely behind.  Even upon entering Kausalya's palace, which was decorated in anticipation of Rama's coronation, and realizing the stark contrast between the joyous preparations and his own misfortune, Rama did not outwardly express his sorrow, mindful of the impact such a display might have on his loved ones.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XX

Even as Sri Rama issued forth from the palace of Queen Kaikeyi, the inmates
of the gynaeceum burst into a piteous wail, extolling the prince’s virtues.
Mother Kausalya embraces and pronounces her benedictions on Sri
Rama as the latter approaches her and falls at her feet. On being
apprised of the circumstances that had brought him there, she
falls to the ground overwhelmed with grief, and weeps
bitterly expressing her deep sorrow

Mother Kausalya embraces and pronounces her benedictions on Sri

__data__.text: As Rama left Kaikeyi's palace, the women inside cried out, lamenting his imminent departure. They praised his virtues, recalling how he cared for them, protected them, and resolved their disputes peacefully.  Meanwhile, King Dasaratha, overcome with shame and sorrow at his decision, hid himself.  Rama, though deeply saddened by the grief of his loved ones, proceeded with Lakshmana to his mother Kausalya's quarters.  He passed the gatekeepers, who greeted him with cheers, then the Brahmins at the second gate, and finally the women and guards at the third gate.  The women joyfully informed Kausalya of Rama's arrival.  Kausalya, who had spent the night in prayer and was continuing her rituals at daybreak, offering oblations to Vishnu, was unaware of the events that had transpired.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: At the third gate of Kausalya's palace, Rama saw women, young girls, and elderly men guarding the entrance.  The women, excited about Rama's supposed appointment as crown prince, rushed inside to share the good news with his mother.  Meanwhile, Kausalya, unaware of the recent events, had spent the night in prayer and continued her morning rituals, offering oblations to Vishnu. Dressed in silk and dedicated to religious practices, she oversaw the priests as they chanted sacred texts and poured offerings into the fire.  Rama entered his mother's chambers and saw the preparations for the divine worship: curds, rice, ghee, sweetmeats, parched paddy, white flowers, a rice pudding, sesame rice, sacrificial wood, and water-filled jars. He observed his mother, pale from fasting, as she performed the rites.  Kausalya, overjoyed to see her son after a long time, rushed to greet him as a mare greets her foal.  Rama embraced his mother.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Queen Kausalya, initially unaware of Rama's exile, was informed by her excited handmaidens of his imminent appointment as crown prince.  Rama, however, gently corrected this misunderstanding, revealing his impending fourteen-year exile to the Dandaka forest. He explained that he would live like an ascetic, subsisting on roots, fruits, and other forest fare, while Bharata would be crowned Prince Regent.  Kausalya, devastated by this news, collapsed like a felled tree.  Rama comforted his grieving mother, brushing the dust from her clothes.  Kausalya lamented her fate, expressing that the pain of separation from her son was far greater than the sorrow of childlessness. She further worried about the unkind words she would endure from her co-wives due to Rama's exile.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Kausalya, overwhelmed with grief, laments Rama's exile. She expresses that the pain of his departure is worse than the sorrow of childlessness, explaining that a barren woman's only anguish is the lack of a child, while she must endure the greater agony of separation from her grown son.  She anticipates unkind remarks from her co-wives due to Rama's banishment and feels her position in the palace will be further diminished. Kausalya mourns that despite her efforts in raising Rama, she is now treated with disdain, a situation she expects will worsen in his absence.  She questions her own resilience, wondering why death does not claim her despite her immense sorrow.  Kausalya feels as though her heart is made of stone, unbreakable despite the weight of her grief.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Kausalya continues to lament, expressing the futility of her past religious practices if they couldn't prevent this tragedy. She believes her heart is unbreakable, as it doesn't shatter from the immense grief. She wishes for death but feels it evades her, much like a lion ignoring a crying deer.  Even if death doesn't come, life is meaningless without Rama.  Like a cow yearning for its calf, she declares her intention to follow him into exile, despite her frailty.  Observing Rama constrained by duty, Kausalya's anguish deepens, and she weeps like a Kinnara whose child is captured.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XXI

Consoling Kausalya in her grief over the impending exile of Sri Rama, Prince
Laksmana opposes the idea of Sri Rama’s leaving for the forest and,
censuring Dasaratha, makes up his mind to accompany his
eldest brother. Kausalya too deters Sri Rama from going
into exile, branding Kaikeyi’s command as unjust.

Sri Rama, however, justifies the command on the
ground of its being countenanced by the
Emperor and requests his mother to grant
him leave and perform auspicious
rites connected with his departure

Canto XXI

Laksmana opposes the idea of Sri Rama’s leaving for the forest and,

__data__.text: Lakshmana, distressed by the situation, addressed the weeping Kausalya. He argued against Rama's exile, questioning Dasaratha's decision to banish his eldest son due to Kaikeyi's influence. Lakshmana couldn't fathom any wrongdoing on Rama's part that merited such punishment, asserting that no one, not even Rama's enemies, could find fault with him.  He criticized Dasaratha for succumbing to Kaikeyi's will, likening the king's behavior to that of a senile man driven by desire.  Lakshmana questioned how any virtuous person could exile such a noble son. He then proposed that Rama seize control of the kingdom before the news of the exile spread, offering his unwavering support and protection. Lakshmana declared he would eliminate anyone who opposed Rama, including those loyal to Bharata.  He even suggested that Dasaratha should face consequences if he sided with Kaikeyi against Rama.  Finally, Lakshmana challenged Dasaratha's authority to bestow the kingdom upon Bharata, questioning his right to create such conflict.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text:
Lakshmana continued his impassioned plea against Rama's exile, suggesting drastic measures against Dasaratha if he persisted in his decision.  He questioned Dasaratha's authority to give the kingdom to Bharata, emphasizing Rama's rightful claim. Lakshmana swore his unwavering loyalty to Rama, vowing to follow him into fire or forest and to avenge this injustice. He promised Kausalya he would resolve the situation with his strength.

Kausalya, deeply saddened, then appealed to Rama, asking him to consider Lakshmana's words.  She urged him to defy Kaikeyi's unjust command and remain in Ayodhya.  Kausalya argued that his filial duty to her superseded any other obligation.  She pleaded with him, emphasizing her deep attachment and declaring she would not survive his departure.

Laksmana opposes the idea of Sri Rama’s leaving for the forest and,
censuring Dasaratha, makes up his mind to accompany his
eldest brother. Kausalya too deters Sri Rama from going

__data__.text:
Kausalya, invoking examples of devoted sons, implored Rama to reconsider his exile, arguing that his duty to her outweighed any other. She declared her life meaningless without him and threatened to fast to death if he left, warning him of dire spiritual consequences for causing her such grief.

Rama, however, gently but firmly explained his decision. Citing examples of legendary figures like Kasyapa, Kandu, Sagara's sons, and Parasurama, he emphasized the importance of obeying one's father, even if the command seemed difficult or unjust.  He assured Kausalya that he wasn't establishing a new precedent but following a well-trodden path of filial duty, one that ultimately brought blessings, not curses.  He asserted that fulfilling his father's wish was the most righteous action he could take in this situation. Finally, acknowledging Lakshmana's fierce loyalty and strength, Rama addressed him, preparing to respond to his brother's impassioned pleas.

He argues that fulfilling his father's wish, even if it seems unjust, is the most righteous action he can take.  Acknowledging Lakshmana’s devotion and strength, Rama addresses his brother's concerns regarding the exile. He reminds Lakshmana of his unsurpassed love and prowess, while expressing concern for Kausalya’s grief.  Rama emphasizes the importance of adhering to righteousness and truthfulness, highlighting that his father's command, although painful, aligns with these principles. He reminds Lakshmana that promises made to parents, particularly in the context of duty, must be kept.  Rama explains his commitment to fulfilling the exile due to Kaikeyi's command and requests Kausalya to bless his journey with auspicious rites, assuring her of his return. He urges her to restrain her grief and accept his decision as a necessary fulfillment of his father’s command.  Finally, having calmed his mother with his steadfast resolve and righteous words, Kausalya regains her composure and prepares to speak again.

__data__.text: Rama consoles his grieving mother, Kausalya, assuring her of his eventual return from exile after fulfilling his father's command. He emphasizes that obedience to his father is an eternal moral law, binding not only himself but also Sita, Lakshmana, and Sumitra.  He declares his resolve to proceed with the exile, urging Kausalya to set aside her grief and support his decision by performing auspicious rites for his journey. Kausalya, deeply moved, expresses her profound love for Rama, questioning the purpose of her life without him.  She pleads with him not to leave her. Witnessing his mother's anguish, Rama becomes even more determined to adhere to his dharma. He addresses both his distraught mother and Lakshmana, reminding Lakshmana of his unwavering devotion and strength, and gently chiding him for adding to Kausalya's distress.  Rama explains that true happiness stems from pursuing righteousness above material gain or worldly pleasure. He emphasizes the importance of honoring a father's command, especially when that father is also a king and preceptor.  Rama reaffirms his commitment to his father's pledge and explains that King Dasaratha is their ultimate protector and guide. He questions how Kausalya could leave Ayodhya while her virtuous husband, the king, still lives.  Finally, Rama implores Kausalya to bless his journey into exile and perform the necessary rites, assuring her of his return, just as King Yayati returned to heaven through truthfulness. He declares his refusal to compromise his honor and righteousness for the sake of worldly power.

__data__.text: Rama continues to console his mother Kausalya, explaining that he must fulfill his father's pledge. He reminds her that his father, Dasaratha, is their rightful authority and the source of their well-being.  He questions how Kausalya could possibly leave Ayodhya while her husband, the king, still lives there. Rama then asks Kausalya to bless his journey into exile and perform the customary rituals for a safe passage. He assures her of his eventual return, drawing a parallel to King Yayati's return from exile.  Finally, Rama reiterates his commitment to righteousness, stating that he will not compromise his honor for worldly power, particularly the kingship, as life is too short to live dishonorably.  Having explained his position and instructed Lakshmana, Rama respectfully takes his leave of Kausalya.

__data__.text: Rama, seeing his brother Lakshmana’s distress and anger over his exile, calmly addressed him.  Rama urged Lakshmana to cast aside his anger towards their father and Kaikeyi, and instead embrace the situation with joy, seeing it as an opportunity to uphold their father's promise and secure his place in heaven.  He requested Lakshmana to redirect the preparations from the coronation to the exile, ensuring the same level of diligence.  Mindful of Kaikeyi’s feelings, Rama urged Lakshmana to reassure her and allay any fears she might have about his reaction to the exile.  Rama affirmed his respect and love for his parents, stating that he had never intentionally or unintentionally offended them. He expressed his desire to honor his father’s promise, freeing him from any guilt or concern. Rama believed his exile was destined and would bring peace to Kaikeyi. He emphasized that he had no desire to defy fate and was ready to depart for the forest without delay.  He concluded by stating that Providence alone was responsible for both his exile and his potential return to power.

__data__.text: Rama continued to counsel Lakshmana, advising him to accept the situation. He suggested that Kaikeyi would find peace once Rama, adorned in ascetic garb, departed for the forest.  Rama stated his intention to honor his father's promise and not resist fate, believing destiny orchestrated his exile. He questioned how Kaikeyi, a woman of noble character, could act in such a way unless influenced by a higher power. Rama reiterated that unforeseen events are the work of Providence and cannot be averted.  He reminded Lakshmana that destiny governs joy and sorrow, gain and loss, even influencing the actions of learned sages.  Unexpected occurrences, especially those interrupting planned events, are also attributed to destiny.  Rama affirmed his own composure despite the disrupted coronation, urging Lakshmana to abandon his grief and halt the coronation preparations. He proposed using the consecrated water gathered for the ceremony for his purification rites before entering exile.

__data__.text: Rama continued to console Lakshmana, explaining that unforeseen events are often the work of a higher power. He remained unperturbed by the sudden change of plans, accepting it as destiny.  He urged Lakshmana to cease the coronation preparations and instead use the consecrated water for his purification rituals before embarking on his exile.  Rama dismissed the importance of the royal items intended for the coronation, stating that simple water would suffice for his ascetic vows. He advised Lakshmana against grieving the loss of kingship, emphasizing that exile held greater significance for him.  Finally, Rama defended both Kaikeyi and his father, attributing the situation to divine influence beyond their control.

from righteousness and, further emphasizing the predominance

up arms against those who interfere with his installation

__data__.text: Lakshmana, distressed by Rama's acceptance of exile, swung between despair and admiration for his brother's righteousness.  His anger manifested physically: he frowned, hissed like a cobra, and his face became fierce like a lion's.  He then vehemently challenged Rama's decision, arguing that his father's command, influenced by Kaikeyi, was unjust and should be defied. Lakshmana questioned Rama's concern for public opinion and his passive acceptance of what he perceived as a deceitful plot. He emphasized Rama's capability to reclaim his rightful position through action and condemned Dasaratha and Kaikeyi's manipulation.  He further asserted that such adherence to a flawed sense of duty was detrimental and disapproved of by the people. Lakshmana expressed his own inability to tolerate anyone but Rama as king and criticized the misplaced piety that led Rama to this predicament. He declared his disapproval even if the situation were divinely ordained, urging Rama to disregard the unjust decree.

__data__.text: Lakshmana continues his impassioned plea to Rama, questioning how Rama can passively accept this unjust exile when he has the power to rectify the situation. He criticizes Rama's adherence to a flawed sense of duty, arguing that it's viewed negatively by the people and serves only the interests of their enemies, Dasaratha and Kaikeyi. Lakshmana rejects the idea that this situation is divinely ordained and insists that true strength lies in defying fate, not submitting to it.  He vows to use his own power to overturn the exile, asserting that neither gods nor men can prevent Rama's rightful coronation.  Lakshmana declares his intent to thwart Dasaratha and Kaikeyi's plans and promises to inflict suffering on anyone who opposes Rama. He sarcastically suggests that Rama should only consider a forest retreat after thousands of years of rule, after entrusting his sons with the kingdom, as was the tradition of ancient sage-kings.  Finally, Lakshmana dismisses any concerns Rama might have about causing unrest by claiming the throne, assuring him there's no need for such apprehension.

__data__.text: Lakshmana boasts of his strength and declares that his might surpasses even destiny, promising suffering to anyone who opposes him. He ironically suggests that Rama should consider a forest retreat only after ruling for thousands of years, following the tradition of ancient kings who passed the kingdom to their sons.  Lakshmana then dismisses Rama’s potential concerns about causing unrest by claiming the throne, pledging to protect both Rama and the kingdom as the shore protects the ocean. He urges Rama to proceed with the coronation, confident that he alone can repel any opposition.  Lakshmana emphasizes that his weapons are not for show but for crushing enemies, asserting he wouldn't hesitate to use them against anyone, even Indra.  He vividly describes the battlefield carnage he will unleash, filling the earth with the bodies of elephants, horses, and warriors, felled by his sword. He questions how any man could stand against him in battle.  Lakshmana boasts of his archery skills and promises to inflict devastating losses on the enemy. He concludes by declaring that his actions today will expose Dasaratha’s powerlessness and establish Rama’s sovereignty, affirming that his powerful arms, fit for adornment and generosity, will be used to eliminate those obstructing Rama’s coronation.

__data__.text: Kausalya, overwhelmed with grief, questioned how Rama, raised in luxury and kindness, could endure the hardships of the forest. She lamented his exile, expressing disbelief that such a fate could befall the beloved prince.  Kausalya declared her intention to accompany Rama, likening herself to a cow following her calf.  Rama gently responded, urging her to remain and care for their father, Dasaratha, as abandoning him would be cruel and against dharma (righteous conduct).  He emphasized the importance of serving their father as long as he lived.  Kausalya, pleased by Rama's wisdom and dutiful nature, accepted his advice.  Despite this resolution, Rama continued to console his grieving mother.

__data__.text: Rama explains to his grieving mother, Kausalya, that abandoning her husband, King Dasaratha, would be a grave transgression against dharma.  He reminds her that their father, as king, deserves their utmost respect and service. Kausalya, moved by Rama's wisdom and dutiful words, accepts his reasoning. However, Rama continues to console her, assuring her that he will return after fulfilling his father's command and spending the stipulated time in the forest. He reminds Kausalya that a wife's duty is to serve her husband, and that this is the path to righteousness.  He urges her to maintain her devotion to the king, observing religious practices and praying for his well-being during Rama's absence.  Kausalya, though still heartbroken, acknowledges Rama's unwavering resolve.  She tearfully expresses her hope to see him return safely from the forest, clad in ascetic garb. Recognizing the inevitability of his departure, Kausalya offers blessings and prayers for his safe return.

__data__.text: Queen Kausalya, accepting the inevitability of Rama's exile, advises him on his duties and expresses her hopes for his safe return.  She reminds Rama that the kingdom is not leaderless, as his brother Bharata will serve as regent.  She urges Rama to be mindful of King Dasaratha's grief, advising him to offer support and comfort to his aging father.  Kausalya counsels Rama to maintain his devotion to the king during his absence. She emphasizes a wife's duty to her husband, highlighting the spiritual merit gained through such service. Kausalya requests Rama to ensure that religious rites and offerings are continued in his absence. She then expresses her own intention to lead a disciplined life, dedicated to serving the king and awaiting Rama's return.  Overcome with sorrow, she acknowledges the power of destiny and bids farewell to Rama, offering blessings for his safe journey and successful completion of his exile. Kausalya finds solace in the hope that Rama will return safely, bringing her joy and comfort.

__data__.text: Queen Kausalya, overcoming her grief, blesses Rama for a safe journey.  She urges him to return swiftly after completing his exile. She prays for his protection, invoking blessings from piety, deities of various domains (temples, crossroads, natural elements), the mystic missiles granted by Vishwamitra, his own virtues, and the service he rendered to his parents. She continues her prayer, calling upon gods like Sadhyas, Vishwadevas, Maruts, the Cosmic Person, the creator, Pusa, Bhaga, Aryama, Indra, and the guardians of the spheres.  She further seeks blessings from deities presiding over seasons, months, years, days, nights, and hours.  Veda, Smriti texts, and Dharma are invoked for his protection. Finally, she implores Skanda, the moon god, Brihaspati, the seven Rishis, Narada, Siddhas, guardians of the four quarters, mountains, oceans, Varuna, heaven, earth, atmosphere, all creation, lunar mansions, sun, planets, and deities of day, night, and twilight to protect Rama during his time in the forest.

__data__.text: Kausalya continues her extensive blessings for Rama's safety in the forest. She calls upon the Siddhas, the guardians of the four directions, mountains, oceans, Varuna, and all of creation, including celestial bodies and their presiding deities, to protect him.  She invokes blessings from the deities of the seasons, months, and even the smallest units of time. She prays that gods and demons alike grant him happiness during his exile as a hermit. Kausalya further beseeches that Rama be safe from fear of ogres, fiends, and predatory animals, as well as from the annoyances of insects and reptiles. She prays for his protection from hostile elephants, lions, tigers, bears, buffaloes, and other dangerous creatures.  Finally, she prays that his journey be blessed, his endeavors successful, and that he be prosperous and happy. She reiterates her prayer for his safety from all beings, both earthly and celestial, including his adversaries.  Kausalya invokes Venus, the moon, the sun, Kubera, and Yama for his protection in the Dandaka forest. She asks the gods of fire, air, and smoke, as well as the sacred texts, to protect him during his daily rituals.  Lastly, she appeals to Brahma, the creator, and other unnamed deities and Rishis to safeguard Rama.  Having completed her prayers, Kausalya performs a ritual offering of flowers, perfumes, and hymns to the gods, and has a Brahmin conduct a fire ceremony for Rama's well-being.

__data__.text: Kausalya continued her blessings, invoking the gods of fire, air, smoke, and sacred texts to protect Rama during his rituals. She called upon Brahma, the creator, and other deities to safeguard him in the forest.  She then offered flowers, perfumes, and hymns to the gods, and had a Brahmin perform a fire ceremony for Rama's well-being, providing ghee, white flowers, firewood, and mustard seeds for the ritual.  The priest offered further oblations for Rama's safety and health, invoking blessings from the guardians of the directions. Kausalya then arranged for Brahmins to recite prayers for Rama's welfare, offering them honey, curds, rice, and ghee.  She gave the head Brahmin a generous fee, invoking blessings similar to those received by Indra and Garuda.  Kausalya applied rice to Rama's forehead, adorned him with sandal paste, tied a protective herb to his wrist with accompanying mantras, and spoke further blessings, though her heart was heavy with sorrow.

__data__.text: Kausalya invoked blessings upon Rama from various divine entities, including sages, oceans, lands, Vedas, and the cardinal directions.  She applied rice to his forehead and sandal paste, and tied a protective herb to his wrist, reciting mantras to enhance its power.  Despite her inner sorrow, she spoke the blessings aloud, maintaining a cheerful facade for Rama's sake.  She embraced him, urging him to depart peacefully and confidently.  She expressed her hope to see him return safely, having achieved his goals. Kausalya envisioned his return as joyful as the rising full moon, seeing him restored to the throne.  She blessed him to fulfill his wife Sita's desires upon his return. She called upon gods and divine beings to protect Rama during his journey.  With tear-filled eyes, Kausalya performed a ritual circumambulation of Rama, embracing him repeatedly before he finally departed for Sita's quarters, radiating with the blessings bestowed upon him.

__data__.text: Sita, unaware of the events that transpired, joyfully awaited Rama's return from his presumed coronation.  Observing his unexpected dejection and lack of customary celebratory fanfare, she questioned him about the change. Rama appeared distressed, pale, and perspiring. Sita noted the absence of the usual signs of a coronation: the ornate umbrella, the ceremonial fanning, the celebratory chants of bards and panegyrists, the ritualistic pouring of honey, curds, and holy water, and the customary procession of ministers, traders, and citizens.  She wondered why he wasn't riding in his decorated chariot, led by four swift horses.

__data__.text: Sita continued to express her confusion and concern over Rama's unexpected return, noting the absence of celebratory musicians and Brahmins who traditionally would have blessed him with honey, curds, and holy water after his coronation.  She also observed the lack of an official procession, questioning why ministers, traders, and citizens weren't following him, and why he wasn't riding in his customary decorated chariot preceded by a stately elephant.  Sita, deeply puzzled by Rama's changed demeanor and the missing signs of celebration, asked him directly about the reason for his distress. Rama then revealed to a distraught Sita that his father was sending him into exile in the forest.

__data__.text: Rama, preparing to leave for exile, instructs Sita on how to conduct herself in his absence. He advises her not to praise him in front of Bharata, the newly appointed regent, as powerful men are often intolerant of others' accolades.  He emphasizes the importance of respecting Bharata, the new king, and following his wishes to maintain a harmonious relationship.  Rama urges Sita to remain steadfast, to engage in religious practices and fasting, and to respect his father, Dasaratha, and his mother, Kausalya. He also asks her to treat his other mothers and his brothers, Bharata and Satrughna, with kindness and familial affection.  Rama stresses the importance of avoiding any conflict with Bharata, emphasizing his new role as ruler. He further advises Sita to be respectful and compliant with Bharata to ensure her well-being in Ayodhya.  Finally, Rama tells Sita of his imminent departure for the Dandaka forest and instructs her to remain in Ayodhya, behaving in a manner that avoids causing offense.

He reiterates his impending departure for the Dandaka forest and instructs Sita to remain in Ayodhya, conducting herself in a manner that avoids causing offense to anyone.

__data__.text: Sita, deeply hurt by Rama's suggestion that she remain in Ayodhya, rebukes him, questioning his logic.  She argues that his words are unbecoming of a warrior prince and contradict the destiny of a wife, which is to share her husband's fate. Sita emphasizes that a husband is a woman's sole refuge and declares her unwavering resolve to accompany him to the Dandaka forest, even walking ahead to clear the path.  She urges him to confidently take her, asserting her purity and lack of any wrongdoing. Sita proclaims that her husband's protection is paramount, surpassing even heavenly comforts. She reminds Rama of her upbringing and her understanding of wifely duties, dismissing the need for further instruction.  Sita expresses her willingness to face the forest's dangers, finding happiness in serving him.  She affirms her faith in Rama's ability to protect her and reiterates her unwavering determination to join him in exile.

__data__.text: Rama, concerned about the hardships of forest life, advised Sita against accompanying him. He attempted to dissuade her, highlighting the difficulties she would face.  He described the forest as a place of constant misery, with the roars of lions echoing through the mountains, and wild animals posing a threat.  He explained that the rivers would be difficult to cross, the paths thorny and rough, and that she would have to sleep on a bed of leaves.  He further detailed the necessity of foraging for fallen fruits, observing fasts, wearing matted hair and bark clothing, and performing rituals for deities and guests. Rama emphasized the demanding nature of forest life, requiring adherence to strict routines and ablutions.

__data__.text: Rama continued to dissuade Sita from accompanying him, emphasizing the hardships of forest life. He explained that sustenance would come from fallen fruits, requiring self-control even in hunger.  Fasting, matted hair, and bark clothing would become necessary.  Daily rituals for deities, ancestors, and unexpected guests would be required, along with strict adherence to ablutions three times a day.  He stressed the need to personally gather flowers for worship and to be content with seasonal food.  Rama painted a bleak picture of fierce winds, pervasive darkness, constant hunger, and lurking dangers. He described numerous snakes, some river-dwelling, blocking paths.  He warned of relentless insects like moths, scorpions, worms, gnats, and mosquitoes.  The terrain itself would be challenging, with thorny trees, sharp grasses, and tangled shrubs. He reiterated the physical and mental hardships and the need to renounce anger and greed while embracing asceticism and fearlessness.  Concluding his appeal, Rama urged Sita to reconsider, perceiving the forest as unsuitable and dangerous for her.  Unmoved, Sita prepared to respond.

She assured him that the forest animals, unfamiliar with his presence, would pose no threat.  Emphasizing her duty as his wife, Sita declared she could not live without him and that even Indra could not harm her while she was at Rama's side.  She reminded Rama of the prophecy foretelling her forest dwelling, heard from Brahmins at her father's home and from a hermitess in her mother's presence.  Sita expressed her excitement and longing for this forest life with him, confident that the hardships would not affect her. She eagerly anticipated serving him in the forest, believing it to be a joyous experience.

Citing religious texts, she emphasized the eternal bond between husband and wife, questioning Rama's refusal to take her. Sita declared her unwavering devotion and willingness to share in both his joys and sorrows.  Desperate, she threatened to take her own life if he left her behind.  Despite her pleas, Rama remained resolute in his decision not to take her to the forest.  Overcome with anxiety and anger, Sita wept.  Seeing her distress, Rama attempted to console her.

__data__.text: Sita, deeply saddened by the prospect of separation, challenged Rama's decision. She questioned his manliness, suggesting that her father, King Janaka, wouldn't have accepted a son-in-law lacking courage.  Sita expressed concern for Rama's reputation, fearing that people would perceive him as weak if he left her behind. She affirmed her unwavering devotion, comparing herself to the loyal Savitri. Sita declared she could never love another and insisted on accompanying him. She questioned how Rama could abandon his wife, particularly one who had been with him since childhood.  Sita urged Rama to consider Bharat's wishes, for whom he was making this sacrifice. She emphasized that he shouldn't go to the forest without her, regardless of whether it was for austerities, a simpler life, or even heaven. Sita asserted that the journey wouldn't burden her; following Rama would be as effortless as a leisurely walk.  She claimed that even the harsh forest terrain would feel soft to her feet in his presence.  Sita romanticized the hardships, stating that even dust raised by storms would be like fragrant sandal powder. She declared that sleeping on the forest floor with Rama would surpass the comfort of the finest beds. Finally, Sita expressed that any food Rama provided, regardless of its simplicity, would be as sweet as nectar to her.

__data__.text: Sita continued to plead with Rama, arguing that the forest's hardships would be trivial in his company.  She envisioned the rough terrain feeling like soft cotton, the dust from storms like fragrant sandal powder, and the forest floor more comfortable than any bed.  Any food he provided, no matter how simple, would be like nectar.  She insisted she would miss neither her family nor her home, finding complete contentment with him.  Sita assured Rama she wouldn't be a burden and that he shouldn't worry about her well-being.  She declared that being with him, even in the forest, would be heavenly, while any place without him would feel like hell.  If he refused to take her, she threatened to take her own life, claiming death in his presence was preferable to living without him.  Overcome with grief, Sita wept, her tears flowing like water from lotuses, her face, usually radiant as the moon, now withered by sorrow.  Witnessing her distress, Rama embraced her and attempted to comfort her.  He assured Sita that causing her pain brought him no joy, and that he feared nothing.

__data__.text: Sita, overcome with grief, wept. Her tears, sparkling like crystals, flowed from her lotus-like eyes. Her usually radiant face, now pale with sorrow, was withered like a lotus plucked from water.  Seeing her faint with distress, Rama embraced her and began to console her. He assured her that her sorrow pained him deeply and that he feared nothing.  He explained that, unaware of her unwavering resolve, he initially hesitated to bring her to the forest, despite his ability to protect her. However, recognizing her determination to share his exile, he acknowledged her inseparable connection to him and agreed to her accompanying him. He affirmed his commitment to uphold the dharma practiced by forest-dwelling sages.  Rama emphasized his obligation to fulfill his father's promise, declaring that obedience to one's parents is a sacred duty. He stressed the importance of honoring one's parents and teachers, stating that such service surpasses even traditional worship in its spiritual significance.  He highlighted the numerous blessings—heavenly bliss, wealth, knowledge, sons, and happiness—that come from fulfilling their wishes.  Rama reiterated his father's unwavering commitment to truth and virtue, which he was determined to emulate. Finally, seeing Sita's steadfast resolve to join him in the Dandaka forest, Rama relented and agreed to take her with him.

__data__.text: Rama explains to Sita the importance of obeying elders, highlighting the spiritual merit and material blessings that come from fulfilling their wishes. He compares this obedience to the dharma practiced by forest sages.  He states his resolve to follow his father's command, who is steadfast in truth and virtue.  Seeing Sita’s unwavering determination to join him, Rama finally agrees to take her to the Dandaka forest.  He lovingly calls her "timid lady with flawless limbs and bewitching eyes," inviting her to be his partner in the ascetic life of the forest.  Rama praises Sita's decision as befitting their noble lineage.  He instructs her to prepare for their forest exile, urging her to quickly distribute their belongings – jewels, fine clothes, household items, furnishings, and conveyances – among Brahmins and the needy.  Sita, delighted by Rama’s acceptance, joyfully begins distributing their possessions as instructed.

He declared his unwavering devotion and insisted that he would walk ahead, armed, ensuring their safety and comfort amidst the wildlife.  Lakshmana expressed his disinterest in heavenly pleasures or power without them. Though Rama initially tried to dissuade him, Lakshmana questioned the sudden change of heart, reminding Rama of his previous permission. Rama, acknowledging Lakshmana's loyalty and virtues, expressed his concern for their mothers, Kausalya and Sumitra, who would be left vulnerable to Kaikeyi's potential ill-treatment after their departure and Bharata's ascension to the throne. He implored Lakshmana to remain in Ayodhya and care for them, securing their well-being by his own efforts or by earning the king's favor.

__data__.text:
Rama, concerned about his mothers' well-being after his departure, advised Lakshmana to stay in Ayodhya. He explained that their father, swayed by Kaikeyi, wouldn't ensure their safety, and Bharata, influenced by his mother, might also neglect them.  Rama urged Lakshmana to care for Kausalya and Sumitra, either independently or by earning the new king's favor. He assured Lakshmana that such service would be equivalent to devotion to Rama himself.

However, Lakshmana countered, arguing that Bharata would undoubtedly respect their mothers due to Rama's inherent influence.  He pledged that if Bharata failed in his duty, he would personally challenge and defeat him, along with any supporters. Lakshmana asserted that Kausalya was more than capable of supporting herself and others, relieving him of that responsibility. He then passionately reiterated his desire to serve Rama and Sita in the forest, promising to scout ahead, clear the path, and gather food, ensuring their comfort and safety at all times.

__data__.text: Lakshmana, fully equipped for the journey, detailed his plan to serve Rama and Sita in the forest. He envisioned himself scouting ahead, clearing their path, and foraging for suitable food, ensuring their comfort and safety.  Pleased with Lakshmana's dedication, Rama instructed him to bid farewell to their loved ones and retrieve the divine weapons gifted to Rama by King Janaka. These weapons, including powerful bows, impenetrable armor, inexhaustible quivers, and radiant swords, were stored at their preceptor Vasistha's residence.  Lakshmana promptly obeyed, returning with the sacred armaments.  Rama, impressed by Lakshmana's swiftness, expressed his desire to distribute his wealth among Brahmins and ascetics before departing, including those devoted to their teachers and residing within the palace, as well as his other dependents.  He then instructed Lakshmana to bring forth Suyajna, Vasistha's son and a respected Brahmin, along with other learned Brahmins, so that Rama could honor them before embarking on his journey to the forest.

__data__.text: Lakshmana, following Rama's order, went to Suyajna's residence.  He invited Suyajna to Rama's palace to witness an important event. Suyajna completed his midday prayers and accompanied Lakshmana to Rama's opulent palace.  Rama and Sita rose to greet the learned Suyajna with respect. Rama presented Suyajna with armlets, earrings, necklaces, bracelets, and other ornaments.  At Sita's urging, Rama offered Suyajna a pearl necklace, a gold chain, and a girdle for Suyajna's wife. Sita also offered ornate armlets and other jewelry for Suyajna's wife.  Additionally, she gifted a jeweled couch with a fine coverlet. Rama also gifted Suyajna an elephant named Satrunjaya and a thousand gold coins.  Suyajna accepted the gifts and blessed Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita. Rama then instructed Lakshmana to honor and bestow gifts upon Agastya and Kausika, two prominent Brahmins, with cows, gold, silver, and gems.

__data__.text: Rama addressed Lakshmana, instructing him to honor Agastya and Kausika, prominent Brahmins, with gifts of cows, gold, silver, and gems. He further directed Lakshmana to generously reward the Brahmin who attended to Kausalya, providing him with a conveyance, servants, silk robes, and ample wealth.  Rama also instructed Lakshmana to reward Citraratha, his charioteer and minister, with jewels, clothing, riches, and livestock.  He then directed Lakshmana to give camels laden with jewels, and bullocks carrying rice to the Vedic students who devoted themselves to studying the Katha and Kalapa recensions.  Rama also ordered a thousand cows to be given to Brahmins maintaining sacred fires. He instructed Lakshmana to provide cows or gold coins to the celibates attending Kausalya so they could afford their preceptor's fees and marriage expenses. He emphasized that Lakshmana should ensure Kausalya was pleased with the generosity of the gifts. Lakshmana then distributed the wealth as instructed.  Rama addressed his tearful dependents, promising them sustenance during his exile and assigning them the task of guarding his and Lakshmana's residences in their absence. He then summoned his treasurer and had his vast wealth brought forth.

__data__.text: Rama, having provided for his dependents during his exile, entrusted them with the care of his and Lakshmana's residences. He then summoned his treasurer and had his vast wealth brought forth. The dependents assembled the impressive hoard. With Lakshmana's assistance, Rama distributed this wealth to Brahmins, both young and old, and to those in need.  Among those seeking aid was a Brahmin named Trijata, who lived in poverty in the nearby forest. Driven by desperation, his wife urged him to approach Rama for assistance. Trijata, wearing a tattered loincloth, made his way to Rama's palace, his spiritual presence evident.  He appealed to Rama, highlighting his poverty and many children. Rama playfully challenged Trijata, offering him as many cows as he could encompass with a throw of his staff.  Trijata, invigorated, threw his staff with all his might. The staff flew across the Sarayu River and landed near a bull amidst a vast herd of grazing cows.  Honoring the unconventional measure, Rama bestowed upon Trijata all the cows from the staff's landing point up to the riverbank.

__data__.text: Rama reassured Trijata, the Brahmin, that he would receive a substantial gift of cows.  Trijata, energized, threw his staff with great force. It soared across the Sarayu River and landed amidst a vast herd.  True to his word, Rama granted Trijata all the cows from the staff's landing point to the riverbank.  Rama then comforted Trijata, explaining that the challenge was merely a playful test of his strength. He further invited Trijata to request anything else he desired, emphasizing that his wealth was meant for the benefit of Brahmins and brought him honor through its distribution.  Overjoyed, Trijata and his wife accepted the cows and blessed Rama with increased fame, strength, joy, and happiness.  Inspired by these blessings, Rama distributed the remainder of his vast, righteously earned wealth to his loved ones.  He ensured that every Brahmin, relative, dependent, and impoverished person in Ayodhya received gifts and attention according to their needs, leaving none unsatisfied.

Having distributed his immense riches among the Brahmanas and others,
Sri Rama, accompanied by Sita and Laksmana, proceeds to his father’s
gynaeceum to take leave of him. Remaining unruffled even on

to apprise their royal father of their arrival

__data__.text: Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana, having given much wealth to the Brahmins, went to see their father, King Dasaratha.  Their weapons, carried by servants, were adorned with garlands and sandalwood paste, likely applied by Sita.  As they walked, people watched sadly from rooftops and balconies, unable to navigate the crowded streets.  They lamented Rama's exile, observing that he who once commanded armies now walked with only Sita and Lakshmana.  The people praised Rama's virtue and commitment to his father's word, despite the luxuries he was forfeiting.  They expressed sorrow for Sita, now exposed to the elements, and questioned Dasaratha's decision, believing only an evil influence could cause him to banish such a virtuous son, beloved by all.  The citizens felt Rama's loss deeply, likening it to a drought harming water creatures or damage to a tree's roots affecting the whole plant.  Some even expressed a desire to join Rama in exile.

__data__.text: As Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana journeyed, the citizens lamented Rama's exile. They extolled his virtues – harmlessness, compassion, learning, amiability, self-control, and tranquility – and mourned his departure as a profound loss, like a drought or damage to a tree's roots.  Some even declared their intent to join him, suggesting they would abandon their homes and follow Rama into the wilderness. They envisioned the forest becoming their city and Ayodhya, deserted by them, turning into a forest. They imagined even wild animals fleeing in fear of them.  They contrasted their willing exile with Kaikeyi's supposed enjoyment of the vacated city, describing the empty palaces as desolate and neglected.  Despite the emotional outpouring and strong words from the people, Rama remained undisturbed and continued towards his father's palace.

__data__.text: Despite the citizens' emotional pleas and pronouncements to follow him into exile, Rama remained composed.  He heard their varied laments, but his resolve did not waver.  He continued towards Kaikeyi's palace, where his father still resided.  Upon reaching the palace, guarded by vigilant soldiers, Rama noticed Sumantra standing nearby, looking dejected. Although witnessing the distress of the people of Ayodhya, Rama appeared serene as he approached his father, ready to obey his command.  He then instructed Sumantra to announce his arrival to the king.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XXXIV

Apprised of Sri Rama’s arrival by Sumantra, Dagaratha commands him to
usher in Sri Rama with his consort. Seeing the Emperor fall unconscious
at the very sight of the heir-apparent, the ladies of the royal household
start wailing piteously. On Sri Rama’s soliciting his permission to
retire to the woods, the king enjoins him to take his father
captive and ascend the throne. Sri Rama in his turn
consoles his father by assuring him that he has no
hankering for royal fortune and that to him
obedience to his father’s command is of
utmost importance. Dasaratha, however,
falls unconscious again at the very thought
of his separation from Sri Rama and
Sumantra as well as all the ladies of
the gynaeceum follow suit

__data__.text: Sumantra informed King Dasaratha of Rama's arrival.  Dasaratha, overcome with emotion, asked Sumantra to bring Rama to him, along with his wives.  Sumantra found the king distressed and grieving for Rama. He relayed Rama's message, explaining that Rama, after donating his wealth, wished to see his father before leaving for the forest.  Dasaratha requested his wives’ presence.  Sumantra conveyed the king's message, and the royal ladies, led by Kausalya, proceeded to the king.  Seeing them arrive, Dasaratha instructed Sumantra to bring Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita before him.  Sumantra promptly returned with them.

__data__.text: The king's wives, led by Kausalya, proceeded to see Dasaratha, their eyes filled with sorrow at the thought of Rama's departure.  Dasaratha, upon their arrival, asked Sumantra to bring Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita to him.  The charioteer quickly returned with them.  Seeing Rama approach, Dasaratha rose from his seat, overwhelmed with grief, and fainted before reaching his son. Rama and Lakshmana rushed to their father's side, while the palace echoed with the cries of the women. Gently, Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita lifted the unconscious king and placed him on a couch.  When Dasaratha regained consciousness, Rama, with folded hands and tearful eyes, requested his father's permission to leave for the Dandaka forest. He also asked that Lakshmana and Sita be allowed to accompany him, despite his earlier attempts to dissuade them.  Rama implored his father to grant them leave, comparing his situation to that of Brahma granting his sons leave to pursue ascetic life in the forest.  Observing Rama patiently awaiting his approval, Dasaratha, still distraught over Kaikeyi's influence, urged Rama to take control of Ayodhya and imprison him, declaring that he had been robbed of his senses by Kaikeyi's boon.  In response, the righteous Rama respectfully addressed his father.

__data__.text: Dasaratha, overwhelmed with grief, implored Rama to take the throne, declaring himself incapacitated by Kaikeyi's manipulation.  Rama, however, calmly requested his father's permission to fulfill his promise and depart for the forest with Sita and Lakshmana.  He reassured his father that he held no desire for kingship and that obedience to his father's command, even this painful one, was paramount.  Rama promised to return after fourteen years. The king, torn between his love for Rama and his unwilling adherence to his given word, lamented his predicament. He urged Rama to depart on an auspicious journey, acknowledging the unwavering nature of Rama's commitment. Dasaratha then pleaded with Rama to delay his departure, even just for one night, so he could find solace in his presence.  He reminded Rama of his filial duty and the difficulty of such a sacrifice. Dasaratha admitted his powerlessness against Kaikeyi's deceptive influence, lamenting that he had been manipulated into this situation. He praised Rama's nobility in upholding a forced promise. He acknowledged and appreciated Rama's unwavering commitment to truth and righteousness, even in the face of such adversity.  Overcome by grief, Dasaratha made one final plea for Rama to stay, while Rama and Lakshmana listened with distress.

__data__.text: Dasaratha, lamenting his manipulation by Kaikeyi, expresses his sorrow at Rama's exile. He acknowledges Rama's nobility in upholding the unwilling promise extracted from him.  Rama, in response to his father's grief, calmly reiterates his decision to depart for the forest. He emphasizes his lack of desire for worldly pleasures and kingship, stating that obedience to his father's command is his foremost priority.  He offers the kingdom to Bharata and declares his unwavering resolve to fulfill the promise, asking his father not to grieve.  Rama reassures Dasaratha that he will find contentment in the forest, urging him to maintain composure. He reaffirms his commitment to truth and righteousness, emphasizing that there is no possibility of altering his decision. He reminds Dasaratha of Kaikeyi's command and his own vow to obey, thereby demonstrating his steadfast adherence to his word.  He promises to embrace the forest life and encourages his father to relinquish his worries.

__data__.text: Rama swears by his virtue and past deeds, urging his father to stay true to his word and allow him to fulfill his promise.  He insists he cannot remain home any longer and implores Dasaratha to control his grief, as there is no turning back. He reminds his father of Kaikeyi's command and his own vow to obey, emphasizing the necessity of honoring his pledge.  Rama assures his father that he will find happiness in the forest amongst the animals and nature. He reiterates his respect for his father, likening him to a deity whose commands must be obeyed.  He promises to return after fourteen years, urging Dasaratha to relinquish his sorrow.  Rama questions his father's grief, pointing out that Dasaratha should be the one offering comfort, not needing it himself.  He renounces his claim to the kingdom, suggesting that Bharata rule in his stead.  He expresses his disinterest in worldly pleasures and kingship, prioritizing obedience to his father's command above all else. He reiterates his lack of desire for the throne, the earth, or even Sita, compared to fulfilling his father’s promise to Kaikeyi.  He envisions a peaceful existence in the forest, living on fruits and roots, and asks his father to find peace in this decision.  Overcome with grief and anguish, Dasaratha faints upon hearing Rama's words. All the queens present, except Kaikeyi, begin to weep, and Sumantra also faints, leaving the scene filled with lamentations.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, overwhelmed by grief and pain after hearing Rama's resolute words, fainted and collapsed to the ground, showing no signs of life.  All the queens present, with the exception of Kaikeyi, began to weep and wail.  Sumantra, also overcome by the scene, fainted as well, adding to the atmosphere of lamentation and despair.

__data__.text: Sumantra, deeply distressed by Kaikeyi's insistence on Rama's exile, rebukes her sharply. He expresses his disapproval through his actions and words, comparing her actions to murdering her husband and destroying her family.  He questions her disregard for Dasaratha, highlighting that a husband's wishes should supersede even those of numerous sons. Sumantra emphasizes the established tradition of inheritance and criticizes Kaikeyi for disrupting it. He declares his loyalty to Rama, stating that he and others will follow Rama into exile. Sumantra condemns Kaikeyi's actions, wondering why the earth doesn't split open in response to her behavior and why the harsh words of the Brahmana sages haven't affected her. He expresses his disbelief at the impropriety of her actions, given the potential loss of support from her family and the wider community.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: Sumantra continues his condemnation of Kaikeyi, asserting that no respectable person, let alone a Brahmana, should remain in her kingdom if she proceeds with her unjust demand to exile Rama. He declares his unwavering loyalty to Rama, stating that he and others will follow Rama into exile, leaving Kaikeyi isolated from her family, Brahmanas, and all righteous individuals. He questions what satisfaction she could possibly derive from ruling a kingdom devoid of such support.  Sumantra expresses his astonishment that the earth itself doesn't react to her appalling behavior.  He wonders why the strong rebukes of the Brahmana sages haven't swayed her from her destructive course. He then draws an analogy, comparing nurturing Kaikeyi with nurturing a Neem tree (known for its bitterness) after cutting down a mango tree (known for its sweetness).  Just as a Neem tree cannot be made sweet even with milk, Kaikeyi's inherent nature, like her mother's, is incapable of producing goodness. Sumantra recounts the story of Kaikeyi's mother and how she manipulated her husband, Dasaratha's father.  He relates how Dasaratha's father was granted the ability to understand the language of all beings and how this led to a situation where his laughter over something a bird said angered his wife. Fearful of her wrath, he initially refused to tell her the reason for his laughter but eventually confided in the sage who granted him the boon. The sage advised him not to reveal the secret, even if it meant his wife leaving him.  Sumantra uses this story to illustrate Kaikeyi's inherited tendency towards manipulative behavior and her influence on Dasaratha's current predicament.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Sumantra continues his impassioned plea to Kaikeyi, recounting how her father wisely heeded the advice of a sage and refused to divulge a secret to his wife, even under threat of her departure. He draws a parallel to Kaikeyi's own behavior, suggesting that she has inherited her mother's manipulative tendencies and is now leading Dasaratha down a dangerous path. He urges her to abandon her wicked advisors and uphold her husband's wishes, reminding her that Dasaratha, like the world's protector, should not be swayed into improper actions.  Sumantra emphasizes Dasaratha's unwavering commitment to his promises and advocates for Rama's rightful ascension to the throne. He warns Kaikeyi of the widespread condemnation she will face if Rama is exiled and assures her that Rama is the only suitable ruler for Ayodhya.  Finally, he predicts Dasaratha's retreat to the forest if Rama is appointed Prince Regent. Despite Sumantra's blend of gentle persuasion and sharp rebukes, Kaikeyi remains unmoved, showing no outward sign of emotion or change of heart.

__data__.text: Sumantra, with a combination of gentle words and sharp reprimands, directly addressed Kaikeyi in the royal court, attempting to sway her from her destructive course.  However, Kaikeyi remained completely impassive, showing no visible reaction to his emotional appeal. Her face remained unchanged, betraying no sign of either agitation or remorse.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XXXVI

DaSaratha instructs Sumantra to take a detachment of the army and the
exchequer alongwith Sri Rama, to which Kaikeyi objects and insists
on his being sent into exile without any resources on the analogy
of Asamafija, son of the king’s forefather, Sagara. Another minister
of the king, Siddhartha by name, who was present there, opposes
Kaikeyi and urges that the analogy of Asamafija, who was a
perverse lad, could not be applied in the case of Sri Rama,
who possessed an ideal character and deserved in every
way to be installed in the office of Prince Regent. In
the event of Kaikeyi vetoing the proposal, the king
threatens to accompany the heir-apparent

__data__.text: Dasaratha, grieving over his forced promise to Kaikeyi, instructs Sumantra to prepare a grand retinue for Rama's exile.  He requests an army detachment, provisions, merchants, entertainers, and Rama's favored wrestlers to accompany him.  Dasaratha further orders that his own treasury and granaries be sent with Rama, ensuring his comfort in the forest. He envisions Rama hunting, enjoying nature, performing religious rites, and living contentedly while Bharata governs Ayodhya.  Overhearing this, Kaikeyi becomes fearful and interrupts. She declares that Bharata will not accept a kingdom stripped of its wealth, comparing it to flavorless wine.  Dasaratha, angered by her callous words, questions why she torments him further and didn't stipulate these conditions earlier. This only fuels Kaikeyi’s anger, prompting a sharp retort.

__data__.text: Kaikeyi argues that Bharata will reject a kingdom emptied of its treasury, comparing it to flavorless wine.  Dasaratha, in growing frustration, questions Kaikeyi's cruel timing, asking why she didn't specify these conditions earlier.  Infuriated, Kaikeyi retorts by citing the example of King Sagara who banished his eldest son, Asamanja.  She insists Rama should similarly be sent away with nothing. Dasaratha, ashamed by her comparison, exclaims, “Shame!”  A sentiment echoed by the onlookers. Undeterred, Kaikeyi remains firm in her resolve.  The respected minister, Siddhartha, intervenes, reminding Kaikeyi of Asamanja's wickedness:  He threw children playing in the streets into the Sarayu river for his amusement. The terrified citizens begged Sagara to choose between them and his cruel son.  Sagara, heeding his people's pleas, banished Asamanja, providing him with only basic tools for survival. Siddhartha emphasizes Asamanja's deserved punishment for his cruel actions and contrasts it with Rama's impeccable character, asking what wrong Rama has committed to warrant such harsh treatment.  He concludes by stating that finding a fault in Rama is as impossible as finding a blemish on the new moon.

__data__.text: Siddhartha continues his argument against Rama's exile. He recounts Asamanja's banishment, highlighting that the king's son was provided with basic necessities – a spade and basket – for his survival in the wilderness.  Siddhartha then pointedly asks what wrong Rama has committed to justify such a severe punishment, emphasizing Rama's flawless character and the impossibility of finding fault with him, comparing it to finding a blemish on the new moon. He implores Kaikeyi to state Rama's transgression, assuring her that if a valid reason is presented, Rama will be exiled. Siddhartha argues that exiling the innocent and righteous Rama would be a grave injustice, detrimental even to the most powerful. He cautions Kaikeyi against such an action, warning that public condemnation will surely follow.  Overhearing this exchange, a grief-stricken Dasaratha, his voice weakened with sorrow, reproaches Kaikeyi for her callous decision. He questions her understanding of his and her own well-being, lamenting her unwise choice and its deviation from righteousness. Dasaratha declares his intention to relinquish his kingdom and all its comforts to accompany Rama into exile, urging Kaikeyi to rule with Bharata.

Canto XXXVII

Sri Rama urges his servants to fetch the bark of trees for being used as his
wearing apparel. At this Kaikeyi herself brings them the desired dress,
which both Sri Rama and Laksmana cover themselves with, taking off

their princely robes. Sita, however, finds it difficult to wear the
bark supplied to her and ultimately puts it on over her own
dress with the help of Sri Rama despite the remonstrances
of Vasistha, who severely castigates Kaikeyi
for her cruelty in exiling them

__data__.text: Upon hearing the chief minister's objection, Rama politely addressed King Dasaratha, stating that an army was unnecessary for his forest exile, as he intended to live off the land. He compared clinging to an army after relinquishing royal comforts to keeping an elephant's tether after giving the elephant away.  Rama requested simple forest attire from Kaikeyi's servants. Kaikeyi herself, shamelessly presented Rama and Lakshmana with bark garments before the assembled crowd.  Rama and Lakshmana accepted the bark clothing and exchanged their princely robes for the ascetic garb.  Sita, accustomed to silk, was distressed by the rough bark clothing.  Overwhelmed and unsure how to wear it, she fumbled with the unfamiliar material.  Rama, ever compassionate, helped her don the bark garment over her silken clothes.

__data__.text: Sita, unfamiliar with forest attire, struggled to put on the bark garment.  She fumbled with the pieces, feeling embarrassed by her inexperience.  Rama, ever mindful of others' needs, gently helped her drape the bark cloth over her silken dress.  Witnessing this, the women of the palace wept, lamenting Sita's undeserved exile.  They pleaded with Rama to leave Sita behind, arguing that she was not obligated to share his hardship. They urged him to go with Lakshmana alone, while Sita remained in the comfort of the palace.  Despite their heartfelt pleas, Rama continued to secure Sita's bark garment, mirroring her quiet acceptance of the situation.  Vasistha, observing this exchange, intervened.  He rebuked Kaikeyi for her heartless actions, declaring that Sita would not go into exile.  He asserted that Sita, as Rama's wife, would remain and assume the throne. Vasistha prophesied that if Sita accompanied Rama, the entire kingdom – people, resources, and even the city itself – would follow them into the forest. He further predicted that Bharata and Shatrughna would also join Rama in his forest exile, emulating his ascetic life.

__data__.text: Vasistha continues to berate Kaikeyi for her cruelty. He argues that Sita, as Rama's wife, is entitled to rule alongside him and will not be exiled.  He foretells that if Sita accompanies Rama, the entire kingdom, its resources, and its people will follow them into the forest.  Even Bharata and Shatrughna will emulate Rama's ascetic life in exile. Vasistha paints a bleak picture of Kaikeyi ruling a desolate kingdom, abandoned by all. He asserts that Bharata would never accept the throne under such circumstances and would remain loyal to Rama.  Vasistha then turns to Sita, urging her to reject the bark clothing and wear her customary finery. He emphasizes that Kaikeyi's request only pertained to Rama's exile, not Sita's. He insists that Sita should accompany Rama with all the comforts and attendants befitting a princess. Despite Vasistha's passionate pleas, Sita remains resolute in her decision to share Rama's exile.

__data__.text: Vasistha, the king's esteemed advisor and a highly influential Brahmin, implored Sita to reconsider her decision to wear the bark clothing and accompany Rama into exile. He argued that Kaikeyi's demand only applied to Rama and that Sita, as a princess, was entitled to remain in the palace with all her comforts and attendants. However, Sita, deeply devoted to her husband and determined to share his fate, remained unwavering in her resolve to join him in exile despite Vasistha's fervent pleas.

Incensed to hear the loud wailing of those present at the scene, on Sita being
dressed as a hermitess, DaSaratha reproaches Kaikeyi. While departing,
Sri Rama entreats his father to take care of his mother

Canto XXXVIII

Incensed to hear the loud wailing of those present at the scene, on Sita being
dressed as a hermitess, DaSaratha reproaches Kaikeyi. While departing,
Sri Rama entreats his father to take care of his mother

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, grieved by the public outcry against Sita's exile, laments his powerlessness.  He rebukes Kaikeyi, arguing that the delicate, young Sita, accustomed to comfort, doesn't deserve such harsh treatment. He questions why the innocent princess is being forced to wear bark clothing like a common hermit. Dasaratha insists that Sita should not be subject to this exile, reminding Kaikeyi that his original promise only concerned Rama. He feels his rash oath and Kaikeyi's subsequent actions, particularly forcing this humiliation upon Sita, will be his ruin.  He asks Kaikeyi what wrong Sita, gentle and kind, has ever done her.  Dasaratha pleads with her to be satisfied with Rama's exile and not compound her sins by exiling Sita as well. He reminds her that he only agreed to Rama's exile, not Sita's.  As Rama departs for the forest, he addresses his dejected father, asking him to care for his kind and elderly mother, Kausalya, who has always spoken well of him.
