__data__.text: Dasaratha continues to criticize Kaikeyi, arguing that exiling Sita, a princess accustomed to luxury, to a life in the forest wearing rough bark clothing is a cruel and unnecessary punishment exceeding his original agreement.  As Rama begins his journey into exile, he turns to his grieving father and requests that he take special care of his mother Kausalya, who is elderly, kind, and has always spoken highly of Dasaratha. Rama implores his father to comfort and support Kausalya in his absence, so that she doesn't succumb to grief and despair over losing her son.  He urges Dasaratha to ensure Kausalya's well-being, emphasizing his mother's advanced age and vulnerability to such emotional distress.

Sent away by Dagaratha, Sumantra gets a chariot ready to take Sri Rama
and his party to the forest and Sita adorns herself with jewels
brought by the Chancellor of the Exchequer. Kausalya
tenders opportune advice to her daughter-in-law and

and offers apology to his other mothers,
who burst into a wail

__data__.text: King Dasharatha, overcome with grief upon seeing Rama dressed as a hermit, fainted.  He was unable to bear the sight of his son in such a state.  Upon regaining consciousness, the king lamented his past actions, believing his current misfortune to be a karmic consequence.  Despite his immense sorrow, he acknowledged that life and death are governed by fate.  He blamed Kaikeyi for the family's suffering, attributing her selfish motives to the present crisis.  Overwhelmed with emotion, Dasharatha managed to utter Rama's name once before being overcome by tears.  He then instructed Sumantra to prepare a chariot and escort Rama out of the kingdom. Lamenting the injustice of a virtuous son being exiled by his own parents, Dasharatha summoned the treasurer and ordered him to provide Sita with fine clothing and jewels befitting her station and the duration of her exile.

__data__.text: Sumantra quickly prepared a decorated chariot and informed Rama of its readiness.  The king summoned the treasurer and ordered him to provide Sita with suitable clothing and jewels for her exile.  The treasurer promptly delivered the items to Sita.  Sita adorned herself with the jewels.  Kausalya embraced Sita and advised her to remain devoted to Rama, even in exile, reminding her that a virtuous wife's loyalty should remain unwavering regardless of her husband's circumstances. Kausalya cautioned against the behavior of those who abandon their spouses during difficult times. Sita respectfully replied that she would heed Kausalya's advice and affirmed her understanding of wifely duties.

__data__.text: Kausalya advised Sita, emphasizing that a husband should be the most sacred figure in a virtuous woman's life.  She urged Sita not to despise Rama due to his exile, reminding her that he remains her divine consort regardless of their circumstances. Sita respectfully assured Kausalya that she understood her wifely duties and would remain devoted to Rama. She declared her inability to stray from virtue, comparing it to the moon's inseparable connection to its light. Sita used metaphors to illustrate her point, stating a vina is useless without strings, a chariot without wheels, and a woman without a husband cannot find happiness, even with many sons. She affirmed that a husband provides unmatched joy, surpassing the happiness offered by parents, siblings, or children.  Sita reiterated her understanding of wifely dharma (duty) and questioned how she could possibly disregard her husband.  Kausalya, deeply moved by Sita's words, wept with a mixture of sorrow and joy.  Observing his mother's distress, Rama comforted her, promising that their exile would end soon and they would return to Ayodhya. He then addressed his stepmothers, asking for their forgiveness for any past transgressions and bidding them farewell.  His words, full of respect and righteousness, resonated with the grieving women.

__data__.text: Rama, having addressed his mother Kausalya, turned to his many stepmothers and observed their shared distress.  Respectful and mindful of righteousness, he asked for their forgiveness for any unintentional offenses he might have committed during their time together.  He then bade them farewell.  His sincere words were met with a chorus of wails and lamentations from the grieving women, transforming the once joyous palace, filled with music and celebration, into a place of sorrow and despair.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XL

Going clockwise round DaSaratha (as a token of respect), Sita, Rama and
Laksmana greet him. Accompanied by Sita, Sri Rama salutes Kausalya.
Laksmana too hails Kausalya first and then his own mother, Sumitra.
Sumitra for her part tenders salutary advice to her son. The princes
and the princess having mounted the chariot, Sumantra flicks
the horses. The citizens that had assembled, closely follow
the chariot; but unable to keep pace with its swift
movement, they return desolate. Dasaratha too
runs after the chariot alongwith Kausalya
and others, but being unable to walk
begins to totter and stops short
on the road yielding to the
remonstrances of his
wise ministers

__data__.text: Rama, Sita, and a grieving Lakshmana circled King Dasaratha, touching his feet as a mark of respect before their departure.  After taking leave of the king, Rama, accompanied by Sita, bowed to his mother, Kausalya. Lakshmana also paid his respects to Kausalya and then to his own mother, Sumitra.  A weeping Sumitra embraced Lakshmana, advising him to faithfully serve his elder brother Rama during their exile. She emphasized that Rama should be his sole refuge and guide, reminding him of their family's tradition of service and sacrifice. Sumitra blessed both Rama and Lakshmana for their journey.  She told Lakshmana to consider Rama as his father, Sita as his mother, and the forest as their home.  Sumantra, the charioteer, respectfully addressed Rama, urging him to board the chariot and promising a swift journey. He reminded Rama that the fourteen-year exile, decreed by Kaikeyi, began that day.  A joyful Sita, adorned with gifts from Dasaratha, then ascended the sun-like chariot.

movement, they return desolate. Dasaratha too
runs after the chariot alongwith Kausalya
and others, but being unable to walk
begins to totter and stops short
on the road yielding to the
remonstrances of his
wise ministers

__data__.text: Sumantra informed Rama that the fourteen-year exile period, decreed by Kaikeyi, began that day.  Sita, adorned with gifts from Dasaratha, boarded the chariot, which was radiant like the sun. Sumantra carefully arranged Sita's clothing, jewelry, and the brothers' weapons and tools in the chariot. Rama and Lakshmana then boarded the chariot.  Sumantra urged the horses forward, and the chariot sped away, leaving the citizens of Ayodhya heartbroken. The city, filled with the sounds of distressed animals and grieving people, followed Rama's chariot.  They begged Sumantra to slow down, wanting one last glimpse of Rama. The citizens lamented Kausalya's strength and Sita's devotion, praising Lakshmana's loyalty to his brother and his wise decision to accompany him. Overcome with grief, they continued to follow Rama, the beloved prince.

__data__.text:
As Rama's chariot departed, the citizens of Ayodhya mourned deeply. They praised Sita's unwavering devotion to her husband, comparing her to a shadow that never leaves its source or the sun's light that perpetually graces Mount Meru.  They commended Lakshmana's wisdom and loyalty in choosing to accompany his brother, recognizing the great fortune and spiritual merit in serving the kind-hearted Rama.  Overcome with emotion, they followed the chariot, tears streaming down their faces.

King Dasaratha, heartbroken and surrounded by his grieving wives, left the palace, determined to see his son one last time. The cries of the women filled the air, echoing the distress of elephants separated from their leader.  Dasaratha, once radiant, now appeared diminished, like the moon eclipsed by Rahu.  Meanwhile, Rama, resolute and composed, urged Sumantra to hasten their departure.  Torn between Rama's command to proceed and the pleas of the citizens to stop, Sumantra could only continue onward. The dust raised by the chariot wheels settled, dampened by the citizens' tears.  The people of Ayodhya, overwhelmed by sorrow, wept and fainted as they watched their beloved prince leave.  Tears flowed from the women's eyes like water droplets from disturbed lotus blossoms. Witnessing the collective grief of his city, Dasaratha collapsed like a felled tree.  A great cry arose from those following Rama as they saw the king's anguish.  Some called out to Rama, others to his mother, Kausalya, who was also following the chariot, her heart filled with sorrow. Rama, glancing back, saw his father's despair and his mother's grief.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, witnessing the collective grief of his people, collapsed like a felled tree.  A cry arose from the crowd following Rama’s chariot, some calling out to Rama, others to his mother Kausalya.  Rama, looking back, saw his father’s despair and his mother’s anguish.  Bound by his duty, he avoided their gaze and instructed Sumantra to hasten their departure. He noted his parents were on foot, though deserving of comfort and a chariot.  Like an elephant driven forward by goads, Rama felt unable to bear the distressing sight. Kausalya, like a cow whose calf is tethered, ran after Rama's chariot, weeping and calling out to Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana. Rama repeatedly looked back at his grieving mother, who followed as if in a trance.  Dasaratha cried out for the chariot to stop, while Rama urged Sumantra to proceed.  Torn, Sumantra received further instructions from Rama: ignore the king's pleas and continue, for prolonging this painful scene would be disastrous.  Sumantra obeyed, urging the horses onward.  The king’s men, unable to keep up with the chariot, returned to Dasaratha, having symbolically accompanied Rama in their minds.  The ministers advised the king against following any further, saying it was unwise to pursue someone you wished to see return.  Overwhelmed with grief and exhaustion, Dasaratha finally stopped, watching his son depart with a heavy heart, accompanied by his equally distressed wives.

__data__.text: Sumantra, the charioteer, followed Rama's command and urged the horses to greater speed, leaving behind the crowd that was struggling to keep up.  The king's men, unable to match the chariot's pace, returned to Dasaratha, having symbolically accompanied Rama in their hearts. The citizens, however, remained, their minds fixed on Rama. Upon their return, the ministers advised Dasaratha against pursuing Rama further, arguing that one should not follow someone they wish to see return.  Hearing this counsel, the distressed Dasaratha, his body perspiring and his face etched with sorrow, finally halted, his wives beside him, watching his son Rama disappear from view.

Canto XLI

The ladies of the royal gynaeceum bewail the exile of Sri Rama and others and
the citizens of Ayodhya too are reduced to a miserable plight

__data__.text: The women of Ayodhya cried out in lament as Rama departed.  They mourned the loss of their protector, a prince who was kind and compassionate to all, regardless of their station.  They questioned the king's decision, feeling abandoned and vulnerable.  The queens, heartbroken, wailed like cows separated from their calves.  Overwhelmed by the cries of his people, King Dasaratha was consumed by grief.  Normal life in Ayodhya ceased.  People neglected their duties and routines, and even nature seemed to reflect the city’s despair.  Omens appeared in the sky, and a dark cloud enveloped the city, symbolizing the sorrow that had befallen Ayodhya with Rama's exile.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: The celestial bodies dimmed, their light fading as if mirroring the city's despair.  A storm gathered, dark clouds swirling like a churning ocean, as Ayodhya trembled with the news of Rama's exile.  Darkness enveloped the city, both literally and figuratively.  The people of Ayodhya were plunged into grief, neglecting their daily lives and lamenting the king's decision.  They openly expressed their sorrow, tears streaming down their faces as they walked the streets.  The natural world seemed to reflect their despair: the breeze vanished, the moon appeared troubled, and the sun's warmth couldn't reach the grieving populace. Family bonds were strained as everyone focused solely on Rama's absence.  Rama's friends, particularly, were overwhelmed with sorrow, unable to find solace in sleep.  The city itself, deprived of its beloved prince, felt as unstable as the earth without its guardian deity, its cries of lament echoing through the streets.

Sri Rama having departed for the forest in an exceptionally swift-going
chariot, Dasaratha vainly seeks to cover the intervening distance on foot.

the Emperor feels doubly disconsolate and drops to the ground.

asks her not to touch his person. Kausalya then lifts him up
and persuades him to return. His personal attendants
take him to the latter's apartments and, seeing him
plunged in grief, Kausalya seats herself beside
him and begins to lament in various ways

__data__.text: King Dasaratha continued to watch the dust trail of Rama's departing chariot as long as it was visible.  As the dust disappeared, marking Rama's increasing distance, the King collapsed to the ground in despair.  Kausalya, his chief wife, rushed to his right side to help him up, while Kaikeyi approached his left.  Seeing Kaikeyi, Dasaratha, overcome with grief and anger, rebuked her, forbidding her from touching him and disowning her as his wife and kin. He declared that he renounced any connection to her or those who served her, blaming her selfish desires and lack of virtue for Rama's exile.  He even disavowed any future benefit from their marriage rituals and declared that any offerings Bharata might make in the afterlife on his behalf should not reach him. Kausalya then lifted the dust-covered king and escorted him back to the palace.  Dasaratha, tormented by the thought of Rama's exile, felt intense remorse, like someone who had committed a terrible act. He repeatedly looked back at the path Rama's chariot had taken, his appearance as diminished as a sun in eclipse.  Anguished, the king lamented the loss of his beloved son.  Upon learning that Rama had reached the city's outskirts, he cried out that while the hoofprints of the chariot horses were still visible, Rama himself was gone from sight.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Dasaratha, heartbroken and inconsolable, repeatedly looked back at the path Rama's chariot had taken, his face as pale as a sun in eclipse.  He lamented the loss of his beloved son, his grief intensifying as he realized Rama had reached the city's outskirts. He cried out in anguish, noting that while the hoofprints of the chariot horses were still visible, Rama himself was gone.  The king imagined Rama's current hardship, contrasting it with his former comfort: how the prince, once accustomed to soft cushions, sandal paste, and gentle breezes, would now be sleeping on the bare ground, under a tree, his head resting on a rough piece of wood or stone. He pictured Rama rising at dawn, covered in dust, sighing deeply. He envisioned forest dwellers seeing the exiled prince, the protector of the world, wandering as a forlorn figure.  Dasaratha also lamented Sita's plight, imagining her delicate feet injured by thorns as she walked through the forest, and her fear upon hearing the roars of wild animals.  Turning his anger towards Kaikeyi, he blamed her for his misery, wishing her a widow's life in the kingdom he now found unbearable without Rama.  Surrounded by his people, the grieving king returned to his palace, now as desolate as a pool emptied of its life.  He looked upon the deserted city, its empty streets and closed shops, a reflection of his own despair.  Entering the palace, which felt empty without Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana, he feebly requested his attendants to take him to Kausalya, Rama's mother, seeking solace in her presence.

__data__.text: Dasaratha, overcome with grief, entered the deserted city.  The normally bustling streets and marketplaces were empty, reflecting the king's despair.  He mourned for Rama, his thoughts consumed by his son's absence. The palace felt empty and lifeless without Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana.  He weakly asked to be taken to Kausalya, Rama's mother, desperately seeking comfort. Though in her presence, his mind remained agitated, the palace seeming as bleak as a starless, moonless night.  He cried out in anguish, lamenting Rama's departure and the uncertainty of his return. He expressed his sorrow for those who would not live to see Rama again, emphasizing the joy that awaited those who would witness his eventual homecoming. As the night deepened, a night that felt like the end of his own world, Dasaratha, lost in his grief, reached out to Kausalya. He could not see her, his vision still fixed on the path Rama had taken.  He asked her to touch him, a small gesture of comfort in his immense despair. Kausalya, witnessing her husband's profound sorrow, sat beside him and wept.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XLIII

The Lament

Canto XLIII

The Lament

__data__.text: Queen Kausalya, heartbroken by Rama's exile, laments to King Dasaratha. She condemns Kaikeyi's actions, comparing her to a venomous serpent, freed from its old skin and now free to inflict further pain. Kausalya expresses her anguish, stating that even a life of poverty in Ayodhya would be preferable to Rama's exile, or even servitude to Kaikeyi. She criticizes Dasaratha for unjustly banishing Rama, likening it to a sacred offering being given to demons. Kausalya paints a vivid picture of Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana entering the forest, and worries about their well-being, forced to endure hardships they've never known.  She yearns for the day Rama will return, envisioning Ayodhya's joyous celebration upon their arrival.  She imagines the city adorned with banners, the crowds rejoicing, and the princes being showered with flowers and fruits upon their return.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

The Lament

Canto XLIII

The Lament

__data__.text: Kausalya continues to lament Rama's exile. She wonders when Rama will return to Ayodhya, victorious, with Sita by his side.  She longs for the day the city will celebrate their return, showering the princes with flowers and fruits as they process through the streets.  Kausalya questions her past actions, believing she must have committed some terrible deed to deserve this suffering.  She likens herself to a cow separated from her calf, blaming Kaikeyi for this forced separation.  Overwhelmed with grief, she declares she cannot live without her son, Rama, and Lakshmana.  Her sorrow burns within her like the scorching summer sun.

Canto XLIV

Establishing the greatness of Sri Rama, Sumitra, who is a pastmaster in
eloquence, assuages Kausalya’s grief

__data__.text: Sumitra consoles the grieving Queen Kausalya, reminding her of Rama's virtues. She emphasizes Rama's nobility in upholding his father's word and adhering to dharma, the righteous path.  Sumitra highlights Lakshmana's unwavering devotion and Sita's willing companionship in Rama's exile. She extols Rama's widespread fame and inherent goodness, suggesting that nature itself will protect him. Sumitra further recounts Rama's strength, recalling his defeat of the demon Subahu and his mastery of celestial weapons. She expresses confidence in Rama's ability to thrive in the forest and ultimately regain his rightful throne, emphasizing his inherent qualities of splendor, valor, and benevolent strength as guarantees of his future success.  Sumitra concludes by declaring Rama's transcendent nature, likening him to the source and essence of light, rule, glory, and forbearance.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:

Establishing the greatness of Sri Rama, Sumitra, who is a pastmaster in
eloquence, assuages Kausalya’s grief

Canto XLIV

Establishing the greatness of Sri Rama, Sumitra, who is a pastmaster in
eloquence, assuages Kausalya’s grief

__data__.text: Sumitra continues her consoling words to Kausalya, emphasizing Rama's strength and invincibility. She recounts Viswamitra's bestowal of celestial weapons upon Rama and his subsequent defeat of the demon Subahu.  Sumitra asserts that Rama will remain undeterred in the forest, as comfortable as he would be in his own palace. She questions how the earth could disobey Rama, whose arrows easily vanquish his foes.  Sumitra further emphasizes Rama's inherent splendor, valor, and benevolent strength, assuring Kausalya that these qualities guarantee his return to the throne. She proclaims Rama's transcendent nature, comparing him to fundamental forces like the sun and fire, and the embodiment of rule, glory, and forbearance.  Sumitra questions what challenges could possibly hinder a being of such divine stature, whether in the forest or in the city. She affirms that Rama will soon be rightfully restored to his throne, accompanied by Sita and the blessings of fortune. Sumitra describes the widespread grief in Ayodhya upon Rama's departure, noting Sita's unwavering devotion in accompanying him, as well as Lakshmana's armed vigilance.  She reiterates her confidence in Rama's triumphant return, urging Kausalya to abandon her grief and despair. Sumitra paints a vivid picture of Rama's eventual return, bowing before his mother, prompting tears of joy. Finally, she implores Kausalya to relinquish her sorrow, reminding her of Rama's unwavering righteousness and suggesting that her grief should be directed towards comforting others who are also suffering from Rama's absence.  She assures Kausalya that she will soon see her son return, surrounded by his companions, and her joy will be abundant.

__data__.text: Sumitra consoles Kausalya, assuring her that Rama will return safe and sound, accompanied by Sita and Lakshmana.  She gently chides Kausalya for her excessive grief, reminding her that others also mourn Rama's absence and need her comfort.  Sumitra emphasizes Rama's unwavering righteousness and virtuous nature, stating that no one is more committed to the right path.  She paints a vivid picture of Rama's homecoming: he will greet his mother respectfully, prompting tears of joy, and will tenderly touch her feet with his hands.  Sumitra's eloquent words of comfort finally bring her to a pause.  Upon hearing Sumitra's consoling speech, Kausalya's grief dissipates like a light autumn cloud.

__data__.text: The people of Ayodhya, deeply devoted to Rama, followed him into exile.  Even when King Dasharatha returned to the city, bound by custom to only accompany Rama a short distance, the citizens refused to leave Rama's side. Rama, renowned for his virtues, was beloved by the people of Ayodhya like the full moon. Though the people pleaded with him, Rama, honoring his father's promise, continued towards the forest.  Looking at them with affection, Rama addressed the citizens, urging them to transfer their love and respect to Bharata. He assured them that Bharata, though young, possessed wisdom and noble qualities and would rule wisely, allaying their fears. Rama emphasized Bharata's suitability as a leader and urged the citizens to obey him and their father, King Dasharatha, so that the king wouldn't grieve during Rama's exile.  The more Rama adhered to his father's wishes, the stronger the people's desire for him as their king became. Rama and Lakshmana, through their virtues, drew the tearful citizens with them as if bound by cords. Elderly Brahmanas, unable to keep pace with Rama's chariot, called out, urging the horses to turn back and be loyal to their master by not taking him further against the people's wishes. They reminded the horses that all creatures, especially those with ears, heard their pleas and should therefore heed them.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: The Brahmanas, especially the elders wise in knowledge, age, and ascetic practice, pleaded with Rama's horses to stop, reminding them of their loyalty to their master and highlighting the people's distress. They appealed to all creatures, particularly those with ears, to hear their plea and persuade Rama to return. They extolled Rama's virtues and argued that such a noble person deserved to be taken back to the city, not further into the forest. Seeing their anguish, Rama quickly descended from his chariot and began walking with Sita and Lakshmana. This gesture allowed the elderly Brahmanas to keep pace. Rama, known for his compassion, couldn't bear to ride while they struggled on foot.  Distressed by Rama's continued departure, the Brahmanas declared their unwavering devotion and intention to follow him, pointing out that even the sacred fires were being carried along. They offered their ceremonial canopies as shade from the sun and expressed their shift in focus from Vedic studies to accompanying him. Assuring Rama of their wives' safety at home, the Brahmanas questioned who would uphold righteousness if Rama, a paragon of virtue, disregarded their pleas.  Lowering their dust-covered, white-haired heads, they implored him to return.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XLV

When the citizens that followed Sri Rama in his journey to the forest
refuse to return even when pleaded by Sri Rama in many ways,
unable as they were to bear separation from him, Sri Rama, with

Sita and Laksmana, gets down from his chariot and begins to
walk. The citizens try to deflect him from his course and
persuade him to return, but in vain. At the close of
the day they all reach the bank of the Tamasa

Canto XLV

When the citizens that followed Sri Rama in his journey to the forest
refuse to return even when pleaded by Sri Rama in many ways,
unable as they were to bear separation from him, Sri Rama, with

Sita and Laksmana, gets down from his chariot and begins to
walk. The citizens try to deflect him from his course and
persuade him to return, but in vain. At the close of
the day they all reach the bank of the Tamasa

__data__.text: The Brahmanas, deeply attached to Rama, declared their unwavering resolve to follow him into exile.  Their minds, once focused on Vedic scriptures, were now fixed on accompanying him.  They assured Rama that their wives were safe and their knowledge of the Vedas secure within their memories.  They questioned how righteousness could prevail if Rama, its embodiment, ignored their pleas.  With heads bowed low, their white hair dusted from prostration, they begged him to return.  They reminded him of the unfinished sacrifices that depended on his presence.  Even the inanimate and animate world, they asserted, yearned for his return, symbolized by the rustling leaves of trees that seemed to cry out and the motionless, hungry birds perched on branches. As this heartfelt plea continued, they reached the Tamasa River, its presence seemingly a further attempt to impede Rama's journey.  Meanwhile, Sumantra, Rama's charioteer, unyoked the weary horses, allowing them to drink, bathe, and graze near the riverbank.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XLVI

Having reached the bank of the Tamasa and thinking of the plight of the
people of Ayodhya, Sri Rama lays himself down on a bed of leaves
bewailing the lot of his parents and feeling reassured by the thought
of Bharata’s noble qualities; while Laksmana opens his dialogue
with Sumantra on the divine excellences of Sri Rama. Waking
up in the meantime, Sri Rama urges the charioteer-minister to
drive the chariot in such a way as to put the citizens, that
had accompanied them, off the scent and lead them to
think that the chariot had turned back towards
Ayodhya instead of proceeding towards the forest.

He then mounts the chariot alongwith Sita and
Laksmana and presses on to the forest

__data__.text: On the banks of the Tamasa river, Rama, looking at Sita, addressed Lakshmana.  He reminded Lakshmana that this was their first night of exile and urged him not to worry about those left behind in Ayodhya.  Rama observed the quiet forest, noting the animals and birds seeking shelter, and lamented that the city of Ayodhya, his father's capital, would be mourning their departure. He acknowledged the people's affection for himself, Lakshmana, Bharata, and Shatrughna. Rama expressed concern for his parents, fearing their grief might overwhelm them. However, he took comfort in Bharata's kind nature, believing he would console their parents. Rama then thanked Lakshmana for accompanying him, stating it relieved him of the burden of seeking aid to protect Sita. He declared his intention to fast that night, subsisting only on water despite the availability of wild fruits.  Rama then instructed Sumantra to attend to the horses. After Sumantra had seen to the horses’ needs, he returned to Rama.  As night fell, Sumantra and Lakshmana prepared a bed of leaves for Rama. Rama and Sita then retired for the night on the riverbank. While Rama slept, Lakshmana spoke with Sumantra about Rama's many virtues.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: After Sumantra attended to the horses, he returned to Rama. Sumantra and Lakshmana then prepared a bed of leaves for Rama and Sita on the banks of the Tamasa river.  Rama and Sita rested there for the night.  As Rama slept, Lakshmana discussed Rama's virtues with Sumantra.  They stayed awake all night, and as the sun rose, Rama awoke to find the citizens of Ayodhya still camped nearby. He observed their devotion and instructed Lakshmana to prepare for their departure quickly while the citizens slept, choosing a safe and secluded route to minimize further hardship for the devoted people of Ayodhya. Lakshmana agreed, and Rama instructed Sumantra to prepare the chariot. Sumantra promptly readied the chariot and informed Rama, urging him to board with Sita and Lakshmana.

__data__.text: Lakshmana agreed with Rama's suggestion to depart quickly.  Rama then instructed Sumantra to prepare the chariot for their journey into the forest.  Sumantra quickly prepared the chariot and informed Rama that it was ready.  Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana boarded the chariot, and they swiftly crossed the Tamasa River.  After crossing the river, they traveled on a smooth, safe road.  To mislead the citizens of Ayodhya who had followed them, Rama instructed Sumantra to drive the chariot north for a short distance and then return discreetly, creating the impression that they were heading back to Ayodhya.  Sumantra followed Rama's instructions and reported back. Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana then settled comfortably in the chariot, and Sumantra drove them towards a forest suitable for ascetic life, initially heading north due to favorable omens.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XLVII

The citizens that had followed Sri Rama in his journey to the forest woke
to find Sri Rama and his party gone and begin to reproach
themselves. Overcome with grief they hunt up the
tracks of his chariot; but unable to find them,
they helplessly return to Ayodhya
in utter despondency

Canto XLVII

The citizens that had followed Sri Rama in his journey to the forest woke
to find Sri Rama and his party gone and begin to reproach
themselves. Overcome with grief they hunt up the
tracks of his chariot; but unable to find them,
they helplessly return to Ayodhya
in utter despondency

__data__.text: The citizens who had followed Rama awoke at dawn to find him gone. Grief-stricken and tearful, they searched for him but could not even catch a glimpse. Dejected and bewildered, they lamented, "Woe to the sleep that kept us from noticing Rama's departure! How could he, whose actions are always purposeful, abandon us, his devoted followers, for a life of asceticism in the forest? He was like a father to us! Should we end our lives here or journey north to die?  What good is life without him?  We could light a funeral pyre and perish together. How can we face those in Ayodhya and tell them that we let Rama, the kind and generous, go?  The city will be devastated.  Having accompanied him, how can we return without him?"  They searched for his chariot tracks for a while, but the trail disappeared.  Disheartened and feeling abandoned by fate, they retraced their steps back to Ayodhya, where the citizens were already in despair.

__data__.text: The citizens, heartbroken like cows who have lost their calves, continued to lament Rama's departure. Following the chariot tracks for a short distance, they were dismayed when the trail vanished, realizing Rama had taken a different route back.  Discouraged and feeling abandoned by fate, they questioned what they should do, lamenting their misfortune.  With heavy hearts, they returned to Ayodhya, finding the city and its people already gripped by despair.  The city, without Rama, appeared desolate, like a river stripped of its serpents or the sky without the moon, bringing the citizens no joy.  They entered their opulent homes, yet their sorrow was so profound they could barely recognize their own families, their happiness completely gone.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: The citizens of Ayodhya, heartbroken by Rama's departure, returned home grieving.  Their sorrow was palpable, their tears flowed freely.  Upon reaching their homes, they were met by their families, and the collective weeping intensified.  Joy vanished from the city. Merchants ceased their trade, households stopped cooking, and even the return of lost wealth or the birth of a son brought no solace.  The women of Ayodhya, distraught at Rama's absence, reproached their husbands, questioning the value of home, family, and possessions without him.  They praised Lakshmana for his unwavering loyalty in accompanying Rama and Sita.  They envied the rivers, ponds, and lakes that Rama would grace with his presence, the forests and mountains that would adorn his path.  Nature itself, they believed, would honor Rama, offering him flowers, fruits, and clear waters.  They declared that where Rama resided, fear and humiliation could not exist.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: The women of Ayodhya continued lamenting Rama's exile.  They envisioned the beautiful flora and refreshing waters that would greet Rama on his journey, believing that nature itself would honor him.  They affirmed that wherever Rama resided, fear and disgrace could not exist.  Hope briefly flickered as they expressed a desire to follow Rama, seeking the protection and solace of his presence.  Their grief then turned to anger directed at Kaikeyi. They declared their unwillingness to live under her rule, fearing her unjust and destructive influence on the kingdom. They predicted the demise of the king and the ruin of the kingdom in Rama's absence, urging their husbands to choose death, exile with Rama, or flight to a place untouched by Kaikeyi's name, rather than endure her reign.

__data__.text: The women of Ayodhya continued to mourn Rama's exile. They questioned how anyone could find happiness under the rule of Kaikeyi, who orchestrated Rama's banishment. They lamented that the kingdom was rudderless and predicted its imminent ruin due to Kaikeyi's actions.  In their despair, they contemplated drastic measures – poison, following Rama into exile, or fleeing to a place untouched by Kaikeyi's influence.  They felt unjustly bound to Bharata's rule, comparing themselves to livestock awaiting slaughter.  Amidst their grief, they described Rama's noble qualities – his strength, his truthfulness, his gentle nature, and his striking appearance.  Their sorrow intensified as night fell, and Ayodhya, once vibrant, became shrouded in darkness and silence.  The cessation of daily activities and the absence of joy transformed the city into a desolate wasteland, mirroring the emptiness in their hearts.  Overwhelmed by grief, they wept and fainted, their love for Rama surpassing even their love for their own sons. The once joyous city became a bleak shadow of its former self, devoid of music, celebration, and prosperity.

__data__.text: The women of Ayodhya, heartbroken over Rama's exile, grieved as if their own sons or brothers had been banished.  Their sorrow was so profound that Rama was dearer to them than their own children.  The city of Ayodhya, once vibrant with music, dance, and celebration, now fell silent.  Its joy vanished, and its bustling trade ceased.  The once-thriving city became a desolate wasteland, like an ocean drained of its water.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto XLIX
Having covered a long distance in the meantime, Sri Rama finishes his
morning bath and devotions and, having crossed the Vedasruti,

Gomati and Syandika rivers, presses forward
talking with Sumantra

Canto XLIX
Having covered a long distance in the meantime, Sri Rama finishes his
morning bath and devotions and, having crossed the Vedasruti,

Gomati and Syandika rivers, presses forward
talking with Sumantra

__data__.text: Rama, obeying his father's order, journeyed a long distance through the night.  As he traveled, the pleasant night passed, and after morning prayers, he continued onward, passing through numerous regions.  He observed well-cultivated villages and blossoming forests as he rode, seemingly at a slow pace but actually covering ground quickly, thanks to his excellent horses.  He overheard the conversations of villagers, who lamented Dasaratha's decision, influenced by desire, to exile Rama. They criticized Kaikeyi for her harshness and improper actions in orchestrating Rama's banishment.  They expressed concern for Sita, accustomed to comfort, now facing hardship, and questioned Dasaratha's lack of affection in abandoning his blameless son. Rama continued his journey, passing out of Kosala, crossing the Vedasruti river, and then the Gomati, a river with cool waters and cows on its banks.  He also crossed the Syandika river, filled with the sounds of peacocks and swans.  He pointed out to Sita the prosperous land of Kosala, gifted long ago by Manu to Ikshvaku, and discussed with his charioteer Sumantra his longing to return to Ayodhya and hunt in the forests with his parents.

__data__.text: Rama crossed the Gomati River and then the Syandika, a river lively with the sounds of peacocks and swans.  He showed Sita the prosperous land of Kosala, a gift from the ancient king Manu to his son Ikshvaku.  Speaking to his charioteer, Sumantra, Rama expressed his longing to return to Ayodhya and hunt in the forests along the Sarayu River with his parents.  While acknowledging that hunting is a favored pastime of royal sages, Rama insisted that he did not yearn for it excessively. He continued his journey, conversing with Sumantra.

__data__.text: Rama, bidding farewell to Ayodhya, addressed the city, expressing his gratitude to its protectors and promising to return after his exile.  He then tearfully addressed the people who had accompanied him, requesting them to return to their homes.  The people, grieving, paid their respects and watched as Rama departed.  He journeyed through the prosperous lands of Kosala, passing through villages filled with resources, charitable people, and places of worship. He then entered a territory of contented people and abundant livestock, marked by the sounds of Vedic chants.  Continuing his journey, Rama reached the banks of the sacred river Ganga, a threefold river frequented by sages and celestial beings, adorned with hermitages and graced by the presence of various divine beings. Its pools, overflowing with water, served as a haven for celestial nymphs and its banks as a playground for gods.

__data__.text: Rama reached the divine Ganga river.  The threefold river flowed through the heavens, earth, and the underworld. Its cool, clear waters were frequented by sages.  The riverbanks, adorned with hermitages, were visited by celestial nymphs and various divine beings. The river flowed through celestial gardens and contained divine lotuses.  Its waters, sometimes calm, sometimes turbulent, created a variety of sounds.  The river, teeming with life, was graced by the presence of swans, cranes, and other birds. Its banks were lined with trees and flowers.  Purified by the touch of Vishnu's feet, the river cleansed those who bathed in or drank its waters.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Rama, accompanied by Sita and Lakshmana, arrived at the Ganga river. The river was described as pure, its waters capable of washing away sins. Its banks were lively with various creatures, from Indra's elephants to common birds.  Rama, noticing a large Ingudi tree, decided to rest there for the night.  He, Sita, and Lakshmana disembarked from the chariot. Sumantra unyoked the horses.  Guha, the local chieftain and a friend of Rama, arrived to greet them. He offered Rama his kingdom and provisions for the night, expressing his honor at having such a distinguished guest.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Guha, deeply saddened by Rama's ascetic attire, embraced him and offered his kingdom of Sragaverapura, along with various foods and provisions. He expressed his honor at hosting such an esteemed guest. Rama, in turn, acknowledged Guha's affection and hospitality, but declined the offerings, explaining his commitment to a life of asceticism, sustained by fruits, roots, and the bark of trees. He requested only fodder for his horses, emphasizing their importance as his father's beloved animals.  Guha immediately ordered his men to provide the horses with proper feed and drink. Rama, after performing evening rituals, consumed only water, observing a fast. Lakshmana washed Rama and Sita's feet before retiring to a nearby tree.  Guha, along with Sumantra, kept watch over the sleeping Rama, discussing his virtues.  Thus, the night passed for Rama, who, accustomed to comfort, now lay on the bare ground.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Rama, clad in barkcloth, performed his evening rituals facing west and broke his fast with only water brought by Lakshmana, a gesture of reverence for the sacred Ganga.  After washing Rama and Sita's feet, Lakshmana retired to a nearby tree.  Guha, along with the charioteer Sumantra, kept watch over the sleeping Rama, discussing his noble qualities.  Thus, the night passed slowly for Rama, who, accustomed to a life of ease and comfort, was now resting on the bare earth.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto LI

Expressing his readiness to guard the Crown prince and his consort,
keeping awake the whole night, Guha importunes Laksmana to repose.
Reminding Guha of Sri Rama’s greatness, Laksmana, however, tells
him that even though the duty of guarding his princely brother and
his consort could as well be entrusted to Guha, he felt that he
did not deserve to lie down in the presence of his elder
brother and sister-in-law and preferred to remain awake.
Expressing grief for his royal father and loving mothers
he therefore spends the night talking with Guha

Canto LI

Expressing his readiness to guard the Crown prince and his consort,
keeping awake the whole night, Guha importunes Laksmana to repose.
Reminding Guha of Sri Rama’s greatness, Laksmana, however, tells
him that even though the duty of guarding his princely brother and
his consort could as well be entrusted to Guha, he felt that he
did not deserve to lie down in the presence of his elder
brother and sister-in-law and preferred to remain awake.
Expressing grief for his royal father and loving mothers
he therefore spends the night talking with Guha

__data__.text: Guha, witnessing Rama and Sita sleeping on the ground, offered Lakshmana a comfortable bed, assuring him of his loyalty and vigilance.  He declared his deep affection for Rama and pledged to protect him and Sita with his kinsmen.  Guha emphasized his familiarity with the forest and his ability to defend them against any threat.  Lakshmana declined the offer, explaining his inability to rest while his brother and sister-in-law slept on the ground.  He lamented their exile and expressed his concern for his father, King Dasaratha, and mothers, fearing they might not survive the grief. He pictured Ayodhya, once filled with cries of anguish, now fallen silent with exhaustion. He worried most about Queen Kausalya, believing his own mother, Sumitra, might find solace in the hope of seeing her other son, Shatrughna.

__data__.text: Lakshmana, deeply troubled, reflected on the dire consequences of Rama's exile. He feared that his father, King Dasaratha, weakened by grief, would not survive the night.  He imagined the lamentations of the women in the palace, now silenced by exhaustion. Lakshmana worried about Queen Kausalya, Rama's mother, and his own mother, Sumitra, though he believed Sumitra might find some comfort in the hope of seeing her other son, Shatrughna. He envisioned Ayodhya, once joyful and prosperous, now desolate and grieving.  He questioned how his father could endure the absence of his eldest son. Lakshmana predicted that if Dasaratha perished, Kausalya and then Sumitra would follow. He lamented that his father would die without seeing Rama ascend the throne.  He pictured the funeral rites, performed by those fortunate enough to survive.  Lakshmana then shifted to a more hopeful vision of Ayodhya, thriving and bustling with activity, should his father live. He expressed his wish that he and Rama would safely return from exile to this prosperous city.  Overwhelmed by grief and worry, Lakshmana continued to lament throughout the night. Witnessing Lakshmana's deep sorrow, Guha, too, wept.

__data__.text: Sri Rama, at daybreak, addressed Lakshmana, noting the sunrise and the sounds of cuckoos and peacocks, and proposed they cross the Ganga river. Lakshmana informed Guha, who promptly ordered a boat.  A minister quickly brought a suitable vessel to the riverbank and informed Guha. Guha, with folded hands, offered the boat to Rama, inquiring if he could offer further assistance.  Rama accepted, requesting everything be loaded quickly.  Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita, armed and armored, then proceeded towards the river.  The charioteer, Sumantra, asked Rama for further instructions.  Rama, touching Sumantra's hand, instructed him to return to Ayodhya and remain composed, explaining that they would continue their journey into the Dandaka forest on foot. Sumantra, distressed at being dismissed, made a final plea to Rama.

__data__.text: Sumantra, the charioteer, overcome with grief, questioned Rama about his own future now that Rama was leaving.  Rama instructed Sumantra to return swiftly to Ayodhya and remain composed. He reiterated his intent to journey to Dandaka forest on foot.  Sumantra, distraught, lamented Rama's exile, comparing it to the life of a common man. He questioned the value of piety and learning if even Rama could face such adversity.  He declared that despite the exile, Rama, accompanied by Sita and Lakshmana, would ultimately achieve glory. Sumantra then expressed his own despair at being left behind, fearing the influence of Kaikeyi.  Overwhelmed, Sumantra wept. Once composed, Rama consoled Sumantra, urging him to comfort Dasaratha and prevent him from grieving. He emphasized Sumantra’s importance as a friend to the Ikshvaku dynasty and instructed him to obey Dasaratha's commands, particularly those related to Kaikeyi, explaining that a king's purpose is to ensure his will is carried out.  Rama's final instruction was for Sumantra to respectfully convey a message to Dasaratha upon his return.

__data__.text: Rama continued to instruct Sumantra, emphasizing the importance of obeying Dasaratha, particularly in fulfilling Kaikeyi’s wishes. He explained that kings rule to ensure their commands are followed.  Rama urged Sumantra to prevent Dasaratha from succumbing to grief.  He then dictated a message for Sumantra to convey: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita were not sorrowful about their exile.  They would return after fourteen years. Sumantra was to offer greetings to Kausalya and the other queens on their behalf. Rama also instructed Sumantra to tell Dasaratha to quickly recall Bharata and install him as the heir. He reassured Sumantra that once Bharata was installed, his grief would lessen.  Furthermore, Rama advised that Bharata should be told to treat all his mothers with respect, especially Kausalya. By accepting the role offered by their father, Bharata would find happiness and prosperity. Sumantra, moved by Rama’s words, responded that his boldness in speaking was motivated by affection and loyalty.  He expressed his anguish at returning to Ayodhya without Rama.

__data__.text: Sumantra, deeply saddened by Rama's exile, expressed his unwavering devotion and questioned how he could return to Ayodhya without him. He predicted the city's despair at seeing the empty chariot, comparing it to an army seeing its leader's chariot without him.  He worried about the people grieving Rama's absence. Sumantra recalled the city's distress at Rama's departure and foresaw an even greater lament upon his return alone. He wrestled with the dilemma of telling Kausalya a comforting lie versus the painful truth. Sumantra declared his inability to return without Rama, his horses refusing to carry anyone else, and requested permission to join Rama's exile. He threatened self-immolation if abandoned and vowed to protect Rama during his austerities using the chariot. He expressed his hope to find solace in serving Rama in the forest.

__data__.text: Sumantra, overwhelmed with grief, pleaded with Rama to allow him to join his exile. He declared his inability to return to Ayodhya without him, likening the city to being inaccessible as heaven to a sinner.  Sumantra vowed to serve Rama, protect him during his austerities, and even offered to use the chariot for defense against wild creatures. He expressed hope that serving Rama in the forest would bring him solace, and reminded Rama of his fondness for his servants. Sumantra further argued that the fourteen years of exile would feel like moments in Rama's presence, but an eternity apart.  Rama, compassionate towards his devoted servant, explained his decision to send him back.  He reasoned that Sumantra's return would assure Kaikeyi of his departure, preventing her from suspecting King Dasaratha of deceit and ensuring she received the kingdom protected by Bharata.

__data__.text: Rama, expressing his deep affection for Sumantra, explained his reasons for sending him back to Ayodhya.  He wanted Kaikeyi to believe he had truly left for the forest, preventing her from wrongly accusing King Dasaratha of deception. Rama's priority was to ensure Kaikeyi received the kingdom, safeguarding it under Bharata's rule.  He then instructed Sumantra to return to Ayodhya and relay his messages as directed.  Turning to Guha, Rama explained his need to reside in a more secluded hermitage, fitting for his exile. He requested the sap of a banyan tree, which he used to mat his and Lakshmana's hair, adopting the ascetic appearance.  Rama then advised Guha to remain vigilant in protecting his kingdom, emphasizing the challenges of governance.  Finally, Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana departed, reaching the riverbank where they found a boat. Rama directed Lakshmana to secure the boat and help Sita aboard.

__data__.text: Upon reaching the riverbank, Rama advised Guha on the complexities of governance, stressing the importance of vigilance concerning the army, treasury, fortifications, and citizens.  After this counsel, Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana took their leave of Guha and proceeded towards the river.  Spotting a boat, Rama instructed Lakshmana to steady it and assist Sita in boarding, followed by himself. Lakshmana dutifully followed his brother's instructions, helping Sita into the boat before boarding himself.  Rama then embarked, after which Guha directed his kinsmen to row. Once aboard, Rama offered prayers. He and Sita respectfully sipped water from the Ganga and bowed to the river, a gesture Lakshmana also repeated. After bidding farewell to Guha and Sumantra, Rama signaled the boatmen to begin their journey.  Propelled by the oarsmen and guided by the helmsman, the boat swiftly traversed the river.  Upon reaching the middle of the Ganga, Sita offered a prayer, asking the river goddess to protect Rama as he fulfilled his exile.  She vowed to return and offer further worship upon their safe return after fourteen years.

__data__.text: Sita prayed to Ganga, promising to return and offer grand worship after their fourteen-year exile. She extolled the river's divine nature, its presence in all realms, and its connection to Brahma and the Ocean-king.  She vowed to offer cows, clothing, food, and other precious items to Brahmans upon Rama's safe return to Ayodhya. She further promised to worship all deities and sacred sites along the riverbanks. Sita concluded her prayer with a plea for Rama, Lakshmana, and herself to safely return from exile. Upon reaching the southern bank, Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita disembarked and continued their journey. Rama instructed Lakshmana to lead the way, with Sita following behind him, while Rama himself would protect them both from the rear. He emphasized the importance of their mutual protection, especially considering the hardships Sita would face entering the untamed forest.

__data__.text: Rama instructed Lakshmana to lead the way, followed by Sita, while he would protect them from behind. He acknowledged the difficulties ahead, particularly for Sita, who was accustomed to palace life and would now experience the hardships of the forest.  She was entering a wild, untamed area, devoid of human activity and comforts.  Lakshmana, Sita, and Rama proceeded in this formation.  Meanwhile, Sumantra, still watching from the northern bank, wept as they disappeared into the distance.  Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita then journeyed through the fertile Vatsa region, between the Ganga and Yamuna rivers.  The brothers hunted four large deer – a Varaha, Rishya, Prishata, and Maharuru – and gathered edible fruits before seeking a suitable tree for their night's rest.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto LIII

Apprehending trouble for Kausalya and others at the hands of Kaikeyi, Sri
Rama, who was seated at the foot of a banyan tree and was a past
master in the art of persuasion, persuades Laksmana to the best
of his ability to return to Ayodhya. Laksmana, however, pleads
inability to survive in his absence and does not budge an
inch from his resolution to stay with his eldest brother.

Sri Rama, therefore, yields and sets his seal to

his continuing with him during his exile

__data__.text: Rama, having arrived at the foot of a tree and performed evening prayers, addressed Lakshmana.  He observed that this was their first night outside their homeland, away from Sumantra, urging Lakshmana not to grieve.  He emphasized the need for vigilance in the coming nights for Sita's safety and well-being.  Rama suggested they make the best of the situation, finding materials to create makeshift beds.  He lamented the king's likely discomfort and contrasted it with Kaikeyi's presumed satisfaction at achieving her aims.  Rama worried about his father's vulnerability to Kaikeyi's influence, especially given his age and isolation. He reflected on the power of desire, questioning why anyone would abandon a loyal son for a woman, as his father had seemingly done.  He mused on Bharata's fortunate position, poised to rule Kosala unopposed.  Rama concluded that Kaikeyi's motives were threefold: to cause Dasaratha's demise, orchestrate Rama's exile, and secure the throne for Bharata. He expressed concern that Kaikeyi's arrogance might lead her to persecute Kausalya and Sumitra.

Canto LIII

Apprehending trouble for Kausalya and others at the hands of Kaikeyi, Sri
Rama, who was seated at the foot of a banyan tree and was a past
master in the art of persuasion, persuades Laksmana to the best
of his ability to return to Ayodhya. Laksmana, however, pleads
inability to survive in his absence and does not budge an
inch from his resolution to stay with his eldest brother.

Sri Rama, therefore, yields and sets his seal to

his continuing with him during his exile

__data__.text: Rama, lamenting his exile, considers the implications for his family. He believes Bharata will enjoy the full benefits of the kingdom now that Dasaratha is old and Rama is in the forest.  He criticizes Dasaratha for prioritizing pleasure over virtue and wealth, predicting his downfall.  Rama suspects Kaikeyi orchestrated the entire situation: Dasaratha's decline, Rama's exile, and Bharata's ascension.  He fears Kaikeyi, empowered by her success, might now target Kausalya and Sumitra out of spite.  Concerned for his mother Sumitra's well-being, Rama urges Lakshmana to return to Ayodhya and protect her, as Kausalya will be vulnerable after Dasaratha's passing. Rama intends to go to the Dandaka forest with Sita alone.  He even entertains the dark thought that Kaikeyi might harm his mothers.  Rama blames himself for his mother's suffering, feeling he has failed her despite her years of devoted care. He laments being a source of sorrow to his mother, comparing himself unfavorably to her pet myna bird, which displays fierce loyalty.  He feels useless in the face of his mother's grief, describing her as heartbroken and desolate. While Rama claims he could conquer Ayodhya and the entire world with his prowess, he refrains from violence out of fear of sin and its consequences in the afterlife.  He chooses to honor his father's commitment to Kaikeyi's boons instead.  Overwhelmed by these thoughts, Rama weeps through the night in the desolate forest.

__data__.text: Rama, deeply saddened by his exile, laments his mother Kausalya's grief, picturing her overwhelmed with sorrow.  He asserts his capability to conquer Ayodhya and the world but chooses to refrain from violence due to fear of sin and the repercussions in the afterlife.  Instead, he accepts his exile to honor his father's promise.  Rama sits through the night, weeping in the lonely forest.  Lakshmana consoles the grieving Rama, comparing Ayodhya without him to a moonless night.  Lakshmana declares his inability to live without Rama, expressing his unwillingness to return to Ayodhya even to see his own family.  Finding a prepared bed under a banyan tree, Rama and Sita rest. Moved by Lakshmana's unwavering loyalty and devotion, Rama agrees for Lakshmana to accompany him and Sita on their exile.  From that point on, the two brothers, strong and fearless, resided in the forest without any fear, like lions on a mountain peak.

__data__.text: Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana, after spending a night under a large tree, continued their journey towards the confluence of the Ganga and Yamuna rivers at dawn.  They traveled through a vast and beautiful forest, observing various landscapes. As the day ended, Rama pointed out the fragrant smoke rising near Prayaga, indicating the presence of Sage Bharadwaja's hermitage. The sound of the converging rivers confirmed their proximity.  Signs of human activity, such as chopped wood and cut trees, were visible within the hermitage.  The two brothers approached the sage's dwelling as the sun set.  Upon reaching the hermitage, Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita respectfully waited at a distance.  They then entered the sage’s hut and greeted the revered Bharadwaja, who was surrounded by his disciples. Rama introduced himself, Sita, and Lakshmana, explaining they were exiled to the forest and Lakshmana chose to accompany them.

__data__.text: Upon receiving permission, Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana entered Sage Bharadwaja's hut.  They found the wise sage, surrounded by his disciples, having just completed his fire offering.  Rama introduced himself and his family, explaining their exile and Lakshmana's voluntary accompaniment. He informed the sage of their intention to live a virtuous life in the forest, sustaining themselves on roots and fruits.  Bharadwaja welcomed them warmly, offering them refreshments and arranging for their comfort.  After they were settled, the sage addressed Rama, acknowledging his awareness of Rama’s unjust exile. He suggested that they stay at the hermitage, located in a peaceful and beautiful setting at the confluence of two great rivers. However, Rama respectfully declined, expressing concern that their presence so close to Ayodhya might attract unwanted visitors, disturbing the peace of the hermitage.  He requested the sage to suggest a more secluded location where Sita could live comfortably.

__data__.text: Rama, concerned about the proximity of the hermitage to Ayodhya, explained to Sage Bharadwaja that his presence might attract unwanted attention from citizens and disrupt the tranquility of the ashram.  He expressed his desire for a more secluded dwelling where Sita could reside in peace and comfort.  In response, Bharadwaja suggested Citrakūṭa, a sacred mountain sixty miles away, inhabited by sages and renowned for its beauty.  He described it as a place frequented by monkeys and bears, resembling the Gandhamādana mountain.  Bharadwaja extolled the virtues of Citrakūṭa, claiming that merely gazing upon its peaks inspired virtuous thoughts and discouraged sin. He recounted how many ascetics had attained enlightenment there after years of dedicated austerities.  As an alternative, Bharadwaja offered Rama the option of remaining at his hermitage for the duration of their exile.  The sage then extended his hospitality to Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana, providing them with all they needed. They spent the night engaged in insightful conversations.  The following morning, after a restful night, Rama requested permission from Bharadwaja to depart for their chosen destination. The sage graciously granted his leave, advising them to journey to Citrakūṭa, a place abundant in honey, roots, and fruits.

__data__.text: Rama, having spent the night at Bharadwaja's hermitage, respectfully requested the sage's permission to depart for their designated place of exile. Bharadwaja granted his leave, directing them towards Citrakūṭa, a mountain abundant in fruits, roots, and honey.  He described Citrakūṭa as an ideal dwelling place, a sacred and beautiful mountain teeming with diverse trees and inhabited by Kinnaras and Nagas. The calls of peacocks and the presence of elephants added to its charm.  Bharadwaja assured Rama that the mountain's natural beauty, including rivers, waterfalls, mountain peaks, caves, and streams, would bring him and Sita joy during their wanderings in the surrounding forests, which were also home to herds of deer and elephants.  He encouraged them to settle on Citrakūṭa, where the pleasant songs of birds would further enhance their experience.

proceed. Accompanied by Sita, Sri Rama and Laksmana

__data__.text: After spending the night at Sage Bharadwaja's hermitage, Rama and Lakshmana bid farewell to the sage and prepared to journey towards Citrakūṭa.  The sage blessed them for a safe journey, offering guidance on their route. He advised them to follow the Yamuna River westward, then find a frequented crossing point where they could build a raft.  He further instructed them to locate a large banyan tree where Sita could offer prayers for their safe return to Ayodhya.  Continuing, they should travel two miles to Nilavana, a pleasant path free of forest fires, which would lead them to Citrakūṭa.  Rama expressed gratitude for the sage's kindness, acknowledging their past good deeds.  Following the sage's directions, Rama and Lakshmana, with Sita leading the way, proceeded towards the Yamuna. Upon reaching the swiftly flowing river, they contemplated how to cross. Resourcefully, the brothers constructed a large raft using wooden logs, dry bamboos, and fragrant Usira roots. Lakshmana thoughtfully fashioned a comfortable seat for Sita using cane shoots and rose-apple twigs.

__data__.text: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita reached the Yamuna River.  Concerned about the swift current, they built a sturdy raft from logs, bamboo, and fragrant roots. Lakshmana fashioned a comfortable seat for Sita on the raft.  Rama carefully helped Sita, along with her belongings, onto the raft.  The brothers then began to row across the river.  Midstream, Sita prayed to the river deity for a safe crossing and for the successful completion of Rama's exile.  She vowed to offer a grand sacrifice upon their return to Ayodhya.  Reaching the southern bank, they disembarked and left the raft. They continued to a nearby banyan tree, where Sita prayed for the fulfillment of Rama's vow and their safe return to see their mothers, Kausalya and Sumitra.  Observing Sita's prayer, Rama instructed Lakshmana to escort Sita ahead while he followed, armed, behind them.

__data__.text: Upon reaching the Yamuna's bank, Sita circled a banyan tree, praying for the successful completion of Rama's exile and their safe return to Ayodhya to see their mothers, Kausalya and Sumitra.  Observing Sita's devotion, Rama instructed Lakshmana to escort her while he followed, armed, close behind.  Rama further instructed Lakshmana to provide Sita with any fruits or flowers she desired.  Sita, captivated by the unfamiliar flora, inquired about the various trees, shrubs, and blossoming creepers.  Lakshmana, eager to fulfill her requests, gathered an array of beautiful flowering branches. Sita also found great joy in observing the river, its sands and waters vibrant, its air filled with the calls of swans and cranes.  Journeying a short distance further, Rama and Lakshmana hunted permissible game in the forest along the Yamuna's banks.  They found a suitable resting place amidst a lively forest teeming with peacocks, monkeys, and elephants, near the river's edge.

__data__.text: As the night passed, Rama awoke Laksmana, pointing out the sweet songs of the forest birds and urging them to continue their journey to Citrakuta.  Laksmana quickly roused himself, and after bathing in the Yamuna, they proceeded on their path.  Along the way, Rama pointed out to Sita the beauty of the flowering trees, the abundance of fruit, and the plentiful honeycombs.  He noted the calls of the birds and the impressive peaks of Citrakuta mountain, suggesting they could live there comfortably.  Upon reaching the mountain, Rama remarked to Laksmana on its beauty, the plentiful food and water, and its suitability as a dwelling place, especially given the presence of other ascetics. He proposed they make their home there.

__data__.text: Upon reaching the desirable Citrakuta mountain, Rama, Sita, and Laksmana found it a beautiful and resourceful place, teeming with birds, fruits, roots, and fresh water.  Rama declared it a suitable dwelling place, particularly noting the presence of other ascetics.  They then entered the hermitage of the sage Valmiki, who welcomed them warmly.  After proper introductions, Rama instructed Laksmana to build a hut of strong wood. Laksmana promptly obeyed, constructing a well-made and attractive dwelling.  Rama then directed Laksmana to retrieve a Gajakanda bulb to perform a ritual for consecrating the new hut, emphasizing the importance of observing scriptural procedures.  Laksmana retrieved the bulb and, following Rama's further instructions, roasted it in the fire as an offering.

__data__.text: Having received Rama's instructions, Laksmana prepared the Gajakanda bulb.  Rama then instructed him to roast the bulb, emphasizing the auspiciousness of the time. Laksmana then placed the bulb into the fire.  Once roasted, Laksmana informed Rama that the offering was ready. Rama, well-versed in ritual practices, performed the necessary rites to consecrate the hut. He then entered the dwelling, finding contentment in its completion. Following this, Rama performed further ceremonies, including offerings to various deities and the consecration of altars within the hermitage.  Finally, the three exiles – Rama, Sita, and Laksmana – entered their new home, a charming leaf-thatched hut, finding respite from their earlier hardships.  Their arrival at Citrakuta, with its abundant resources and natural beauty, brought Rama relief from the pain of exile.

Having been told of Sri Rama’s departure for Citrakuta by the spies of Guha
and taking leave of the latter, Sumantra drives back to Ayodhya. Entering
the royal gynaeceum, he submits to the Emperor what he reported
earlier to the citizens who followed his chariot. Dasaratha
and Kausalya fall into a swoon to hear about
Sri Rama’s departure for Citrakita and
all the inmates of the gynaeceum
burst into a wail from agony

__data__.text: Sumantra, grieving after Rama's departure, left Guha and returned to Ayodhya.  His journey back, passing woodlands, rivers, and towns, was swift.  Arriving in Ayodhya at dusk, he found the city eerily quiet.  Concerned, he worried that the city was suffering from Rama’s absence. As he entered the city, crowds of people rushed to him, desperate for news of Rama. Sumantra sadly informed them of Rama's departure across the Ganga.  The news was met with grief and despair. People lamented their loss, expressing how much they valued Rama’s presence in their city, recalling his role in their celebrations and his fatherly care. Sumantra also heard the sorrowful cries of women from the windows of their homes as he passed through the city streets.

__data__.text: As Sumantra journeyed through Ayodhya, he heard the city's residents lamenting Rama's absence. They mourned their beloved prince, expressing their despair at not seeing him in their daily life, at festivals, and ceremonies.  He heard women grieving from their windows, heartbroken by Rama’s exile.  Sumantra, his face veiled in sorrow, proceeded towards the palace.  He passed through the palace gates, witnessing the shared grief of the palace staff.  The women of the palace, having learned of Sumantra's return without Rama, cried out in anguish.  Their eyes, filled with tears, reflected their deep sorrow.  Sumantra overheard the queen's anxieties about how he would convey the news to Kausalya, Rama's mother. They marveled at Kausalya's strength in enduring her son's departure. Overwhelmed by the collective grief, Sumantra entered the palace.  He found Dasaratha, weak and distressed, consumed by sorrow for his son.  Sumantra relayed Rama's message, upon which the king fainted from grief.  The news of Dasaratha’s collapse spread through the palace, intensifying the existing sorrow, and the women wailed in despair.

__data__.text: Upon reaching the eighth gateway, Sumantra found King Dasaratha within the white chamber, grief-stricken and weakened by his son's exile.  Sumantra approached the seated king, offered his respects, and faithfully relayed Rama's message.  The king listened in silence, his mind in turmoil.  Overwhelmed by sorrow, he collapsed unconscious to the floor.  The news of the king's collapse quickly reached the women's quarters, causing great distress. As the king fell, the women cried out, raising their arms in anguish.  Queen Kausalya, aided by Sumitra, lifted her fallen husband. She questioned why he hadn't responded to Sumantra, who had completed the difficult task of accompanying Rama. Kausalya implored Dasaratha to rise, reminding him of his virtue and urging him not to succumb to despair. She reassured him that Kaikeyi, whose influence had led to this situation, was not present, so he could speak freely.  Overcome by her own grief, Kausalya then collapsed, weeping. Seeing Kausalya and the king in such states, the other women surrounding them began to wail. This collective cry of sorrow spread throughout the palace, drawing the attention of men and women, young and old, from all corners of Ayodhya, plunging the city once again into a state of turmoil.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, regaining consciousness after swooning, summoned Sumantra the charioteer. Sumantra, distressed and grieving for Rama, approached the king, who was overwhelmed with sorrow. Dasaratha lamented Rama's hardships, questioning how his son, accustomed to comfort, could endure living under a tree in the forest. He wondered how Rama, once followed by an entourage, could bear the isolation, and how he, Lakshmana, and Sita could reside in a place inhabited by wild animals.  The king questioned how the princes and Sita, accustomed to royal comforts, could travel on foot. He praised Sumantra for witnessing Rama's departure and urged him to recount every detail of Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita's words and actions in the forest, clinging to these details for solace.  Sumantra, deeply moved, began his narration, describing how Rama respectfully instructed him to convey his obeisance to his father.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, desperate for news of his exiled sons, implored Sumantra to recount every detail of their time in the forest.  Sumantra, heartbroken and struggling to speak through his tears, began to relay Rama's instructions. Rama, ever dutiful, requested that Sumantra convey his respects to his father, Dasaratha.  He also asked that his greetings be conveyed to the entire royal household, with special attention to his mother Kausalya's well-being.  He urged Kausalya to remain steadfast in her devotion and to treat Kaikeyi with the utmost respect.  Addressing Bharata, Rama emphasized the importance of filial duty, advising him to serve their father respectfully, regardless of the king's advanced age, and to treat Kausalya as his own mother.  Overcome with emotion, Rama wept as he delivered these messages. Lakshmana, filled with anger, questioned the injustice of Rama's exile, blaming Kaikeyi's influence and the king's hasty decision for their suffering.

__data__.text: Rama, weeping, reiterated his request to Bharata to consider Kausalya as his own mother.  Lakshmana, enraged, questioned the justification for Rama's exile. He criticized the king's hasty decision, influenced by Kaikeyi, declaring their suffering undeserved.  He questioned whether greed or a granted boon motivated the exile, asserting that regardless, it was a grave misstep.  Lakshmana professed his deep devotion to Rama, considering him a brother, master, kinsman, and even a father figure. He lamented the king's decision to exile Rama, the beloved of all, questioning how the populace could be pleased.  Meanwhile, Sita stood silently, overcome with grief, as if possessed by a spirit. She wept, unable to speak, her gaze fixed on Rama. As Sumantra prepared to leave, Sita, her face pale, burst into tears, her eyes fixed on her departing husband. Rama, with tear-streaked face and joined palms, stood supported by Lakshmana, as Sita continued to weep, watching the royal chariot depart.

__data__.text: Sita, her face pale and drawn, watched her husband as Sumantra prepared to leave for Ayodhya.  Overcome with grief, she suddenly burst into tears.  Simultaneously, Rama, his face streaked with tears and his palms pressed together in a gesture of respect, stood supported by Lakshmana. As the royal chariot began its departure, Sita, deeply sorrowful, continued to weep, her gaze fixed on Rama and the departing chariot.

__data__.text: Sumantra informs King Dasaratha of the sorrowful departure of Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita.  He describes his own grief and the reluctance of his horses to leave Rama.  Sumantra waited at Guha's residence for days, hoping Rama might summon him back. He then describes the widespread despair in the kingdom, marked by withered trees, dried-up rivers and ponds, and the stillness of the forest creatures.  The flowers lack fragrance, the fruits are less vibrant, and the gardens in Ayodhya are desolate.  His return to the city was met with silence and sighs, as the people mourned Rama's absence. Women wept from their homes upon seeing the chariot return without Rama, their faces streaked with tears.  Sumantra concludes by observing that everyone, regardless of their relationship to Rama, is equally consumed by grief.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Sumantra returns to Ayodhya and finds the city plunged into grief over Rama's exile.  Women weep openly, lamenting their inability to see Rama. Their tears streak their faces, blurring their carefully applied kohl.  Sumantra observes that everyone, regardless of their relationship with Rama – friend, foe, or neutral – is equally distressed. The city itself seems to mirror the people's sorrow; the animals are listless, the sounds of the city are replaced by sighs and cries of despair, and a sense of joylessness pervades everything.  He reports this desolate scene to Dasaratha, who responds with anguish.  Dasaratha blames himself for rashly agreeing to Rama's exile, manipulated by Kaikeyi and without consulting his advisors.  He wonders if this tragedy is divine punishment for some unknown sin.  He begs Sumantra to take him to Rama, his senses yearning for one last glimpse of his son. He desperately commands that someone bring Rama back, declaring he cannot live without him, even for an hour. He pleads with Sumantra to take him to Rama, no matter how far he has traveled. He longs to see his son, with his pearl-like teeth and mighty bow, alongside Sita.  Dasaratha declares that he will surely die if he cannot see Rama. Overwhelmed by grief and the pain of separation, the King compares his sorrow to a vast, turbulent ocean, fueled by Rama's and Sita's absence, with waves of tears and the cries of his people.  He laments his misfortune at not being able to see Rama in his current state of distress, and cries out to Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita, lamenting that they are unaware of his profound suffering.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, overwhelmed by grief, cries out, longing to see his son Rama. He believes he will die if he cannot see Rama, who has pearl-like teeth and a mighty bow, along with Sita. He laments his inability to see Rama in his current state, adorned with gem earrings. Dasaratha compares his sorrow to a vast ocean, difficult to navigate.  The force of the ocean is Rama's grief, its other shore is Sita's absence. Tears form the turbulent waters, while his flailing arms mimic fish leaping within.  Loud wails represent the ocean's roar, and his disheveled hair resembles seaweed. Kaikeyi is likened to submarine fire, Manthara's words to alligators, the boons granted to the coastline, and the term of exile to the ocean's vastness. The king collapses onto his couch, lamenting his inability to see Rama and Lakshmana. Queen Kausalya, hearing his cries, is gripped by renewed fear.
