__data__.text: Sumantra tried to console the grieving Queen Kausalya, who was distraught over Rama's exile. He told her that Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana were living peacefully in the forest.  He described how Lakshmana dutifully served Rama, and how Sita, though in a lonely forest, remained composed and found joy as if she were at home. Sita’s spirits were high, finding pleasure in the natural surroundings and inquiring about the various sights they encountered.  Sumantra added that Sita felt as if she were merely on a short trip from Ayodhya when Rama or Lakshmana were by her side. He briefly mentioned a comment Sita had made about Kaikeyi but quickly moved on to more comforting topics.

__data__.text: Sumantra continued his attempts to comfort Queen Kausalya by describing Sita's resilience and positive attitude in the forest.  He related how Sita, ever curious, inquired about the villages, cities, rivers, and trees they passed.  Her spirits remained high, and she felt as though she were on a mere outing from Ayodhya when Rama or Lakshmana were by her side. Sumantra briefly recalled a comment Sita had made about Kaikeyi, but quickly dismissed it as an unimportant aside. He emphasized Sita's enduring beauty, undiminished by the hardships of the journey.  Her face, he said, remained radiant, and her feet, though no longer adorned with alta, retained their reddish hue.  Sita continued to wear her jewelry, finding pleasure in it, and her graceful movements were reminiscent of the elegant gait of swans.  Despite the wild surroundings, she showed no fear of the forest's creatures, trusting in Rama's protection.  Sumantra concluded his description by reassuring Kausalya that Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana were not to be pitied, and that their story would become legendary.  He reminded her that they were fulfilling their father's promise and finding contentment in their simple forest life.  Despite Sumantra's comforting words, Queen Kausalya remained inconsolable, continuing to cry out for her beloved son, Rama.

__data__.text: Queen Kausalya, grieving the departure of Rama, rebukes King Dasaratha.  She questions how he could send Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana, raised in luxury, to endure the hardships of the forest. Kausalya worries how the delicate Sita will cope with the harsh weather, simple food, and frightening sounds of the forest, contrasting them with her accustomed comforts.  She laments Rama's absence, wondering where he sleeps now, deprived of his palace bed. Kausalya expresses her deep sorrow, comparing her steadfast heart to adamant. She condemns Dasaratha's decision as cruel, forcing her loved ones into an impoverished state.  Kausalya doubts Bharata will relinquish the throne upon Rama's return, comparing the situation to Brahmins refusing leftover food after a ritual. She questions why Rama, the rightful heir, would accept a kingdom already ruled by his younger brother.

__data__.text: Kausalya continues her lament, using analogies to illustrate the impossibility of Rama accepting the kingdom after Bharata's rule.  She compares it to Brahmins refusing leftover food, even if offered by other Brahmins, and to a tiger refusing to eat prey killed by another.  She also likens it to the reuse of ritualistic items, which are considered unsuitable for a second ceremony.  Kausalya asserts that Rama would find the kingdom, having been ruled by Bharata, as unappealing as spoiled wine or leftover Soma. She emphasizes Rama's strength and unwillingness to tolerate such an insult, comparing him to a mighty tiger that wouldn't allow its tail to be twisted.  Kausalya declares that even if all the worlds opposed him, Rama wouldn't be intimidated, but his adherence to righteousness prevents him from seizing the throne by force.  She portrays Rama's exile as a devastating blow dealt by his own father, comparing it to a fish devouring its own offspring.  Kausalya then questions Dasaratha's sense of righteousness, given his treatment of Rama. She lists a woman's sources of support – husband, son, and family – and declares that Dasaratha, controlled by Kaikeyi, is no longer a support to her, and Rama has been banished.  She feels utterly destroyed.  Kausalya concludes by declaring that Dasaratha has ruined the kingdom, his counselors, his citizens, herself, and Rama, leaving only Bharata and Kaikeyi pleased. Overwhelmed by Kausalya's harsh words, Dasaratha faints, crying out for Rama, and remembers his ill-fated promise.

__data__.text: Queen Kausalya continues her condemnation of King Dasaratha. She states that a woman relies on her husband, son, and family for support, but she has none.  Her husband is controlled by Kaikeyi, and her son, Rama, has been exiled. Kausalya is left without support and expresses her devastation, refusing to follow Rama into the forest as she cannot bear separation from the king. She accuses Dasaratha of ruining the kingdom, its people, their counselors, herself, and Rama. The only ones happy, she asserts, are Bharata and Kaikeyi. Overwhelmed by her harsh words, Dasaratha faints, crying out for Rama and remembering his ill-fated promise.

__data__.text: Overwhelmed by Kausalya's harsh words, King Dasaratha, already heartbroken over Rama's exile, fainted. Upon regaining consciousness, he saw Kausalya beside him and was overcome with remorse, remembering a past sin: the accidental killing of a young hermit.  Consumed by both this old guilt and his current grief, he trembled and pleaded with Kausalya for forgiveness, acknowledging her compassionate nature and emphasizing a wife's duty to her husband. Kausalya, deeply moved by his distress, wept and begged *his* forgiveness for her harsh words, attributing her outburst to the overwhelming grief of losing her son.  She acknowledged the impropriety of her actions, emphasizing the high regard she held for her husband.

__data__.text: Kausalya, prostrate at Dasaratha's feet, begged his forgiveness. She lamented that her grief-stricken outburst had caused her husband, a man deserving of respect, to plead with *her*.  Acknowledging her lapse in propriety, she confessed that her words were spoken out of turn, driven by the anguish of Rama's exile. Kausalya described grief as an insidious enemy that destroys reason and patience, making even small sorrows unbearable. For her, the mere five days of Rama's absence felt like five years.  Her sorrow, like a vast ocean fed by swift rivers, deepened with every thought of her son. As Kausalya spoke these conciliatory words, night fell, and the king, comforted by her remorse and exhausted by his own grief, drifted into sleep.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto LXIII

Waking from sleep after a while and recalling his sinful deed, which spelt
death to him, Dasaratha proceeds to tell Kausalya how, while he was Prince
Regent of Ayodhya, he went out a-hunting in the forest one day and
heard during the last watch of the night the sound of a hermit boy filling
his pitcher with water by submerging into Sarayu river. Mistaking the
gurgling sound for the trumpeting of an elephant, DaSaratha hit the
boy with an arrow, which dug deep into his body and mortally
wounded him. On approaching his quarry he discovered the
fatal blunder and tendered his heartfelt apology to the hermit
boy, who asked him to extract the arrow from his body and
report the matter to his aged parents. The boy died
immediately the arrow was taken out of his body

__data__.text: King Daśaratha, overwhelmed by grief after Rama and Lakshmana's exile, is consumed with anxiety.  The king, once mighty like Indra, is now shadowed by sorrow.  Remembering a past misdeed he believes responsible for his current suffering, Daśaratha decides to confess to Kausalya.  On the sixth night of Rama's exile, the king recalls his sin.  He acknowledges that one reaps what one sows, lamenting that a fool fails to consider the consequences of his actions.  Daśaratha uses analogies, comparing himself to someone who destroys a mango grove to cultivate less fruitful Palāśa trees, and to someone who tends to a Kimśuka tree, unaware of its undesirable outcome. He confesses to having made a grave error, driven by youthful folly. Daśaratha explains that, as a young prince skilled in archery, he earned the title of "sound-hitter," able to strike unseen targets by their sound alone. He now realizes this skill led to a terrible mistake, likening it to a child innocently consuming poison. Just as someone might be deceived by the Palāśa's beautiful flowers, Daśaratha was misled by his archery prowess. He sets the scene: Kausalya was not yet his wife, he was the prince regent, and the monsoon season, which fueled his desire to hunt, had arrived. The sun, having absorbed the earth's moisture and scorched the world, was now traversing the southern quarter, a region associated with spirits and fear.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: In the darkness, Daśaratha heard a sound like an elephant filling its trunk at the river.  Driven by his hunting instincts, he shot an arrow towards the sound, hoping to kill a large game animal. As dawn broke, a cry of pain pierced the air, followed by a human voice.  A forest-dweller, wounded and dying, questioned why he, a harmless ascetic, had been attacked. He lamented his fate, explaining he had only come to the secluded riverbank at night to collect water.  He wondered who shot him and what wrong he could possibly have committed to deserve such a fate.

__data__.text: Daśaratha recounts the tragic tale of the accidental killing. The dying ascetic cried out, questioning why he, a harmless, unarmed forest-dweller collecting water at night, was shot.  He lamented his fate and wondered who the archer was and what wrong he had done to deserve this. The ascetic further questioned how anyone could justify attacking a peaceful, hermit-like individual clad in bark and deerskin. He worried about the devastating impact of his death on his elderly, dependent parents.  He then described his failing condition and instructed Daśaratha to inform his father of the incident, providing directions to his hermitage. The ascetic reassured Daśaratha that his father’s anger, though intense, would not consume him if he confessed his guilt.  Despite being near death, the ascetic attempted to maintain composure and console Daśaratha by revealing he wasn't a Brahmin, thus lessening the karmic weight of the deed.

__data__.text: Dasaratha continues his tragic account.  The dying ascetic expressed concern for his elderly parents, who depended entirely on him. He wondered how they would survive without him. He lamented being killed by an unknown person while innocently collecting water, questioning what wrong he had committed to deserve such a fate. Daśaratha, deeply distressed by the young man's suffering, dropped his bow and arrow. Overwhelmed with grief and confusion, he rushed to the ascetic's side.  He found the young man lying by the Sarayu River, wounded by the arrow, his matted hair disheveled and his water pitcher emptied.  The ascetic, observing Daśaratha’s distress, questioned the king’s actions, emphasizing his own innocence and the devastating impact this would have on his blind, elderly parents who were waiting for him. The ascetic directed Daśaratha to inform his father of the tragedy, assuring him that his father’s anger, while justifiable, would not consume him if Daśaratha confessed. Then, noticing Daśaratha's hesitation to remove the arrow, fearing he would hasten the young ascetic’s death, the ascetic addressed the king’s concern, urging him to extract the arrow despite the inevitable outcome.  Daśaratha concluded this part of the narrative by describing the ascetic's labored breathing and eventual death on the riverbank.

Waking from sleep after a while and recalling his sinful deed, which spelt
death to him, Dasaratha proceeds to tell Kausalya how, while he was Prince

heard during the last watch of the night the sound of a hermit boy filling

__data__.text: Dasaratha, filled with remorse, recounts how he approached the dying ascetic youth, begging his forgiveness. The young man, suffering intensely, requested Dasaratha to remove the arrow, comparing its agonizing effect to a river eroding a sandbank.  Dasaratha hesitated, realizing that removing the arrow would likely hasten the youth's death.  The ascetic, sensing Dasaratha's inner turmoil, reassured him, urging him to remove the arrow despite the inevitable outcome.  Composing himself with remarkable fortitude despite his failing strength, the ascetic comforted Dasaratha further, revealing he was not a Brahmin but the son of a Vaisya and a Sudra woman, thereby lessening the gravity of Dasaratha's offense. As the young man’s condition worsened, Dasaratha finally removed the arrow. The ascetic, his gaze fixed on Dasaratha, then passed away, leaving the king heartbroken and filled with regret as he watched the lifeless body on the riverbank.

DaSaratha continues to tell Kausalya how, having sought the presence of
the aged parents of the deceased, he apprised them of the sad incident
and escorted them to the river bank, where their son lay dead; how,
clasping the boy to their bosom, they both piteously wailed and
offered libations of water to his spirit for its benefit; how, invested
with an ethereal body, the spirit ascended to heaven while

the ghost and attained the Supreme. Having thus

__data__.text: Dasaratha, stricken with grief, recounted to Kausalya how he mistakenly killed the hermit boy.  He described his journey to the boy's hermitage, finding the boy's elderly, blind parents waiting expectantly for their son's return. Dasaratha's heart was heavy with dread as he approached them.  He explained how the old hermit, unaware of his son's death, called out for him. The hermit urged his son to return quickly, reminding him of his anxious mother and reassuring him that any perceived offense would be forgiven. The hermit's words, emphasizing the boy's importance as their eyes and support, intensified Dasaratha's guilt.  Dasaratha then confessed his true identity to the hermit, revealing he was a Kshatriya king and not the boy's son. He detailed how, while hunting near the river, he mistook the sound of the boy filling a water pitcher for an animal and shot him with an arrow.

__data__.text: Dasaratha, struggling to control his fear, confessed his true identity and the circumstances of the boy's death to the boy's father. He explained that, mistaking the sound of the boy filling a water pitcher for an animal, he had shot him with an arrow. He described finding the boy near death on the riverbank and how, after removing the arrow, the boy ascended to heaven. Dasaratha pleaded with the hermit for guidance on what to do next.  The hermit, despite his grief and fury, acknowledged Dasaratha's unintentional act. He explained that had Dasaratha not confessed, he would have faced dire consequences.  The hermit then asked Dasaratha to lead him and his wife to their deceased son. He expressed their desire to see their son one last time, describing the heartbreaking image of their son lying lifeless, covered in blood, and with his deerskin clothing scattered around him.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha recounts the hermit's lament over his dead son. The hermit questioned why his son lay lifeless, bathed in blood, his deerskin clothes scattered around him. He wondered who would now perform his morning rituals and console him in his grief. He worried about how he would survive without his son's support, particularly given his wife's advanced age and blindness.  Despairing, the hermit expressed his intention to join his son in death, asking Yama, the god of death, to allow his son to remain with them.  He pleaded with Yama, appealing to his piety, to grant him this one boon and ease his suffering.

__data__.text: Dasaratha continued his narration to Kausalya, describing how the hermit grieved for his deceased son.  The old man pleaded with his son's spirit not to depart for the afterlife yet, promising to join him soon along with his wife. He lamented their helpless state in the forest and envisioned himself beseeching Yama, the god of death, to allow his son to remain and care for them. The hermit argued his piety and asked for this single boon. He then urged his son to ascend to the heavenly realms reserved for warriors slain in battle, comparing his fate to that of noble kings and those who lived virtuous lives. The hermit declared that no one born in their lineage could face an evil destiny, implying that the one responsible for his son's death would suffer such a fate.  He then performed the last rites for his son. Dasaratha then described how the young man, now possessing a celestial form, ascended to heaven accompanied by Indra. The hermit’s son consoled his parents, assuring them they would soon rejoin him in the afterlife before departing in a magnificent aerial chariot.  He then turned to Dasaratha, who stood respectfully nearby.

__data__.text: Dasaratha recounted how the hermit’s son, after conversing with his parents and Indra, ascended to heaven in a celestial chariot.  He consoled his parents, promising they would soon join him.  The hermit, having performed the last rites, turned to Dasaratha and cursed him. He lamented that Dasaratha had rendered him childless and asked the king to end his life as well.  He declared that Dasaratha would also suffer the agony of losing a son. Although acknowledging Dasaratha's unintentional error, the hermit pronounced a severe curse, emphasizing the pain of his loss. He explained that the consequence of the king's action would inevitably come, just as sickness follows unhealthy food. After the curse, the hermit couple ascended to heaven. Dasaratha then lamented to Kausalya, recognizing the curse's fulfillment. He cried out in grief for Rama, his beloved son and protector, feeling overwhelmed by the approaching sorrow.

__data__.text: King Dasaratha, overcome with grief and fear, told Kausalya that the sage's curse was upon him.  He lamented his impending death due to his separation from Rama, saying he could no longer even see her.  He wished Rama could touch him or that he could regain his wealth and position, believing these might save him.  Dasaratha acknowledged the injustice he had inflicted upon Rama and the nobility Rama had shown in accepting his exile. He questioned how any father could banish his son, and how any son wouldn't resent such treatment.  His memory fading, Dasaratha felt the messengers of death urging him onward. He expressed his anguish at dying without seeing Rama again.  Dasaratha described the unbearable grief of Rama's absence, comparing it to a scorching sun drying up a small amount of water. He declared that those who would see Rama's return after fourteen years would be blessed. He described Rama's beauty – his earrings, face, teeth, nose – and envisioned his return to Ayodhya.  His heart sinking, Dasaratha described his failing senses, unable to perceive sound, touch, or taste, his mind overwhelmed with sorrow.  He continued to lament in the presence of Kausalya and Sumitra, ultimately succumbing to his grief and passing away in the middle of the night.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Dasaratha's lament continued, his senses failing as his grief intensified. He mourned his inability to see Rama, whose absence felt like a scorching sun drying up his very life force. He envied those who would witness Rama's return, describing his son's beauty and the joy his arrival would bring.  Dasaratha's heart sank, his mind clouded with sorrow.  He felt the messengers of death closing in, urging him to his end.  He cried out in anguish, unable to see or hear, consumed by the pain of Rama's exile.  The king then succumbed to his grief, passing away in the middle of the night in the presence of Kausalya and Sumitra.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: The king's retinue, including bards and musicians, gathered at the royal residence early the next morning.  They sang praises and played instruments, filling the palace with music and echoing benedictions.  Birds, both free and caged, joined the chorus with their own songs.  Attendants, primarily women and eunuchs, stood ready to serve the king.  Water scented with sandalwood, along with auspicious items like holy water and fresh garments, were prepared for the king's bath and attire. As the sun rose, the retinue grew anxious, wondering why the king hadn't yet emerged. The ladies-in-waiting near his bedside attempted to rouse him gently, but found no response.  Touching him with care and reverence, they detected no signs of life, noticing the absence of a heartbeat or pulse.  Fearful and trembling, they began to suspect the worst, realizing the king had passed away.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available

__data__.text: The king's secondary wives approached him gently, attempting to wake him.  Despite their respectful efforts, they found no signs of life in the king.  Recognizing the stillness, they became fearful, trembling like reeds in a current.  Their suspicion that the king had died became a certainty.  Meanwhile, Kausalya and Sumitra remained deeply asleep, overcome by grief from their sons' absence. Kausalya, dimmed by sorrow, appeared like a star hidden by darkness.  Neither she nor Sumitra, whose face was tear-stained, showed any outward awareness of the king's condition.  Observing the sleeping queens and the lifeless king, the palace women concluded King Dasaratha had passed away in his sleep.  They began to wail loudly, their cries echoing through the palace like elephants mourning a lost leader. The noise startled Kausalya and Sumitra awake.  They too, upon seeing and touching the king, cried out in grief and collapsed.  Kausalya, covered in dust, lay on the floor. The other women watched in despair as Kausalya, overcome by grief, fell to the ground.  Kaikeyi and the king's other wives, also overwhelmed with sorrow, wept and fainted.  The palace filled with the sound of their combined lamentations.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available

__data__.text: The king's wives, including Kaikeyi, overwhelmed by grief, fainted. Their cries intensified the already deafening wails emanating from the women's quarters, amplifying the sorrowful sounds. The palace, once a place of joy, was now filled with fear, confusion, and the cries of distraught relatives mourning King Dasaratha's passing.  The king's wives, deeply saddened, wept and clung to his lifeless body, lamenting their loss like helpless women.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Queen Kausalya, overcome with grief, mourned the passing of King Dasaratha and the exile of Rama.  She lamented to Kaikeyi, blaming her for the king's demise and Rama's banishment. Kausalya expressed her despair, feeling unable to live without her husband and son. She condemned Kaikeyi's actions, claiming they stemmed from greed and led to the ruin of their family.  Kausalya worried about Sita's well-being in the forest and feared that the news of Rama's exile would devastate her father, King Janaka.  Overwhelmed by sorrow, she declared her intention to join her husband in death by self-immolation. The ministers, witnessing her profound grief, gently separated her from the king's body and prepared it for preservation, knowing that the funeral rites could not be performed without a son present.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: The ministers gently removed the grieving Queen Kausalya from King Dasaratha's body.  They then placed the king in an oil-filled trough for preservation, following the instructions of Vasistha and the other priests.  Aware of the proper funeral rites, the ministers knew they could not proceed without a son present. The queens, learning of the king’s placement in the oil-filled trough, lamented his death. Overwhelmed with grief and crying, they questioned why the king had abandoned them, especially after Rama's exile.  They expressed their despair at facing the future without their husband and sons, and in the presence of Kaikeyi. The queens mourned the loss of their protector and lord, Rama, who had left for the forest. They wondered how they could endure life without the king and Rama, subjected to Kaikeyi’s presence.  The queens lamented, highlighting Kaikeyi's role in their abandonment. Filled with sorrow, they wept for Dasaratha.  Ayodhya, without its king, was likened to a starless night or a woman without her husband. The city, once vibrant, was now filled with the sounds of mourning.  As the king passed away and his queens grieved, the sun set and darkness fell. The king's friends and relatives, gathered there, decided against cremation without a son present, and so they left the king in the oil-filled trough.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Ayodhya mourned King Dasaratha.  Without its king, the city felt as empty as a starless night or a woman without her husband.  Its streets and houses were desolate, its people weeping openly. With the king gone and his queens grieving, the sun set, symbolizing the descent of darkness upon the kingdom.  The king's friends and relatives decided against cremation in the absence of a son, placing his body in an oil-filled trough for preservation.  The city, without Dasaratha, was like the sky without the sun or a night without stars, its crossroads filled with grieving people.  Throughout Ayodhya, groups of people gathered, expressing their sorrow and blaming Kaikeyi for the tragic events.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: The city of Ayodhya mourned through a long, sorrowful night.  At daybreak, the Brahmanas who had assumed the king's duties gathered at court.  Along with the ministers, respected sages like Markandeya, Maudgalya, Vamadeva, Kasyapa, Katyayana, Gautama, and Jabali addressed Vasistha, the royal priest. They lamented the king's passing due to grief over Rama and Lakshmana's absence. With the king gone, Rama and Lakshmana in the forest, and Bharata and Satrughna away in Kekaya, the sages stressed the urgent need for a new king.  They argued that a kingdom without a ruler quickly descends into chaos.  They cited the lack of rain, the reluctance to cultivate crops, the breakdown of social order, the absence of security, and the cessation of public works, religious ceremonies, and festivals as consequences of a kingless state.  They urged Vasistha to appoint a new king from the house of Ikshvaku immediately to avert disaster.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

on the throne immediately

__data__.text: The sages continued to emphasize the critical need for a king.  Without a ruler, they argued, Ayodhya would descend into chaos. Public projects like assembly halls, gardens, and temples would be abandoned.  Religious ceremonies and sacrifices, especially the elaborate "satras," would cease. Even wealthy Brahmanas would hesitate to offer substantial donations, fearing they might become targets for thieves.  Public festivals and gatherings would dwindle, and legal disputes would remain unresolved.  Young women would be afraid to venture out, even in groups. Farmers and herders, despite having guards, would feel insecure.  Travel for pleasure or trade would be dangerous.  Ascetics would struggle to find food and shelter. The army would weaken, leaving the kingdom vulnerable to attack.  Scholars would not be able to meet and debate freely.  Even simple acts of worship would become difficult.  In short, without a king, there would be no prosperity, no security, and no joy.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

Vasistha to install any of the princes
on the throne immediately

__data__.text: The sages continued their impassioned plea for the immediate installation of a king. They described a kingdom without a ruler as vulnerable and chaotic.  Without a king, the army would be weak and unable to defend against enemies. People would not feel safe to travel or display their wealth.  Scholars would be unable to gather and discuss important matters.  Religious ceremonies and offerings would cease.  Even princes would lose their prominence and vibrancy.  A kingdom without a leader is like a river without water, a forest without grass, or cattle without a herder. They mourned the passing of King Dasaratha, who had been their guiding light, comparing him to the standard of a chariot or smoke indicating a fire. His absence left a void, and they feared that without a new king, people would turn on each other like predatory fish. They warned that the absence of law and order would embolden those who disregard societal norms and religious principles.  Just as an eye guides the body, a king guides the kingdom, embodying truth and righteousness. The king, they argued, is the source of stability and prosperity, a protector like a parent, and even surpasses powerful deities in his role. Without a king to distinguish right from wrong, the world would be plunged into darkness.  Reaffirming their loyalty and obedience to Vasistha, the sages implored him to choose and crown a successor from the Ikshvaku dynasty, or anyone suitable, to restore order and prevent further decline.

__data__.text: Vasistha, after hearing the counsel of the sages, addressed the ministers and Brahmins, explaining that Bharata, who was given the kingship, was living happily with Satrughna in his maternal grandfather's kingdom.  He proposed sending swift messengers to retrieve them.  The assembly agreed, and Vasistha summoned Siddhartha, Vijaya, Jayanta, Asoka, and Nandana, instructing them to travel quickly to Rajagrha and inform Bharata of urgent business, without mentioning Rama's exile, Dasaratha's death, or any potential threat to the Raghu dynasty. They were also instructed to take gifts of fine clothes and ornaments for Bharata and his grandfather.  The messengers prepared for their journey, gathering provisions and mounting swift horses.  After receiving Vasistha's permission, they departed, traveling past the Malini River between the Aparatala and Pralamba mountains. They then crossed the Ganga at Hastinapura, passing through the Panchala territory and Kurujangala, observing the lakes and rivers along the way.  Finally, they quickly crossed the Saradanda River, a beautiful waterway teeming with birds and clear water.

__data__.text: The messengers continued their journey, passing the Malini River between the Aparatala and Pralamba mountains.  They then crossed the Ganga at Hastinapura, traveling westward through Kurujangala and the Panchala territory. They observed the flourishing lakes and clear rivers along their route.  They quickly crossed the Saradanda River, a beautiful, clear waterway frequented by various birds.  After respectfully circling a sacred tree called Satyopayacana on the riverbank, they entered the city of Kulinga.  Continuing on, they passed through the village of Tejobhibhavana and then Abhikala, crossing the Iksumati River. They traveled through the Bahlika kingdom, past the Sudama mountain, observing Brahmins dedicated to their spiritual practices. They saw the rivers Vipasa and Salmali, along with wells, ponds, and lakes.  They also observed various animals, including lions, tigers, deer, and elephants.  Despite their horses' fatigue, they efficiently traveled a long, peaceful road to Girivraja, the capital of the Kekaya kingdom. Driven by their loyalty to Vasistha, their concern for the people's welfare, and their desire to uphold their king's honor, the messengers entered the city that night.

__data__.text: Bharata had a disturbing dream the night messengers arrived in Girivraja.  He awoke troubled near dawn.  His friends tried to comfort him with music, dancing, and storytelling.  However, Bharata remained despondent. When asked about his sadness, Bharata described his dream: his father falling from a mountain into a pool of cow dung, swimming in it, drinking oil, and then eating rice covered in sesame oil before submerging himself in oil. He also saw a dried-up ocean, the moon fallen to earth, the world shrouded in darkness and plagued by demons, a broken elephant tusk, extinguished fires, a cracked earth with withered trees and smoking, crumbling mountains.  He then saw his father, dressed in black and seated on an iron throne, being attacked by dark-complexioned women.  Finally, he saw his father, wearing a red garland and smeared with red paste, riding south in a chariot pulled by donkeys.

__data__.text: Bharata's unsettling dream continued. He saw the king's elephant's tusk shattered, and ceremonial fires abruptly extinguished. The earth was cracked, the trees withered, and the mountains crumbled and smoked.  He witnessed his father, dressed in black and seated on an iron throne, being harassed by dark-complexioned women.  His father then appeared wearing a red garland and red paste, riding south in a donkey-drawn chariot. A grotesque, red-clad ogress mocked and pulled the king.  Bharata feared this dream foretold the death of himself, Rama, his father, or Lakshmana. He recalled the ill omen of riding a donkey-drawn chariot in a dream, signifying an impending funeral pyre.  He admitted his distress to his friends, explaining his parched throat, unease, and inexplicable self-loathing. Although he couldn't pinpoint the source of his fear, he felt a deep sense of foreboding, especially as he dwelled on his father's uncertain fate.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Bharata, while recounting a dream to his friends in Rajagrha, was visited by messengers from Ayodhya.  The messengers, having completed their arduous journey, were respectfully received by the King of Kekaya and his son.  They delivered Sage Vasistha's message to Bharata, assuring him of everyone's well-being and urging his swift return to Ayodhya for an important matter. The messengers also presented Bharata with valuable gifts of robes and jewels to be given to his maternal grandfather and uncle.  Twenty crores worth of gifts were designated for the king and ten crores for the uncle. Bharata, known for his affection for family, dutifully accepted and distributed the gifts.  He then inquired about the well-being of his father, King Dasaratha, his brothers Rama and Lakshmana, and his mothers Kausalya, Sumitra, and Kaikeyi.  The messengers reassured Bharata of their good health and prompted him to prepare for his journey, indicating that fortune favored him.  Bharata agreed to depart after seeking permission from his grandfather.  He assured his grandfather of his return in the future.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available

__data__.text: Bharata, assured of his family's well-being, prepared for his return to Ayodhya.  He informed the messengers of his intention to seek his grandfather's permission.  He respectfully addressed his grandfather, expressing his desire to leave and promising to visit again.  His grandfather affectionately granted him permission, conveying his blessings to Kaikeyi and requesting Bharata to send his regards to his parents, Sage Vasistha, other Brahmanas, Rama, and Lakshmana. The king of Kekaya generously bestowed gifts upon Bharata, including elephants, blankets, deerskins, gold, large dogs, gold coins, and horses.  King Aswapati provided esteemed ministers as escorts, and Bharata's uncle, Yudhajit, gifted him elephants and mules.  Despite these generous offerings, Bharata felt anxious due to the messengers' urgency and a disturbing dream he had experienced, and he prioritized his departure.  He returned to his residence, then departed, traveling along a bustling main road filled with people, elephants, and horses. He then proceeded to the palace’s inner chambers, entering unhindered.

__data__.text: Bharata, despite receiving lavish gifts from the king of Kekaya, was preoccupied with his imminent departure for Ayodhya.  A disturbing dream and the messengers' urgency fueled his anxiety.  He briefly returned to his residence before venturing onto the bustling main road, crowded with people, elephants, and horses.  He then proceeded to the palace's inner chambers, entering without hindrance.  After bidding farewell to his maternal grandparents and uncle Yudhajit, Bharata, accompanied by Satrughna, embarked on his journey.  A retinue of one hundred chariots provided by King Aswapati, along with camels, bullocks, horses, mules, and servants, followed Bharata.  Under the protection of his grandfather's guards and accompanied by esteemed ministers, Bharata, known for his strength and lack of enemies, departed with Satrughna, much like a celestial being leaving the heavens.

__data__.text: Bharata journeyed from Rajagrha, crossing the Sudama and Hradini rivers. He then crossed the westward-flowing Satadru river and another at Ailadhana.  He continued through the Aparaparvata region and a river said to petrify objects, reaching the Salyakarsana area. After purifying himself in the Silavaha river, he crossed the MahaSaila hills towards the Caitraratha forest. He proceeded past the Saraswati river where it meets the Ganga, through Viramatsya, and into the Bharunda forest. After crossing the Kulinga river and reaching the Yamuna, Bharata's party rested.  He saw to the horses' needs before continuing. Bharata swiftly crossed a large, unfrequented forest.  He then approached the Ganga river near the village of Arnsudhana, aiming to cross at Pragvata. He crossed the Ganga at Pragvata and the Kutikostika river, arriving at Dharmavardhana.  He passed through Torana and reached Varutha.  He rested in a nearby grove before traveling to Ujjinana's gardens, known for its Kadamba trees.  There, he switched to faster horses and continued ahead, allowing his army to follow at a slower pace. He then stopped at Sarvatirtha. The next morning, he crossed the Uttanika and other rivers using mountain ponies, reached Hastiprsthaka, and crossed the Kutika river and, at Lohitya, the Kapivati.

__data__.text: Bharata, after spending a night in a pleasant grove, traveled east towards Ujjinana's gardens, known for their Kadamba trees.  Upon reaching the gardens, he switched to faster horses, allowing his army to follow at a more relaxed pace. He stopped for the night at Sarvatirtha. The next morning, he crossed the Uttanika river and other waterways using mountain ponies.  He reached Hastiprsthaka and crossed the Kutika river. At Lohitya, he crossed the Kapivati river.  He crossed the Sthanumati river at Ekasala and the Gomati river near Vinata. He quickly arrived at a sal tree grove near Kalinganagara and, despite his horses' exhaustion, pressed on through the night.  After seven nights of travel, he reached Ayodhya at dawn. Upon seeing the city, Bharata remarked to his charioteer that Ayodhya seemed subdued and less vibrant than he remembered.  He noted the absence of the usual bustling crowds of people and the lack of activity in the gardens. He observed the quietness, missing the sounds of people and animals that once filled the city.  He found the gardens, usually full of life and joy, now deserted and melancholic.

__data__.text: Bharata, upon reaching Ayodhya, observed a stark change in the city's atmosphere.  It seemed deserted, like a woodland, devoid of the usual activity of people traveling on conveyances, elephants, or horses. The gardens, once vibrant and joyful, were now desolate and melancholic.  The joyous sounds of animals and birds were absent.  Even the fragrant breeze, usually carrying the scent of sandalwood, aloe, and incense, was missing.  The music of kettledrums, tomtoms, and vinas, which once filled the city, had ceased.  Bharata noticed unsettling omens, causing him anxiety and a sense of foreboding.  He felt a deep unease, his heart sinking with worry.  Entering Ayodhya through the Vaijayanta gate, his horses exhausted, he questioned his charioteer about the hurried and unexplained journey, expressing his growing fear of misfortune. He saw signs that he associated with the death of kings, noting the unkempt houses, their doors ajar, and their overall lack of splendor.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: Bharata observed further signs of neglect and despair in Ayodhya. The houses were unswept and lacked their usual splendor, their doors left ajar.  Offerings and incense were absent, and families seemed to have gone without food. The temples were deserted, devoid of garlands and the usual bustling activity.  Merchants were distressed, their businesses stagnant. Even the animals in sacred places appeared troubled. The city's inhabitants were dressed in soiled clothes, their faces tear-streaked and sorrowful.  Deeply disturbed by these ominous signs, Bharata proceeded to the royal palace. The once vibrant city, comparable to Indra's celestial capital, was now covered in dust and desolate.  Overcome with grief at this unfamiliar and distressing sight, Bharata entered his father's quarters, his head bowed in sorrow.

__data__.text: Bharata returned to the palace and, not finding his father in his usual chambers, went to see his mother, Kaikeyi.  Overjoyed at his return, Kaikeyi rose from her golden seat to greet him. Bharata, noticing the lack of splendor in her apartments, respectfully touched her feet. After embracing him affectionately, Kaikeyi inquired about his journey and the well-being of his maternal grandfather and uncle.  Bharata responded that it had been seven nights since he left his grandfather's home, that his relatives were well, and explained that he had returned ahead of the rest of the party due to the slow pace of the treasure-laden animals.  He then expressed his concern about the king's absence, noting the empty, gold-plated couch and the subdued demeanor of the royal attendants.  He asked his mother where the king was, wondering if perhaps he was with Kausalya. Kaikeyi, driven by her ambition, then began to tell Bharata the terrible news of his father's death, phrasing it as though it were a natural occurrence.

__data__.text: Bharata, not finding his father in Kaikeyi's chambers, expressed his desire to pay respects to his father. He wondered if his father was perhaps with Kausalya. Kaikeyi, blinded by her ambition, informed Bharata of his father's passing, presenting it as the natural course of life. Upon hearing this news, the unsuspecting Bharata collapsed to the ground, overwhelmed by grief. He lamented his father's absence, comparing the now desolate royal couch to a moonless sky or a dried-up sea.  Overcome with sorrow, Bharata wept, covering his face. Witnessing her son's anguish, Kaikeyi urged him to rise, reminding him of his virtuous nature and the strength of his character.  Despite his grief, Bharata continued to weep and lament, recalling his joyful anticipation of returning to Ayodhya for Rama's installation as Prince Regent or a royal sacrifice.

__data__.text: Bharata, overcome with grief, questioned his mother, Kaikeyi, about the circumstances of his father's death. He expressed his surprise and sorrow at not being able to see his father upon his return, lamenting the missed opportunity to receive his father's affectionate embrace.  Bharata yearned to be reunited with Rama, now his support and guide in his father's absence. He implored Kaikeyi to convey his arrival to Rama, emphasizing his deep bond with his brother.  Bharata then inquired about his father's final words.  Kaikeyi revealed that Dasharatha passed away calling out for Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana.  She further relayed Dasharatha's final message, expressing his wish that people would witness the return of Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana to Ayodhya. This news deepened Bharata's sorrow.

__data__.text: Upon learning of his father's passing, Bharata inquired about his final moments. Kaikeyi revealed that Dasharatha died calling out for Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana, expressing his hope that people would witness their return to Ayodhya.  Distressed by this news, Bharata asked about Rama's current whereabouts. Kaikeyi explained that Rama, along with Sita and Lakshmana, had gone to the Dandaka forest.  Concerned, Bharata questioned whether Rama had committed any wrongdoing that led to his exile. Kaikeyi assured him that Rama had harmed no one and hadn't taken anyone's property or wife. She then confessed that she had requested Dasharatha to exile Rama and make Bharata king. Dasharatha, true to his word, granted her request.  Kaikeyi concluded by stating that the king's grief over Rama's exile led to his demise.

__data__.text: Kaikeyi continued her explanation to Bharata, asserting that Rama had not committed any offense deserving of exile. He had neither stolen anyone's possessions nor wronged any individual, including never looking upon another man's wife with improper intent. Kaikeyi then confessed her own role in Rama's exile, admitting that she had requested Dasharatha to banish Rama and appoint Bharata as king.  Driven by his commitment to his word, Dasharatha agreed to her demands.  Overwhelmed by grief at Rama's departure, the king eventually passed away. Kaikeyi urged Bharata to assume the throne, emphasizing that her actions were motivated by her desire for his advancement.  She advised him to remain resolute and not succumb to sorrow, reminding him that the welfare of Ayodhya now rested upon his shoulders. Finally, she instructed Bharata to promptly conduct his father's funeral rites according to proper custom and then ascend to the throne, ruling with unwavering strength and courage.

__data__.text: Bharata, overwhelmed with grief, questioned the purpose of kingship without his father and noble brother, Rama. He accused Kaikeyi of causing immense suffering by orchestrating the king's demise and Rama's exile, comparing her actions to rubbing salt into his wounds.  He condemned her as a destructive force, likening her to the night of universal dissolution, and blamed her for his father's death. He lamented her blindness to good, highlighting the suffering she inflicted upon his family, particularly his mothers Kausalya and Sumitra.  Bharata emphasized Rama's devotion and service, contrasting it with Kaikeyi's actions. He questioned her lack of remorse for exiling Rama, a noble and selfless hero.  He declared his own inability to rule without Rama and Lakshmana, emphasizing Rama's strength and the king's dependence on him.

__data__.text: Bharata continues his condemnation of Kaikeyi, questioning her motives for exiling the virtuous Rama. He highlights Rama's innocence and self-control, wondering what Kaikeyi hoped to gain by sending him into the forest.  Bharata asserts that her greed for power blinded her to his own deep devotion to Rama, leading to this tragic situation. He despairs at the thought of ruling without the strength and support of Rama and Lakshmana, likening his position to a calf trying to carry a load meant for a bull.  Even if he could manage the kingdom, Bharata vows to thwart Kaikeyi's ambitions and prevent her from achieving her goals. He expresses his disgust at her sinful actions, restrained only by Rama's unwavering respect for her. Bharata questions how such a malicious plan could have taken root in Kaikeyi's mind, contrasting it with the established tradition of the eldest son inheriting the throne. He accuses her of disregarding the established moral code and customs of kings, particularly the Ikshvaku dynasty, emphasizing the importance of honoring the eldest son's right to rule. He laments the damage her actions have inflicted on their family's reputation for righteousness. Finally, Bharata vows to defy Kaikeyi's wishes, bring Rama back from exile, and serve him devotedly.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto LXXIII

Bitterly reproaching Kaikeyi in many ways, and cutting her to the quick
by his caustic remarks, Bharata takes a vow before her to bring
back Sri Rama from the forest, install him on the throne
of Ayodhya and wait upon him as
an attendant to offend her

Canto LXXIII

Bitterly reproaching Kaikeyi in many ways, and cutting her to the quick
by his caustic remarks, Bharata takes a vow before her to bring
back Sri Rama from the forest, install him on the throne
of Ayodhya and wait upon him as
an attendant to offend her

__data__.text: Bharata continues his sharp rebuke of Kaikeyi, questioning how someone of her lineage could succumb to such corrupted reasoning. He declares he will defy her wicked plan, which he believes has brought ruin upon them all.  To further oppose her, Bharata vows to bring Rama back from the forest and serve him devotedly.  After this declaration, deeply grieved but resolute, Bharata roars like a lion, his voice echoing his anguish and determination.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto LXXIV

Severely reproaching his mother once more, and making up his mind not only
to bring back Sri Rama from the forest and crown him king, but also
to go into exile for fourteen years in order to redeem his elder
brother’s vow; Bharata, who is filled with rage at the
thought of the mischief wrought by his mother,
cannot contain his grief and falls
unconscious on the ground

Canto LXXIV

Severely reproaching his mother once more, and making up his mind not only
to bring back Sri Rama from the forest and crown him king, but also
to go into exile for fourteen years in order to redeem his elder
brother’s vow; Bharata, who is filled with rage at the
thought of the mischief wrought by his mother,
cannot contain his grief and falls
unconscious on the ground

__data__.text: Bharata, overwhelmed with anger, again reproached his mother, Kaikeyi, for her wicked actions. He accused her of abandoning virtue and causing the exile of Rama and death of Dasaratha. Bharata lamented that due to Kaikeyi’s actions, he had lost his father, his brother was exiled, and he himself faced disgrace.  He questioned her relationship with his other mothers and compared her to a destructive ogress rather than the daughter of the wise king Aswapati. He further pointed out the pain she caused Kausalya by separating her from both her husband and son, and questioned what afterlife awaited her given her sins.  Bharata also emphasized the deep bond between mother and son, highlighting Rama's importance as Kausalya's firstborn and likened the situation to the distress felt by Surabhi, the divine cow, upon seeing her sons collapse while working.

__data__.text: Bharata continues to condemn Kaikeyi's actions, questioning her fate in the afterlife after separating the pious Kausalya from her husband and son. He reminds her of Rama's importance as his elder brother, equivalent to a father figure.  Bharata then explains the deep bond between a mother and son, emphasizing a son's origin from both parents, making him especially dear to his mother. He recounts the story of Surabhi, the divine cow, grieving over her exhausted sons pulling a plow. Surabhi's tears fall upon Indra, who, witnessing her sorrow, inquires about the cause. Surabhi explains her distress at seeing her sons suffer, emphasizing the unmatched love of a mother for her child. Indra, observing Surabhi's grief, recognizes the profound bond between mother and son.

__data__.text: Bharata, deeply distressed, uses the example of Surabhi, the divine cow, to illustrate the profound love a mother has for her child.  Even Surabhi, with countless offspring, grieved over her sons' suffering. Indra, witnessing her tears, recognized the depth of her maternal love. Bharata emphasizes that if even Surabhi, with so many children, feels such pain, how much more will Kausalya suffer, having only Rama and now separated from him?  He condemns Kaikeyi again, predicting her suffering in this life and the next for her actions.  Bharata declares his plan: he will return Rama to Ayodhya and restore his rightful place.  He will then go into exile himself, fulfilling Rama's fourteen-year vow.  Overwhelmed by grief and anger at Kaikeyi, Bharata collapses unconscious, his garments loosened and jewels scattered.

__data__.text: Bharata, overcome with grief and anguish, collapsed to the ground. His clothing was disheveled, and his jewels were scattered around him.  His fall was likened to a banner of Indra, lowered after a grand festival, symbolizing the abrupt end to his and the kingdom's joy.

__data__.text: Bharata, overwhelmed with grief and remorse, vehemently denies any involvement in his mother Kaikeyi's schemes.  He asserts he was away with Shatrughna and unaware of Rama's coronation or subsequent exile.  Meanwhile, Kausalya, hearing Bharata's lament, mistakenly assumes he is celebrating his newfound kingship.  She bitterly reproaches him, questioning Kaikeyi's motives and expressing her desire to join Rama in exile.  Overcome with emotion, Kausalya collapses.  Both Bharata and Shatrughna rush to her side and embrace her.  Kausalya, regaining consciousness, continues to lament, urging Bharata to either send her to Rama or take her there himself.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Kausalya, believing Bharata complicit in Rama's exile, continues her lament.  She questions why Kaikeyi would send Rama, dressed as an ascetic, into the forest. Kausalya expresses her desire to join Rama, suggesting Kaikeyi should send her as well, or that she will go herself, accompanied by Sumitra. Alternatively, she implores Bharata to take her to Rama.  She accuses Bharata of benefiting from Rama's exile, inheriting a prosperous kingdom filled with riches and military resources.  Bharata, deeply hurt by these accusations, faints.  Upon regaining consciousness, he falls at Kausalya's feet and declares his innocence.  He professes his love for Rama and curses whoever was truly responsible for Rama's exile, wishing upon them a litany of misfortunes, from a lowly social status to various religious and ethical transgressions.

__data__.text: Bharata continues to pronounce imprecations on the person responsible for Rama's exile. He calls down curses upon them for various transgressions, including failing to protect their subjects, neglecting to uphold dharma (righteous conduct), and withholding promised payments to priests. He further curses them to forget sacred teachings, never see Rama on the throne, and suffer the consequences of disrespecting elders and betraying confidences.  Bharata wishes them a life of shame, social isolation, and familial misfortune, including dying childless and before their time.  He invokes the sins associated with harming vulnerable individuals and abandoning dependents. Finally, he curses them to a livelihood of selling forbidden substances and to die a coward's death on the battlefield.

__data__.text: Bharata continues to curse the individual responsible for Rama's exile.  He decrees that this person will not have male heirs and will die prematurely and in agony.  Furthermore, they will suffer the karmic repercussions of harming the vulnerable – kings, women, children, and the elderly – and abandoning dependents.  Bharata curses them to a life of selling illicit goods like lacquer, honey, meat, iron, and poison to survive.  He wishes for them to die a coward's death fleeing from battle.  The person will become a deranged beggar, wandering and clad in rags.  They will be consumed by addiction to alcohol, women, and gambling, and ruled by lust and anger.  Their wealth will be plundered by thieves, and they will be devoid of piety, squandering resources on the undeserving. They will suffer the consequences of neglecting sacred duties like prayers and ancestral rites, and will be unable to care for their parents. They will lose their good reputation and be cast out from the company of righteous people.  Finally, Bharata curses them to abandon their mother and follow a wicked path, plagued by poverty, fever, and constant hardship.  He adds that the person will betray the hopes of those who look to them for help.  They will live a deceitful life, slandering others, impure, fearful of authority, and devoted to unrighteousness.

__data__.text: Bharata continues to pronounce imprecations upon the person responsible for Rama's exile. He declares that this individual will abandon their mother, live in poverty, suffer from fever, and endure constant hardship. They will betray the hopes of those who depend on them.  Deceitful and impure, they will live in fear of authority and embrace unrighteousness.  Bharata further curses them to neglect their wife, commit acts against Brahmins and their rituals, mistreat cows, and pursue adultery.  He calls upon them to suffer the combined consequences of polluting water and poisoning others, as well as the sin of withholding water from the thirsty. He adds that they will bear the guilt of biased observation in disputes.  Overwhelmed by grief, Bharata collapses while making these pronouncements. Kausalya consoles him, relieved that his righteousness remains intact, assuring him that his truthfulness will lead him to a virtuous afterlife. She then embraces him and weeps.

__data__.text: Bharata, overwhelmed by grief and desperately trying to prove his innocence in Rama's exile, fainted.  Kausalya comforted him, relieved that his commitment to righteousness remained unshaken. She assured him that his honesty would lead him to a virtuous afterlife.  She embraced him and wept.  Bharata continued to lament, his mind clouded by sorrow and confusion. He lay unconscious on the ground, sighing deeply.  The night passed with Bharata in this sorrowful state.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto LXXVI
Removing his father’s dead body from the vessel full of oil and cremating it
with the help of articles befitting an Emperor, and offering libations
of water to the spirit of the deceased on the bank of the Sarayd,
as enjoined by Vasistha, Bharata returns to Ayodhya

Canto LXXVI
Removing his father’s dead body from the vessel full of oil and cremating it
with the help of articles befitting an Emperor, and offering libations
of water to the spirit of the deceased on the bank of the Sarayd,
as enjoined by Vasistha, Bharata returns to Ayodhya

__data__.text: Bharata, overwhelmed with grief, is consoled by Sage Vasistha, who advises him to perform the overdue funeral rites for his father, King Dasaratha.  Heeding the sage's counsel, Bharata initiates the necessary preparations. Dasaratha's body, preserved in oil, is retrieved and laid upon a decorated bier.  Bharata laments his father's death, questioning his decision to exile Rama and Lakshmana, and expressing concern for the kingdom's future without them.  Vasistha again urges Bharata to proceed with the funeral arrangements.  Bharata complies, directing the priests and advisors to expedite the process.  The sacred fires, removed from the palace upon the king's passing, are duly honored.  The king's body is then carried to the cremation ground by grieving servants, preceded by mourners scattering offerings along the path.

Canto LXXVI

__data__.text: Following Vasistha's instructions, Bharata directed the priests and advisors to hasten the funeral preparations for King Dasaratha.  The sacred fires, previously removed from the palace, were rekindled and offered worship.  Dasaratha's body was then placed upon a bier and carried to the cremation ground by grieving servants. Mourners preceded the procession, scattering offerings of gold, silver, cloth, sandalwood, aloe, bdellium, pine, and cedar along the path.  At the cremation ground, additional fragrant substances were added to the pyre before the king's body was placed upon it.  Priests performed the necessary rites, reciting sacred texts and chanting hymns.  The king's wives, accompanied by guards, arrived at the cremation ground in palanquins and chariots.  They, along with the priests, circled the pyre before descending to the banks of the Sarayu river to offer water to Dasaratha's spirit.  Overwhelmed by grief, they wailed and lamented.  Afterwards, they returned to the city with Bharata, the counselors, and the priests, where they observed a ten-day period of mourning.

Canto LXXVII

Having performed with the help of Satrughna all the obsequial rites relating to
his deceased father on the twelfth day after his death, Bharata gifts to the
Brahmanas abundant gold and jewels as a part of the obsequies.
Overwhelmed with grief on reaching the funeral pile in order to pick
up the principal bones of the deceased on the thirteenth day, the
prince drops to the ground. Rolling on the ground, Satrughna
for his part wails in profusion. Comforted by Vasistha and
Sumantra, the two brothers urge the ministers to

expedite the work of picking up the bones

his deceased father on the twelfth day after his death, Bharata gifts to the

__data__.text: Bharata, having completed the eleventh day of mourning rituals for his deceased father, proceeded with the twelfth-day ceremonies. He generously offered Brahmanas a vast array of gifts, including gold, jewelry, food, fine clothing, precious stones, a flock of white goats, silver, and numerous cows.  He also bestowed upon them male and female servants, chariots, and grand houses for the benefit of his father in the afterlife.  On the thirteenth day, at daybreak, overcome with sorrow, Bharata began to lament at his father's cremation site.  Distressed, he cried out to his father, expressing his anguish at being left desolate after Rama's exile. He questioned his father's departure, leaving his mother Kausalya, whose support and son were now banished to the forest. The sight of the ash-covered cremation spot, red from the fire and strewn with bones, deepened Bharata's grief, causing him to collapse like a falling banner.  His ministers rushed to his aid. Witnessing Bharata's profound sorrow, Satrughna also fainted, remembering his father's loving nature. He lamented, describing their grief as a vast and menacing sea, stirred by Manthara and fueled by Kaikeyi's demands, which had swallowed them whole. He mourned the loss of his father, who had always cherished and pampered Bharata, questioning who would now provide for them as their father had.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Overwhelmed by grief, Satrughna laments his father's death, recalling his loving nature. He compares their sorrow to a vast sea, stirred by Manthara and fueled by Kaikeyi's requests for the king, which has consumed them.  He mourns the loss of his father's care, particularly for Bharata. Satrughna expresses disbelief that the earth remains intact despite the loss of such a righteous king. He declares his intention to end his life, unable to bear the pain of his father's passing and Rama's exile.  He vows not to return to Ayodhya, now desolate without them, but to seek refuge in a forest hermitage. The ministers, witnessing the brothers' intense grief, are deeply moved. Both Bharata and Satrughna collapse in despair. Vasistha, their family priest, intervenes, reminding Bharata of the thirteenth-day rituals for their father and urging him to complete them, emphasizing the inevitability of life's joys and sorrows. Sumantra consoles Satrughna similarly.  The princes rise, their grief evident. The ministers encourage them to proceed with the remaining ceremonies.

The ministers, witnessing their distress, encourage them to proceed with the remaining thirteenth-day ceremonies.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto LXXVIII

While Bharata is contemplating to undertake a journey to meet Sri Rama,
Satrughna blames Laksmana for failing to restrain his father and forcibly
prevent Sri Rama’s exile. In the meantime Bharata catches sight of
Manthara standing at the door decked with ornaments and points
to her as the root of all mischief. Satrughna thereupon pounces
upon the maidservant standing in the midst of her
companions and, seizing her by her locks, drags her
on the floor and reproaches Kaikeyi too. On the
latter pleading for mercy on Manthara’s behalf,

Bharata intervenes and Satrughna lets her go

Satrughna blames Laksmana for failing to restrain his father and forcibly

Bharata intervenes and Satrughna lets her go

__data__.text: While Bharata grieved over Rama's exile, Satrughna blamed Lakshmana for not preventing it, even if it meant defying their father.  As they spoke, Manthara, elaborately adorned in jewelry and fine clothes, appeared.  Bharata pointed to her as the cause of their troubles.  Satrughna, enraged, seized Manthara and dragged her, despite her companions' presence.  The palace guard declared Manthara responsible for Rama's exile and their father's death, leaving her to Satrughna's judgment.  Distressed, Satrughna declared Manthara should face the consequences of her actions. He grabbed her, causing her and her companions to cry out.  Frightened by Satrughna's anger, Manthara's companions fled, believing he intended to harm them all. They decided to seek refuge with Kausalya, whom they considered a reliable protector.

__data__.text: Satrughna, furious, seized Manthara, causing her and her companions to scream.  Terrified by his rage, the companions fled, believing he would harm them all, and sought refuge with the compassionate Kausalya.  Satrughna dragged Manthara across the floor, shattering her jewelry. The scattered pieces adorned the palace floor like stars in the night sky.  Still enraged, Satrughna then rebuked Kaikeyi, who had come to Manthara's defense.  Frightened by his harsh words, Kaikeyi turned to Bharata for protection. Bharata intervened, reminding Satrughna that women should not be harmed and that Rama would be displeased if they killed Kaikeyi or Manthara.  Heeding Bharata's words, Satrughna released the unconscious Manthara, who then collapsed at Kaikeyi's feet, weeping. Observing Manthara's distress, Kaikeyi comforted her.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto LXXIX

On the fourteenth day the foremost of counsellors entreat Bharata to
accept the throne. Turning down their entreaty, Bharata, who is keen
to bring Sri Rama back to Ayodhya, urges them to detail expert
engineers to construct a road, bridges and causeways etc.,
to facilitate his journey to the place of Sri Rama’s abode

Canto LXXIX

On the fourteenth day the foremost of counsellors entreat Bharata to
accept the throne. Turning down their entreaty, Bharata, who is keen
to bring Sri Rama back to Ayodhya, urges them to detail expert
engineers to construct a road, bridges and causeways etc.,
to facilitate his journey to the place of Sri Rama’s abode

__data__.text: Bharata, on the fourteenth day, addressed the king's counselors who urged him to accept the throne. He declined, stating his intent to return Rama. He then commanded the preparation of a facilitated route to Rama's location, including roads, bridges, and passages constructed by skilled engineers, guarded by those knowledgeable about difficult terrains.  He declared his intention to bring Rama back and crown him king, refusing to let his mother's ambitions be fulfilled.  He affirmed his resolve to dwell in the forest until Rama's return, emphasizing Rama's rightful place as king. The counselors praised Bharata's noble decision and wished him good fortune in his endeavor.

Canto LXXIX

On the fourteenth day the foremost of counsellors entreat Bharata to
accept the throne. Turning down their entreaty, Bharata, who is keen
to bring Sri Rama back to Ayodhya, urges them to detail expert
engineers to construct a road, bridges and causeways etc.,
to facilitate his journey to the place of Sri Rama’s abode

__data__.text: Bharata reinforces his refusal to accept the throne, declaring he will live in the forest while Rama rules. He orders the construction of a path to Rama's location, including leveling uneven terrain and assigning guards familiar with difficult passages.  The counselors respond with blessings for Bharata's devotion to Rama and his intent to restore him to the throne. Overwhelmed with joy and gratitude, Bharata sheds tears. The counselors confirm that engineers and guards, loyal to both Rama and Bharata, are already carrying out his orders to prepare the route.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary: No summary available
No summary available

__data__.text: Bharata's ministers organized a team of skilled individuals, including surveyors, architects, well-diggers, road builders, carpenters, and other laborers, to construct a wide, accessible road from Ayodhya to the Ganga river.  This large group set forth with enthusiasm and various tools. They cleared the path by removing obstacles like branches, creepers, rocks, and trees. In treeless areas, they planted trees for shade, while also trimming existing trees where necessary.  They leveled uneven ground, filled in pits and abandoned wells, bridged streams, and cleared blockages in waterways.  The workers even created reservoirs by damming rivulets. They also dug wells in dry areas, adding platforms for resting. The completed road was paved and lined with flowering trees, decorated with banners, and sprinkled with fragrant sandalwood water, creating a beautiful path reminiscent of a celestial roadway.

__data__.text: The workers dug numerous wells, complete with platforms, in arid regions to provide water. The road built for the army was paved and lined with blossoming trees, attracting many birds.  Decorated with banners and sprinkled with sandalwood-scented water, the road was as beautiful as a celestial path.  Bharata's officers selected pleasant, fruit-filled locations for his camp, adorning his chosen tent with decorations.  Experts ensured the camps were set up at auspicious times and locations. Protective mounds and moats surrounded the camps, which housed elegant tents and were interconnected by well-planned roads.  Multi-storied buildings graced the camp, making it resemble Indra's city. The road extended to the Ganga river, a cool, clear river teeming with fish, and surrounded by diverse forests.  This beautifully constructed road shone like a starlit night sky.

__data__.text: The bards and panegyrists sang Bharata's praises as the night ended and the day of the planned coronation ceremonies began.  The celebratory music of drums, conches, and other instruments, however, only deepened Bharata's grief. He stopped the music and lamented to Satrughna about their mother Kaikeyi's actions, which he felt had harmed the kingdom and caused their father's death. He mourned that the kingdom, once firmly rooted in righteousness under their father's rule, was now adrift.  He also grieved that Rama, their protector, had been exiled.  Hearing Bharata's lamentations, the women of the palace also began to wail.  Amidst this sorrow, the sage Vasistha entered the magnificent council chamber and instructed messengers to summon the Brahmins, Kshatriyas, warriors, ministers, commanders, princes, and other counselors – including Bharata, Satrughna, Yudhajit, and Sumantra – to the court for an urgent meeting.  A commotion arose as people arrived in chariots, on horseback, and atop elephants. The ministers and people greeted Bharata's arrival with enthusiasm, reminiscent of the way they had greeted King Dasaratha.

The approaching retinue created a clamor as people arrived in chariots, on horses, and atop elephants.  The ministers and citizens enthusiastically greeted Bharata's arrival, reminiscent of the reverence shown to King Dasaratha.  Bharata's presence in the court, filled with important figures, evoked the same grandeur and sense of occasion as when his father, Dasaratha, presided over the assembly.

Sage Vasistha urges Bharata to accept the kingdom bequeathed to him by his
father and elder brother. Bharata, however, scornfully declines the offer and
takes a solemn pledge to depart for the forest and try his level best to

__data__.text:
Vasistha addressed the assembled court, praising Bharata's intelligence and comparing the gathering to a moonlit night.  He urged Bharata to accept the kingship, stating that Dasaratha had bequeathed it to him and that Rama, ever dutiful, had honored his father's command.  Vasistha emphasized that the kingdom was now rightfully Bharata's, free of obstacles, and encouraged him to accept the homage of the surrounding rulers.

However, Bharata, deeply grieved, rejected the offer. He questioned how he could usurp the throne of his elder brother, Rama, a man of virtue and learning.  Bharata declared that Rama, a noble descendant of Kakutstha, was the rightful heir, comparing him to the illustrious kings Dilīpa and Nahusha.  He refused to participate in the injustice done to Rama and stated that even from the palace, he bowed to his brother in the forest.

__data__.text: Bharata passionately rejected the idea of inheriting the throne, asserting that it rightfully belonged to Rama. He argued that accepting the kingship would be a grave sin, bringing dishonor upon their lineage.  He vowed to follow Rama into the forest, recognizing Rama as the true ruler. The assembly was moved to tears by Bharata's devotion.  He declared his intention to either convince Rama to return or remain in the forest with him and Lakshmana. To this end, Bharata had already dispatched workers to prepare the route and instructed Sumantra to mobilize the army for the journey.  The citizens and generals rejoiced at the prospect of Rama's return, and the soldiers' wives eagerly urged their husbands to depart swiftly with the army, utilizing horses, carts, and chariots. Observing the army's readiness, Bharata requested Sumantra to prepare his chariot.

__data__.text: Bharata's decision to seek Rama in the forest sparked immense joy among the citizens and generals of Ayodhya.  The soldiers' wives, eager for Rama's return, urged their husbands to depart swiftly. The generals, echoing this eagerness, mobilized the army, utilizing horses, carts, and chariots. Witnessing these preparations, Bharata instructed Sumantra to ready his chariot.  Sumantra, honored by the task, quickly brought forth a chariot drawn by magnificent horses.  Bharata, resolute in his purpose, declared his intention to persuade Rama to return from the Dandaka forest.  He instructed Sumantra to assemble the army generals and prepare the forces for the journey, emphasizing his goal of bringing Rama back for the benefit of the kingdom.  Sumantra, inspired by Bharata's resolve, relayed the orders to the leading citizens, army chiefs, and Bharata's close associates.  The entire populace, including Brahmins, Kshatriyas, Vaishyas, and Sudras, enthusiastically prepared for the journey, assembling various modes of transport, from camels and chariots to donkeys, elephants, and fine horses.

Canto LXXXIII

Leaving the capital early next morning, accompanied by the family-priests,
artisans, army and the citizens, and covering a long distance, Bharata
reaches the bank of the holy Ganga at Srigaverapura ruled
by Guha and, encamping the army, breaks journey in
order to do Sraddha and Tarpana in honour of his
deceased father on the bank of the Ganga
and also to give rest to his followers

Canto LXXXIII

Leaving the capital early next morning, accompanied by the family-priests,
artisans, army and the citizens, and covering a long distance, Bharata
reaches the bank of the holy Ganga at Srigaverapura ruled
by Guha and, encamping the army, breaks journey in
order to do Sraddha and Tarpana in honour of his
deceased father on the bank of the Ganga
and also to give rest to his followers

__data__.text: At daybreak, Bharata, eager to see Rama, departed on a magnificent chariot.  Preceding him were counselors and priests in chariots, reminiscent of the sun god's own.  A grand procession followed: nine thousand decorated elephants, sixty thousand chariots filled with archers, and a hundred thousand horses with riders.  Kaikeyi, Sumitra, and Kausalya, anticipating Rama's return, rode in separate, ornate chariots.  Brahmins, excited to see Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita,  wondered aloud when they would see Rama again, comparing his presence to the sun dispelling darkness.  The citizens of Ayodhya, filled with joy, embraced and followed, discussing the anticipated reunion.  Esteemed merchants, other citizens, and skilled artisans—lapidaries, potters, weavers, weaponsmiths, fan makers, sawyers, gem piercers and polishers, ivory carvers, lime makers, perfumers, goldsmiths, blanket weavers, bath providers, masseurs, physicians, fumigators, distillers, washermen, tailors, village chiefs, dancers, and boatmen—all joined the extensive procession toward Rama.

__data__.text: Bharata's massive procession, including skilled artisans like lapidaries, potters, weavers, weaponsmiths, fan makers, sawyers, gem workers, ivory carvers, lime makers, perfumers, goldsmiths, blanket weavers, bath attendants, masseurs, physicians, fumigators, distillers, washermen, tailors, village leaders, dancers, and boatmen, journeyed from Ayodhya.  Brahmins, learned in the Vedas and known for their good conduct, followed in large numbers.  Everyone, dressed in fine clothes and adorned with fragrant sandalwood paste, traveled in various conveyances.  The joyous army, loyal to Bharata and supportive of his mission to retrieve Rama, accompanied him.  After a long journey, they arrived at the Ganga River near Sringaverapura, where Rama's friend Guha ruled.  The army rested on the riverbank, adorned with Cakrawaka birds. Bharata, observing the scene, instructed his ministers to set up camp, stating their intention to cross the river the following day. He also expressed his desire to perform rites for his deceased father in the river.  The ministers obeyed, arranging the camp according to each unit's preference.  As the well-equipped army settled along the Ganga, Bharata contemplated how to convince Rama to return.

Scenting mischief in the mind of Bharata, who was taking a large army
with him, Guha orders the ferrymen to guard the boats so as to prevent
Bharata’s men from crossing the Ganga, and himself seeks the
presence of Bharata in order to know his mind. Being satisfied

Canto LXXXIV

Scenting mischief in the mind of Bharata, who was taking a large army
with him, Guha orders the ferrymen to guard the boats so as to prevent
Bharata’s men from crossing the Ganga, and himself seeks the
presence of Bharata in order to know his mind. Being satisfied
about his good intention, he entertains Bharata and his
men with fruits and roots etc., brought by him and,
