metadata.canto_summary:
Canto CIX

Jabali explains the reasons for his advocating the theory of Nastikas to
Sri Rama, who exposes the hollowness of the ethics propounded
by the sage, dubbing it as the very antithesis of Dharma

Canto CIX

Jabali explains the reasons for his advocating the theory of Nastikas to
Sri Rama, who exposes the hollowness of the ethics propounded
by the sage, dubbing it as the very antithesis of Dharma

__data__.text: Rama, a man of unwavering strength and virtue, responded to Jabali's proposition. He argued that Jabali's advice, while seemingly pleasant, was ultimately harmful, like a disguised poison.  A person who disregards established morality and embraces sinful conduct loses respect among the wise.  One's actions, not lineage, reveal their true character.  Jabali's philosophy, Rama argued, would make a person appear noble while being ignoble, clean while being unclean, and virtuous while being flawed.  Embracing such unrighteousness disguised as piety would lead to societal chaos and a rejection of virtuous deeds.  No discerning person would respect Rama if he abandoned his dharma and became a corrupting influence.  Rama questioned whose example he should follow if he abandoned his vow, as Jabali suggested. He feared that by prioritizing personal desires, he would set a negative example, leading others astray.  True kingship, Rama declared, is rooted in unwavering honesty.  Truth is the foundation of a kingdom, and indeed, the entire world.  Sages and gods alike value truthfulness above all else, and it is the path to the highest, eternal realm.  People shun liars like serpents.  Truth is the essence of virtue and the root of all things.  It is divine, and there is no higher goal.  Charity, sacrifices, austerities, and even the Vedas themselves are founded on truth.  Therefore, one must always remain devoted to truth.  Finally, Rama explains that one's past actions determine their destiny, whether it be ruling the world, supporting their family, suffering in hell, or finding glory in heaven.

__data__.text: Rama continues to uphold the importance of truth and dharma. He states that people shun liars as they would a serpent, and that truth is the foundation of virtue and all things.  Truth is equated with God, the highest goal, and the basis of religious practices like charity, sacrifice, and even the Vedas.  One's past actions dictate their fate, be it ruling a kingdom or suffering in hell.  Therefore, Rama feels bound to keep his father's truthful promise, made with a solemn oath.  He will not break this vow due to greed, delusion, or ignorance.  He knows that gods and ancestors do not accept offerings from those who break their promises.  Rama recognizes truth as the highest virtue and accepts the burden of his exile. He chooses to renounce the unrighteous aspects of kingship, often pursued by those driven by greed and cruelty.  Rama describes how sinful deeds are conceived in thought, spoken with words, and then enacted physically.  He acknowledges that land, fame, fortune, and wise individuals all favor truth, urging devotion to it. Rama rejects Jabali's advice to take the throne, recognizing it as improper despite the seemingly logical arguments. He questions how he could abandon his exile after giving his word to his father and bringing joy to Kaikeyi. He intends to fulfill his father's command and his worldly duties while living a pure life in the forest, content with simple sustenance and devoted to the gods and ancestors.

__data__.text:
Rama, bound by his promise to his father, questions how he can go back on his word and heed Bharata's request to return, especially after Kaikeyi rejoiced at his initial vow.  He intends to maintain purity and fulfill his filial duties by living a simple life in the forest, sustaining himself with roots, fruits, and flowers while offering them to the gods and ancestors.  Rama affirms the importance of virtuous action, citing examples of Indra and the Vedic seers who attained higher realms through sacrifices and austerities.

Upon hearing Jabali's heretical arguments, Rama condemns his father for choosing such a counselor. He denounces Jabali's atheistic views, likening him to a thief who steals people's faith and comparing him to a follower of Buddha who rejects the Vedas.  Rama emphasizes the importance of following Vedic injunctions and performing virtuous deeds like those practiced by past generations and righteous sages. He highlights their devotion to truthfulness, harmlessness, and generosity.

Jabali, realizing Rama's anger, retracts his previous statement, claiming he is not a true unbeliever and only adopted that stance to dissuade Rama from his exile and to highlight Rama's own righteousness. He explains that his heretical argument was a strategic ploy to ultimately appease Rama.

__data__.text: Rama continues to extol the virtues of righteous sages, describing them as luminous, generous, harmless, and pure.  He points to figures like Vasistha as exemplars of these qualities.  Observing Rama's anger at his previous arguments, Jabali attempts to appease him.  He assures Rama that he doesn't truly adhere to atheistic beliefs and only feigned such a position to dissuade Rama from his exile.  Jabali explains that his heretical argument was a calculated strategy, first to test Rama's resolve and then to highlight Rama's steadfast righteousness by provoking a strong reaction. He now clarifies that his revised, orthodox stance is intended to soothe Rama's anger.

Canto CX

Pacifying Sri Rama, who had got angry with Jabali, Sage Vasistha
urges him to get himself consecrated as king since from
Ikswaku, the progenitor of his race, downward the eldest

son alone had ascended the throne of Ayodhya and
because he was not only the eldest of his
father’s sons, but the ablest too

father’s sons, but the ablest too

__data__.text: Sage Vasistha intervened on Jabali's behalf, explaining that Jabali, despite his unconventional arguments, understood the cycle of life and death.  His intention was simply to persuade Rama to return to Ayodhya. Vasistha then began to recount the creation of the world, starting with water and the emergence of Brahma. Brahma, also identified as Vishnu, then raised the earth and created the universe with his sons. He described the lineage from Brahma to Ikshvaku, the first king of Ayodhya, detailing the succession of kings and highlighting their virtues and accomplishments. This lineage included Kuksi, Vikuksi, Bana, Anaranya (whose reign was marked by prosperity and absence of crime), Prithu, Trisanku (who ascended to heaven), Dhundhumara, Yuvanaswa, Mandhata, and finally, Susandhi and his two sons.

__data__.text: King Triśaṅku, due to Viśvāmitra's unwavering vow, ascended to heaven.  His son, the renowned Dhundhumāra, fathered the glorious Yuvanāśva, who in turn fathered the illustrious Māndhātā.  Māndhātā's son, Susandhi, had two sons: Dhruvasandhi and Prasenajit. Dhruvasandhi's son, Bharata, a vanquisher of enemies, fathered Asita. Asita faced opposition from the Haihayas, Tālajāṅghas, and Śakabindus, and despite his efforts, was defeated and exiled, choosing a life of asceticism in the mountains.  His two pregnant wives faced contrasting fates. One, virtuous and longing for a worthy son, sought the blessings of Sage Cyavana. The other, driven by malice, attempted to poison her co-wife's unborn child.  Sage Cyavana, residing in the Himalayas, granted the first wife's wish, promising her a renowned, righteous, and powerful son who would establish a lineage.  The child was born with the poison intended to harm him, thus earning the name Sagara. King Sagara, with his sixteen thousand sons, undertook a massive excavation project, possibly related to a sacrificial ritual, instilling fear with their rapid progress. Sagara's eldest son, Asamañjas, was known for his wickedness and was consequently disowned by his father.  Asamañjas's valiant son, Anśumān, fathered Dilīpa, who in turn fathered Bhagīratha.

__data__.text: King Sagara, born with the poison meant for him in the womb, later ordered the excavation of the ocean, a feat performed by his sixteen thousand sons with alarming speed. His eldest, the wicked Asamañjas, was banished. Asamañjas's son, Anśumān, fathered Dilīpa, whose son was Bhagīratha.  From Bhagīratha descended Kakutstha, whose lineage became known as the Kakutsthas. Kakutstha's son, Raghu, gave rise to the Raghu dynasty (Raghavas). Raghu's son had several names: Pravṛddha, Puruṣādaka, Kalmāṣapāda, and Saudāsa. Kalmāṣapāda's son, Śaṅkhaṇa, despite his military prowess, perished in battle.  Śaṅkhaṇa's son was the valiant Sudarśana, whose son was Agnivarṇa. Agnivarṇa's son was Śīghraga, who fathered Maru. Maru's son, Praśuśruta, fathered the wise Ambarīṣa. Ambarīṣa's son was the steadfast Nahuṣa, whose son, Nābhaga, was known for his piety.  Nābhaga had two sons: Aja and Suvrata. Aja fathered the righteous King Daśaratha. Rama is Daśaratha's eldest son and rightful heir. Therefore, he should accept the kingship and protect the world.  The Ikshvaku tradition dictates that the eldest son inherits the throne, and Rama, being the eldest, should uphold this ancient custom and rule the prosperous kingdom like his father.

__data__.text: Vasistha emphasized the Ikshvaku tradition where the eldest son inherits the throne, stating that a younger son is never crowned while an elder brother lives. He urged Rama to honor this ancient custom of the Raghu dynasty and rule the earth, rich in resources and vast kingdoms, just as his illustrious father, Dasaratha, had done.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto CXI

Pleading the superiority of a teacher in comparison to one’s parents, Sage
Vasistha by virtue of his superiority and as a concession to Bharata, a
dependant of Sri Rama, whose vow it has been to respect the feelings of
his dependants, urges Sri Rama to grant the prayer of Bharata. Sri
Rama, however, contends that one’s parents are more worthy of
respect even than one’s teacher and is accordingly insistent on
implementing the pledge already given by him to his father. Thus
discomfited in argument, Bharata makes up his mind to undertake
a fast unto death as a last resort to exert pressure on him.
On Sri Rama’s dubbing such a step as repugnant to the
code of conduct prescribed for a Ksatriya, Bharata
requests his elder brother to allow him to remain in
exile in the forest as the latter’s proxy. Sri Rama,

however,

rules out this proposition also as

something preposterous, saying that one’s duty
is entrusted to a proxy only on the former's
incapacity, which could not be predicated
of him, and reaffirms his decision to
enter Ayodhya only after fulfilling
the pledge given to his father

__data__.text: Sage Vasistha advised Rama that a preceptor, along with one's parents, are deserving of the highest respect. He argued that while parents give birth and nurture, a teacher imparts wisdom, making them superior.  Vasistha, being both Rama's and his father's teacher, urged Rama to heed his advice and return to Ayodhya, especially given the pleas of his subjects and family, including his mother and Bharata.  However, Rama countered that parents are more worthy of respect and that he must fulfill the promise he made to his father.  Bharata, saddened by Rama's refusal, declared his intention to fast unto death at Rama's doorstep until he agreed to return. He instructed Sumantra to prepare a bed of Kuśa grass for him.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

__data__.text: Bharata, deeply saddened by Rama's unwavering decision, declared his intent to fast unto death unless Rama returned to Ayodhya. He instructed Sumantra to prepare a bed of Kuśa grass at Rama's doorstep for his vigil.  Observing this, Rama questioned Bharata's actions, reminding him that such protests were not befitting a Kshatriya.  He urged Bharata to abandon his fast and return to Ayodhya.  Even as Bharata sat in protest, he appealed to the gathered citizens and country folk to intervene on his behalf.  They, however, acknowledged the validity of Bharata's plea but pointed out Rama's steadfast adherence to his father's command, making it difficult to sway him.  Rama, overhearing this exchange, reiterated his commitment to his father's promise and urged Bharata to reconsider his extreme action.  He advised Bharata to rise, symbolically purify himself with water, and abandon his vow.  Complying, Bharata addressed the assembly, clarifying that he never sought the kingship for himself and did not endorse Rama's exile. He then proposed an alternative: If fulfilling their father's command was paramount, he would undertake the fourteen-year exile in Rama's place.

respect even than one’s teacher and is accordingly insistent on
implementing the pledge already given by him to his father. Thus
discomfited in argument, Bharata makes up his mind to undertake
a fast unto death as a last resort to exert pressure on him.
On Sri Rama’s dubbing such a step as repugnant to the
code of conduct prescribed for a Ksatriya, Bharata
requests his elder brother to allow him to remain in
exile in the forest as the latter’s proxy. Sri Rama,

however,

rules out this proposition also as

something preposterous, saying that one’s duty
is entrusted to a proxy only on the former's
incapacity, which could not be predicated
of him, and reaffirms his decision to
enter Ayodhya only after fulfilling
the pledge given to his father

__data__.text: Bharata, having composed himself, addressed the assembly, declaring he neither sought the kingship nor approved of Rama's exile. He asserted that if adhering to their father's decree necessitated a forest dwelling, he would undertake the fourteen-year exile in Rama's stead.  Rama, observing the gathering, responded that his father's decisions, made during his lifetime, were inviolable. He emphasized that sending a proxy would be inappropriate, as it implied his own inability to fulfill the commitment. He reiterated that the exile was a consequence of his father's promise to Kaikeyi, a commitment justly earned through her past service.  Rama expressed confidence in Bharata's noble character and affirmed his eventual return to rule alongside his brother.  He implored Bharata to uphold their father's honor by governing Ayodhya in his absence.
__data__.text_template: Content: {content}, Extra Chapter context for reference: {metadata_str}

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto CXII

Collected in the heavens to hear the dialogue of Bharata and Sri Rama,
heavenly beings applaud the two brothers. Having decided upon his course
of action, Sri Rama turns his face towards his hut; while pleading his
inability to protect the kingdom, now that his attempts to take Sri Rama
back to Ayodhya had been frustrated, Bharata prays to Sri Rama for
mercy once more. But when he comes to know that his elder
brother cannot be made to swerve an inch from his vow, he
places a new pair of wooden sandals decked with gold in
front of his brother and requests him to place his feet on
them. Readily acceding to his request, Sri Rama hands
over the sandals to Bharata, who takes a vow before
the former to enter the fire in case he did not return
immediately on the expiry of his term of exile. Sri
Rama confirms his vow and after embracing
Bharata and Satrughna and bidding good-
bye to all with due respect according to
the rank of each, enters his cottage

__data__.text: The sages, both visible and invisible, were astonished by the meeting of Rama and Bharata, praising their noble nature and virtuous dialogue. They urged Bharata to heed Rama's advice, reminding him of Rama's exile fulfilling his father's wishes. After expressing their desire to see Rama free from his filial obligations, the sages departed.  Pleased with their words, Rama thanked them. Bharata, trembling, pleaded with Rama to return and rule, claiming he couldn't manage the kingdom or satisfy the people alone. He emphasized everyone's dependence on Rama, comparing him to a rain cloud.  Bharata urged Rama to accept and stabilize the kingdom, asserting his capability to protect the people.  He then fell at Rama's feet, beseeching him to accept sovereignty. Rama, in turn, lifted Bharata onto his lap and prepared to respond.

__data__.text: Bharata, comparing Rama to a much-needed rain cloud, implored him to accept the kingdom and restore stability, highlighting Rama's capability to protect the people.  He fell at Rama's feet, desperately pleading with him.  Rama, affectionately, acknowledged Bharata's humility and wisdom, suggesting he could manage the kingdom with the help of his advisors.  Rama then firmly stated his resolve to honor his father's promise, declaring that even the moon losing its brightness or the Himalayas their snow would be more likely than him breaking his vow. He advised Bharata not to resent Kaikeyi, regardless of her motivations. Bharata, placing golden sandals before Rama, requested him to symbolically accept the kingdom's responsibility by stepping onto them.  Rama obliged, then handed the sandals to Bharata.  Bharata vowed to live as an ascetic, awaiting Rama's return, and to enter fire if Rama did not return after fourteen years.  Rama accepted this vow, embraced Bharata and Satrughna, and advised them to treat Kaikeyi with respect.

__data__.text: Bharata, placing Rama's golden sandals on his head, vowed to live a simple life, consuming only fruits and roots, dressed in bark and matted hair, residing outside the city until Rama's return.  He solemnly swore to enter fire if Rama didn't return after fourteen years. Rama, with tears in his eyes, accepted Bharata's vow, embraced him and Satrughna, and reminded them to treat Kaikeyi with respect. He entrusted Bharata to uphold his duty. Bharata then carried the sandals respectfully, placing them atop an elephant. After bidding farewell to the assembled elders, advisors, subjects, and his brothers, Rama returned to his dwelling, his mothers overcome with grief and unable to speak. Rama, also weeping, greeted his mothers before entering his hut.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto CXIII

Departed back for Ayodhya alongwith Sage Vasistha and others, Bharata
pays his respects on the way to Sage Bharadwaja and, apprising him
of what had happened at Citrakuta and also of the pair of wooden
sandals that he had received from Sri Rama as the latter's
representative, and proceeding further, crosses the rivers
Ganga and Yamuna alongwith his followers. Departing
from Srigaverapura, he catches sight of Ayodhya
and points out to Sumantra its gloomy appearance

pays his respects on the way to Sage Bharadwaja and, apprising him
of what had happened at Citrakuta and also of the pair of wooden

__data__.text: Bharata, joyful, placed the sandals on his head and mounted the chariot with Satrughna.  The esteemed advisors, including Vasistha, Vamadeva, and Jabali, led the procession.  They journeyed eastward, keeping the Mandakini River and Citrakuta mountain on their right.  Bharata and his army observed various beautiful minerals along the way as they traveled alongside the mountain.  Soon, they reached the hermitage of Sage Bharadwaja.  Bharata respectfully dismounted the chariot and greeted the sage.  Bharadwaja warmly welcomed him and inquired about his meeting with Rama and whether his mission was successful. Bharata, a righteous individual, explained to the wise sage that he was returning to Ayodhya with Rama's sandals, having been granted permission to depart.  He recounted how Rama, though pleased by their pleas to return, remained resolute in his commitment to fulfill his father's pledge of fourteen years of exile.  Rama then respectfully addressed Vasistha, and offered the gold-adorned sandals to Bharata, entrusting him to govern Ayodhya in his stead.  Following Vasistha's instructions, Rama stood on the sandals facing east, allowing Bharata to receive them. Bharadwaja, upon hearing this positive account from Bharata, offered a comforting response.

__data__.text: Bharata, having received Rama's sandals, departed from Bharadwaja's hermitage.  The sage blessed Bharata, commending his noble character and devotion to virtue.  Bharata then respectfully took his leave and journeyed towards Ayodhya with his entourage. The army traveled by chariot, cart, horseback, and elephant, retracing their path.  They crossed the Yamuna and Ganga rivers. After passing through Sringaverapura, Bharata caught sight of Ayodhya.  Overcome with sorrow at seeing the city without his father and Rama, Bharata remarked to Sumantra, his charioteer, that Ayodhya appeared desolate, joyless, and lifeless.

Valmiki portrays the gloomy appearance of Ayodhya with the help
of a number of similes. Bharata enters his father’s palace
with a heavy heart, speaking to Sumantra about the
sorry spectacle which Ayodhya presented
at the moment

Canto CXIV

Valmiki portrays the gloomy appearance of Ayodhya with the help
of a number of similes. Bharata enters his father’s palace
with a heavy heart, speaking to Sumantra about the
sorry spectacle which Ayodhya presented

__data__.text: Bharata entered Ayodhya, finding the city eerily quiet and desolate.  The closed houses, lack of activity, and presence of only cats and owls made it feel like nighttime.  Ayodhya appeared diminished, like a star fallen from the sky or a fire extinguished with milk. It resembled a defeated army, its banners torn and warriors slain, or a once-roaring ocean wave now stilled.  The city seemed abandoned, like an altar after a ceremony or a cowpen without a bull.  Its former splendor was gone, like a necklace stripped of its jewels. The once-bustling markets were now quiet, the streets unswept and filled with debris.  Ayodhya resembled a tavern, its patrons gone and its good wine replaced with broken vessels.  Even the water storage shed was collapsed and broken.

__data__.text: Bharata observes Ayodhya's further decline, comparing the city to a blossoming creeper scorched by wildfire, a sky obscured by clouds, and a deserted tavern littered with broken vessels and devoid of fine wine.  He notes the broken water storage shed, its platform destroyed and covered with debris. Ayodhya also resembles a broken bowstring cast to the ground and a warhorse felled in battle.  Turning to Sumantra, Bharata laments the absence of music, pleasant fragrances, and the sounds of chariots and joyful citizens. He notes that the city's usual celebratory atmosphere has vanished due to Rama's exile, and its former glory has departed with him.

__data__.text: Bharata continues to lament Ayodhya's desolate state, comparing the city to a joyless festival and a moonless night.  He longs for Rama's return, envisioning the joy it would bring, like rain clouds in summer.  The once bustling streets, filled with elegantly dressed people, are now empty and lifeless.  Entering his father's palace, Bharata finds it as desolate as a lion's den without its majestic inhabitant. The palace's inner chambers, once vibrant, are now gloomy and disordered, like a sunless day mourned by the gods.  Overwhelmed by grief, Bharata weeps despite his usual composure.

__data__.text: Bharata, deeply saddened by recent events, informed his advisors, including Vasistha, of his intention to reside in Nandigrama and endure his grief there while awaiting Rama's return to rule.  His advisors praised Bharata's loyalty and devotion to his brother.  Bharata then ordered his chariot prepared and, accompanied by Satrughna, departed for Nandigrama.  The Brahmanas, led by Vasistha, preceded them, followed by the army and citizens, though Bharata had not summoned them.  Bharata, carrying Rama's sandals reverently upon his head, arrived at Nandigrama.  There, he addressed his advisors, presenting the kingdom and the sandals as sacred trusts bestowed upon him by Rama, emphasizing their significance for the prosperity and safety of the realm.

__data__.text: Upon reaching Nandigrama, Bharata reverently carried Rama's sandals on his head.  He addressed his advisors, presenting the sandals and the kingdom as a sacred trust.  Bharata declared his intention to uphold this trust until Rama's return, emphasizing the sandals' symbolic representation of Rama's authority.  He vowed to personally return the sandals to Rama upon his brother's return.  Bharata then committed himself to serving Rama after relinquishing the kingdom.  He declared that returning the kingdom and sandals would absolve him of any perceived guilt associated with Rama's exile. Bharata expressed that witnessing Rama's coronation and the people's joy would bring him greater happiness than holding the kingdom himself. Despite his sorrow, Bharata, residing in Nandigrama, diligently administered the kingdom alongside his council, adopting an ascetic lifestyle.  He eagerly awaited Rama's return, upholding his vow and demonstrating his devotion by symbolically crowning the sandals and conducting all affairs of state as if under their direction.  Every matter and valuable offering was presented to the sandals before any action was taken.

Canto CXVI

Learning from outward signs that the ascetics who lived in the vicinity of
Sri Rama were anxious to shift to some other forest, the latter asks
them why they wished to leave the place. Being told in reply
that they were being molested by Raksasas under the
leadership of Khara, he gives his consent and,
sending them away with great respect,
retires to his own hermitage

__data__.text: The ascetics living near Rama grew anxious and perturbed after Bharata's departure.  They whispered amongst themselves, glancing at Rama with worried expressions.  Concerned, Rama questioned the eldest ascetic, fearing some fault in his own conduct, Lakshmana's, or Sita's. The sage reassured Rama that Sita, ever devoted to goodness, could not be the cause. The ascetics' distress stemmed from the threat of Khara, Ravana's brother, a cruel and powerful Rakshasa who had driven out the ascetics of Janasthana.  Since Rama's arrival, Khara's demons had tormented the ascetics, appearing in terrifying forms and subjecting them to horrifying ordeals.

__data__.text: Khara, Ravana's cruel and powerful brother, has been terrorizing the ascetics living in Janasthana. Since Rama's arrival, the demonic harassment has intensified, with Khara's forces appearing in frightening forms and tormenting the ascetics with horrific acts. They contaminate sacred objects, disrupt rituals, and even hide within the hermitage to prey on the unsuspecting.  Fearing for their safety, the ascetics urged Rama to relocate, suggesting a safer, resource-rich woodland nearby. Concerned for the ascetics' well-being, Rama was unable to dissuade them.  The leader of the ascetics, after expressing his concerns and bidding farewell to Rama, departed with his followers.  Rama respectfully escorted them, receiving their blessings before returning to his now-deserted hermitage, though the ascetics' thoughts remained with him.

by her husband, Anasuya gives her instructions

on the duties of a devoted wife

__data__.text: Rama, after the ascetics departed, decided to leave Citrakūṭa for several reasons, including the painful memories of his family and the lingering impact of Bharata's army's encampment.  He then journeyed to the hermitage of Sage Atri, who warmly welcomed Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita.  Atri introduced his wife, Anasuya, a revered ascetic known for her piety,  miraculous deeds (including creating sustenance during a drought and diverting the Ganga river), and spiritual accomplishments.  Atri urged Sita to meet Anasuya, describing her as worthy of reverence and eternally peaceful.  Rama concurred with Atri, advising Sita to seek out the hermitess for her own benefit.

Sri Rama leaves that mountain and, reaching the hermitage of Sage
Atri, greets the aforesaid Rsi. Glorifying the ascetic life of his wife,

on the duties of a devoted wife

__data__.text:
Sage Atri continued to extol Anasuya's virtues to Rama, highlighting her asceticism and compassion. He described how Anasuya, through her dedicated practices, had brought forth sustenance during a decade-long drought and even diverted the flow of the Ganga river.  He recounted her extensive thousand-year-long austerities and her ability to remove obstacles for other sages. He even mentioned an instance where, for a divine purpose, she compressed ten nights into one. Atri then urged Sita to visit the elderly Anasuya, emphasizing her worthiness of respect and her perpetually peaceful nature.

Rama concurred, advising Sita to seek Anasuya's wisdom for her own well-being. Sita respectfully listened to Rama's counsel and approached the venerable hermitess.  Anasuya appeared frail, her age evident in her wrinkled skin and gray hair, her body trembling like a banana tree in a strong wind.  Sita calmly greeted Anasuya, introducing herself and inquiring about her health. Anasuya commended Sita for her devotion to righteousness, especially for following her exiled husband, Rama, and forsaking her royal comforts.  She emphasized that a woman who cherishes her husband finds true prosperity regardless of their circumstances. Anasuya proclaimed that a noble woman regards her husband as her highest deity, regardless of his flaws or worldly possessions.  She believed a husband to be a woman's greatest ally and the source of her fulfillment. Anasuya contrasted this with women driven by selfish desires, who disregard virtue and bring shame upon themselves.

__data__.text: Anasuya continued her advice to Sita, explaining that for noble women, a husband is their highest deity, regardless of his character or wealth. She stressed that a husband is a woman's greatest friend and protector, comparable to the hard-earned fruits of spiritual practice.  However, she warned against women who, driven by selfish desires, disregard virtue and disrespect their husbands, ultimately leading to their downfall. Anasuya praised Sita as a virtuous woman who understands right and wrong, assuring her that such devotion would lead to a blessed afterlife. Finally, Anasuya encouraged Sita to remain devoted to Rama, treating him with utmost respect and supporting his righteous actions, promising that this path would bring her both fame and spiritual merit.

Canto CXVIII
At the end of her dialogue with Sita, Anasiya requests her to ask a boon
and, highly pleased with her, the hermitess bestows heavenly flowers

and ornaments on her. Urged by Anasiya, She relates to

__data__.text: Sita, showing respect, responds to Anasuya's advice, stating that a wife's devotion to her husband is well known to her and she considers her husband as her guru.  She emphasizes that even if Rama were flawed, her duty would remain unchanged.  Sita praises Rama's virtues – his compassion, self-control, unwavering love, and righteousness. She describes Rama's respectful treatment of all his mothers, including his stepmothers.  Sita recalls the lessons imparted by her mother and mother-in-law about a wife's duties, particularly emphasizing service to her husband as the highest form of devotion. She cites Savitri and Rohini as examples of devoted wives revered in heaven.  Anasuya, delighted with Sita's words, expresses her affection and, citing her spiritual power gained through religious practices, invites Sita to ask for a boon.

Canto CXVIII
At the end of her dialogue with Sita, Anasiya requests her to ask a boon
and, highly pleased with her, the hermitess bestows heavenly flowers

and ornaments on her. Urged by Anasiya, She relates to
the hermitess the story of her marriage

__data__.text: Sita explains that women devoted to their husbands are revered in the celestial realm due to their virtuous actions.  Anasuya, overjoyed by Sita's words, affectionately smells Sita's head and offers her a boon, citing her spiritual power. Sita humbly states that Anasuya's grace has already fulfilled everything.  Anasuya, further pleased by Sita's humility, offers her celestial garments, ornaments, cosmetics, and a fragrant unguent, assuring Sita that these gifts will enhance her beauty and remain eternally pristine.  Anasuya predicts that adorned with these gifts, Sita will grace Rama as Lakshmi graces Vishnu. Sita gratefully accepts the gifts.  Anasuya then requests Sita to narrate the story of her marriage to Rama, which she had heard involved a test of strength.  Sita agrees to recount the tale to Anasuya, beginning with a description of her father, Janaka, the virtuous and just king of Mithila.

Canto CXVIII
At the end of her dialogue with Sita, Anasiya requests her to ask a boon
and, highly pleased with her, the hermitess bestows heavenly flowers

and ornaments on her. Urged by Anasiya, She relates to
the hermitess the story of her marriage

__data__.text: King Janaka learned that Sita had been won by Rama in a contest of strength. Anasuya expresses her eagerness to hear the full story from Sita. Sita agrees and begins her narration. She describes her father, Janaka, the righteous king of Mithila, and recounts her miraculous birth from the earth while he was plowing a field.  Astonished, the childless Janaka accepted her as his daughter, a decision affirmed by a divine voice.  Janaka prospered after Sita's arrival.  Sita was raised with loving care by Janaka's queen.  As Sita reached marriageable age, Janaka became anxious about finding a suitable husband for her, aware of the difficulties faced by fathers of unmarried daughters.  His concern was compounded by Sita's unusual birth.  He eventually decided to hold a swayamvara, allowing Sita to choose her own husband.  Sita mentions the sacred bow and inexhaustible quivers of arrows given to Janaka's ancestor by the god Varuna.

Canto CXVIII
At the end of her dialogue with Sita, Anasiya requests her to ask a boon
and, highly pleased with her, the hermitess bestows heavenly flowers

and ornaments on her. Urged by Anasiya, She relates to
the hermitess the story of her marriage

__data__.text: King Janaka, worried about finding a suitable husband for Sita, given her unusual birth, was deeply anxious. He realized he couldn't find a worthy match for her on his own.  He decided to hold a swayamvara, allowing Sita to choose her husband.  Janaka possessed a sacred, immensely heavy bow and inexhaustible quivers, gifted by the god Varuna to his ancestor. This bow was so heavy that no king could even lift it, let alone string it. Janaka announced that whoever could string the bow would marry Sita.  Many kings tried but failed even to lift the bow. After a long time, Rama arrived with Lakshmana and Viswamitra.  They were received with honor by Janaka. Viswamitra requested Janaka to show Rama the divine bow.  Rama easily lifted, strung, and in the process, broke the bow, producing a thunderous sound.  Janaka, true to his word, prepared to give Sita to Rama in marriage.  However, Rama, seemingly unaware of his father's wishes regarding the marriage, did not immediately accept Sita.

Canto CXVIII
At the end of her dialogue with Sita, Anasiya requests her to ask a boon
and, highly pleased with her, the hermitess bestows heavenly flowers

Canto CXVIII
At the end of her dialogue with Sita, Anasiya requests her to ask a boon
and, highly pleased with her, the hermitess bestows heavenly flowers

and ornaments on her. Urged by Anasiya, She relates to
the hermitess the story of her marriage

__data__.text: Sita continued her story to Anasuya.  With immense strength, Rama effortlessly bent the mighty bow of Shiva and strung it. As he drew the bow, it snapped in two with a deafening roar like thunder. King Janaka, true to his word, prepared to give Sita to Rama, even bringing water to wash the groom's feet, a traditional gesture of respect. However, Rama, seemingly unaware of his father, King Dasaratha's, desire for this union, did not immediately accept Sita.  Subsequently, King Dasaratha was invited to Janaka's kingdom, where Sita was formally given in marriage to the self-realized Rama.  At the same time, Sita's younger sister, Urmila, was given to Lakshmana.  Thus, Sita affirmed her devoted and pious marriage to Rama, the bravest of men.

metadata.canto_summary:
Canto CXIX

Glorifying the evening shades, that had now fallen, Anasuya sends away Sita,
decked with the ornaments bestowed on her by herself, to the presence of
Sri Rama. To the latter, who was agreeably surprised to behold Sita
resplendent with heavenly jewels, she relates how she received the
jewels as a loving gift from the hermitess. Accepting the hospitality
of the hermits and staying with them for the night, Sri Rama
asks leave of them in the morning to proceed further
and, following the route shown by the hermits,
the princely party enters the Dandaka forest

Canto CXIX

Glorifying the evening shades, that had now fallen, Anasuya sends away Sita,
decked with the ornaments bestowed on her by herself, to the presence of
Sri Rama. To the latter, who was agreeably surprised to behold Sita
resplendent with heavenly jewels, she relates how she received the
jewels as a loving gift from the hermitess. Accepting the hospitality
of the hermits and staying with them for the night, Sri Rama
asks leave of them in the morning to proceed further
and, following the route shown by the hermits,
the princely party enters the Dandaka forest

__data__.text: As Sita finished her story, the wise Anasuya embraced her affectionately. Anasuya praised Sita's eloquent narration of her self-choice marriage. However, as the sun set, signaling the arrival of night and the sounds of birds returning to their nests, Anasuya noticed the hermits returning from their ablutions, preparing for the evening fire offering. The surrounding trees, though sparsely leaved, appeared dense in the fading light, and the directions became indistinguishable.  Anasuya noted the deer resting peacefully while nocturnal predators began to roam.  With the starlit night fully upon them and the moon shining brightly, Anasuya gave Sita permission to return to Rama, expressing her pleasure with Sita’s sweet words.  Anasuya then offered Sita heavenly garments and jewels, asking her to adorn herself with them. Sita, looking radiant, adorned herself and respectfully bowed before Anasuya before going to meet Rama.  Rama was delighted to see Sita adorned with the beautiful gifts given by the hermitess. Sita then recounted to Rama the details of Anasuya's generous gift of clothing, jewels, and a garland.  Both Rama and Lakshmana were overjoyed at the extraordinary hospitality Sita had received.

__data__.text: Adorned in Anasuya's gifts, Sita respectfully took leave of the hermitess and returned to Rama.  Rama was pleased to see Sita wearing the beautiful ornaments and clothing. Sita then described Anasuya's generous gifts of clothing, jewels, and a garland. Both Rama and Lakshmana were delighted by the exceptional hospitality shown to Sita.  Honored by the hermits, Rama spent the night there. The next morning, after performing their ablutions, Rama and Lakshmana prepared to depart and sought directions from the forest-dwelling ascetics. The hermits warned them about the dangers of the forest, describing it as infested with various monstrous creatures and wild animals that preyed on humans, particularly those who were careless or negligent. They advised Rama to follow a specific path used by the sages, which would offer the safest passage through the difficult terrain. After receiving these instructions and blessings from the hermits, Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana entered the dense forest, much like the sun breaking through a mass of clouds.

Urged by the Emperor to solemnize the installation of Sri Rama as Prince
Regent, Vasistha enjoins in his turn DaSaratha’s ministers, Sumantra
and others, to get ready all requisites for the ceremony. Sent by
Sumantra, Sri Rama in the meantime calls on his father, who
announces his decision to install him as Prince Regent and
also tenders some opportune advice to him. Sri Rama's
chums break the news to Sri Rama’s mother,

Kausalya; treasuring in his mind the
exhortation of his father and bowing
low to him, Sri Rama returns to
his own apartments

Canto VII

Beholding the festivities and finding Kausalya bestowing large gifts of
money on the Brahmanas, Manthara, a hunchback hereditary maid-
servant of Queen Kaikeyi, who had accidentally ascended the roof
of the palace, inquires of Sri Rama’s erstwhile nurse the occasion

for the festivities and, on being told of Sri Rama’s forthcoming
installation, feels enraged and, approaching Kaikeyi,
instigates her to stop the installation. Kaikeyi,
on the other hand, feels rejoiced over the
news and gifts her a jewel as a
token of her pleasure

Canto IX

Kaikeyi, whose heart was poisoned by the malicious gossip of Manthara as
aforesaid, takes a vow to see that Rama is sent into exile and Bharata
installed as Prince Regent, and asks Manthara herself how to
secure that consummation. Manthara tells her how in the
course of a conflict between gods and demons, in which
Dasaratha’s help was enlisted by the gods, the queen,
who had accompanied her husband to the field of
operations and had rendered valuable assistance
to him at a critical juncture, was offered a couple
of boons, which she had kept in abeyance.

Manthara now asks Kaikeyi to demand the
banishment of Rama and the installation of
Bharata against the two promised boons.

Kaikeyi accordingly throws away her
ornaments and lies down on the bare
floor in the sulking-room in
a sullen mood

Bharata against the two promised boons.

Kaikeyi accordingly throws away her
ornaments and lies down on the bare
floor in the sulking-room in
a sullen mood

Canto IX

Kaikeyi, whose heart was poisoned by the malicious gossip of Manthara as
aforesaid, takes a vow to see that Rama is sent into exile and Bharata
installed as Prince Regent, and asks Manthara herself how to
secure that consummation. Manthara tells her how in the
course of a conflict between gods and demons, in which
Dasaratha’s help was enlisted by the gods, the queen,
who had accompanied her husband to the field of
operations and had rendered valuable assistance
to him at a critical juncture, was offered a couple
of boons, which she had kept in abeyance.

Manthara now asks Kaikeyi to demand the
banishment of Rama and the installation of
Bharata against the two promised boons.

Kaikeyi accordingly throws away her
ornaments and lies down on the bare
floor in the sulking-room in
a sullen mood

Canto X

Having told off Sumantra and others to get together necessaries for the
installation of Sri Rama, Dagaratha calls on Kaikeyi to break the happy
news to her. Not finding her in her apartments, however, he makes
inquiries from the portress, who tells him of her presence in
the sulking-chamber. The Emperor calls on her there and,
lifting her up, cajoles her

Canto XI

Egged on by Kaikeyi to grant her desire, DaSaratha gives his word of
honour to her to that effect. Invoking the presence of gods as
witnesses and reminding the Emperor of what took place
during the conflict of gods and demons, Kaikeyi asks of
him the two boons promised by him in the shape of
exiling Sri Rama for a period of fourteen years
and installing Bharata as Prince Regent

Canto XII

Extolling the virtues of Sri Rama and showing him undeserving of exile, the
Emperor endeavours to dissuade Kaikeyi from her pertinacity in sending
Sri Rama into exile. Quoting the examples of Harigcandra and others,
Kaikeyi, however, redoubles her insistence. The Emperor for his
part rebukes her in harsh words and goes the length of falling
at her feet in order to bring her round but in vain

Canto XIV

Citing other instances of the Emperor's fidelity to truth and threatening to lay
down her life in the event of his not carrying out her wishes, Kaikeyi
insists on his sending Sri Rama into exile at once and does not
desist from her purpose even when railed at by her husband.

In the meantime Sumantra makes his appearance in the
gynaeceum and, extolling the Emperor, reminds him of
his intention to install Sri Rama as Prince Regent
and eventually leaves the gynaeceum in
order to summon Sri Rama at the
instance of the Emperor

Canto XX

Even as Sri Rama issued forth from the palace of Queen Kaikeyi, the inmates
of the gynaeceum burst into a piteous wail, extolling the prince’s virtues.
Mother Kausalya embraces and pronounces her benedictions on Sri
Rama as the latter approaches her and falls at her feet. On being
apprised of the circumstances that had brought him there, she
falls to the ground overwhelmed with grief, and weeps
bitterly expressing her deep sorrow

Canto XXI

Consoling Kausalya in her grief over the impending exile of Sri Rama, Prince
Laksmana opposes the idea of Sri Rama’s leaving for the forest and,
censuring Dasaratha, makes up his mind to accompany his
eldest brother. Kausalya too deters Sri Rama from going
into exile, branding Kaikeyi’s command as unjust.

Sri Rama, however, justifies the command on the
ground of its being countenanced by the
Emperor and requests his mother to grant
him leave and perform auspicious
rites connected with his departure

Canto XXIII

Enraged to hear the exhortation of Sri Rama, Laksmana urges in reply that
the word of their father was worth ignoring inasmuch as it was divorced
from righteousness and, further emphasizing the predominance
of personal effort over destiny, persuades Sri Rama to take
up arms against those who interfere with his installation
and occupy the throne of Ayodhya by force. Sri Rama,
however, pacifies Laksmana and impresses on him
the imperative necessity of their carrying out
the command of their father

and serve him, she consents to Sri Rama’s
departure to the forest

Canto XXXII

Having received gifts of jewels and ornaments from Sri Rama and Sita,
Suyajfia invokes divine blessings on the couple. Enjoined by Sri
Rama, Laksmana then bestows silk costumes and ornaments
etc., on the sons of Sage Agastya and others. Urged by his
wife, a sage, Trijata by name, approaches Sri Rama and
seeks riches from him. Sri Rama thereupon bestows
on him thousands of cows and distributes
his remaining wealth among other
Brahmanas as well as among his
relations and dependants

Suyajfia invokes divine blessings on the couple. Enjoined by Sri
Rama, Laksmana then bestows silk costumes and ornaments
etc., on the sons of Sage Agastya and others. Urged by his
wife, a sage, Trijata by name, approaches Sri Rama and
seeks riches from him. Sri Rama thereupon bestows
on him thousands of cows and distributes
his remaining wealth among other
Brahmanas as well as among his
relations and dependants

Canto XXXIII

Having distributed his immense riches among the Brahmanas and others,
Sri Rama, accompanied by Sita and Laksmana, proceeds to his father’s
gynaeceum to take leave of him. Remaining unruffled even on
hearing en route the diverse talks of the citizens gathered
here and there, with melancholy writ large on their faces,
the royal trio reach their destination and urge Sumantra
to apprise their royal father of their arrival

Canto XXXIV

Apprised of Sri Rama’s arrival by Sumantra, Dagaratha commands him to
usher in Sri Rama with his consort. Seeing the Emperor fall unconscious
at the very sight of the heir-apparent, the ladies of the royal household
start wailing piteously. On Sri Rama’s soliciting his permission to
retire to the woods, the king enjoins him to take his father
captive and ascend the throne. Sri Rama in his turn
consoles his father by assuring him that he has no
hankering for royal fortune and that to him
obedience to his father’s command is of
utmost importance. Dasaratha, however,
falls unconscious again at the very thought
of his separation from Sri Rama and
Sumantra as well as all the ladies of
the gynaeceum follow suit

Canto XXXVI

DaSaratha instructs Sumantra to take a detachment of the army and the
exchequer alongwith Sri Rama, to which Kaikeyi objects and insists
on his being sent into exile without any resources on the analogy
of Asamafija, son of the king’s forefather, Sagara. Another minister
of the king, Siddhartha by name, who was present there, opposes
Kaikeyi and urges that the analogy of Asamafija, who was a
perverse lad, could not be applied in the case of Sri Rama,
who possessed an ideal character and deserved in every
way to be installed in the office of Prince Regent. In
the event of Kaikeyi vetoing the proposal, the king
threatens to accompany the heir-apparent

Canto XXXVII

Sri Rama urges his servants to fetch the bark of trees for being used as his
wearing apparel. At this Kaikeyi herself brings them the desired dress,
which both Sri Rama and Laksmana cover themselves with, taking off

their princely robes. Sita, however, finds it difficult to wear the
bark supplied to her and ultimately puts it on over her own
dress with the help of Sri Rama despite the remonstrances
of Vasistha, who severely castigates Kaikeyi
for her cruelty in exiling them

dressed as a hermitess, DaSaratha reproaches Kaikeyi. While departing,
Sri Rama entreats his father to take care of his mother

Canto XXXIX

Sent away by Dagaratha, Sumantra gets a chariot ready to take Sri Rama
and his party to the forest and Sita adorns herself with jewels
brought by the Chancellor of the Exchequer. Kausalya
tenders opportune advice to her daughter-in-law and
the latter bows to it. Sri Rama comforts Kausalya
and offers apology to his other mothers,
who burst into a wail

Canto XL

Going clockwise round DaSaratha (as a token of respect), Sita, Rama and
Laksmana greet him. Accompanied by Sita, Sri Rama salutes Kausalya.
Laksmana too hails Kausalya first and then his own mother, Sumitra.
Sumitra for her part tenders salutary advice to her son. The princes
and the princess having mounted the chariot, Sumantra flicks
the horses. The citizens that had assembled, closely follow
the chariot; but unable to keep pace with its swift
movement, they return desolate. Dasaratha too
runs after the chariot alongwith Kausalya
and others, but being unable to walk
begins to totter and stops short
on the road yielding to the
remonstrances of his
wise ministers

Canto XLI

The ladies of the royal gynaeceum bewail the exile of Sri Rama and others and
the citizens of Ayodhya too are reduced to a miserable plight

Canto XLI

The ladies of the royal gynaeceum bewail the exile of Sri Rama and others and
the citizens of Ayodhya too are reduced to a miserable plight

Canto XLII

Sri Rama having departed for the forest in an exceptionally swift-going
chariot, Dasaratha vainly seeks to cover the intervening distance on foot.
The very dust raised by the chariot having disappeared after a while,
the Emperor feels doubly disconsolate and drops to the ground.
When Kaikeyi comes forward to support him, he scolds her and
asks her not to touch his person. Kausalya then lifts him up
and persuades him to return. His personal attendants
take him to the latter's apartments and, seeing him
plunged in grief, Kausalya seats herself beside
him and begins to lament in various ways

Canto XLV

When the citizens that followed Sri Rama in his journey to the forest
refuse to return even when pleaded by Sri Rama in many ways,
unable as they were to bear separation from him, Sri Rama, with

Sita and Laksmana, gets down from his chariot and begins to
walk. The citizens try to deflect him from his course and
persuade him to return, but in vain. At the close of
the day they all reach the bank of the Tamasa

Canto XLVI

Having reached the bank of the Tamasa and thinking of the plight of the
people of Ayodhya, Sri Rama lays himself down on a bed of leaves
bewailing the lot of his parents and feeling reassured by the thought
of Bharata’s noble qualities; while Laksmana opens his dialogue
with Sumantra on the divine excellences of Sri Rama. Waking
up in the meantime, Sri Rama urges the charioteer-minister to
drive the chariot in such a way as to put the citizens, that
had accompanied them, off the scent and lead them to
think that the chariot had turned back towards
Ayodhya instead of proceeding towards the forest.

He then mounts the chariot alongwith Sita and
Laksmana and presses on to the forest

Canto XLVII

The citizens that had followed Sri Rama in his journey to the forest woke
to find Sri Rama and his party gone and begin to reproach
themselves. Overcome with grief they hunt up the
tracks of his chariot; but unable to find them,
they helplessly return to Ayodhya
in utter despondency

his birth-place and, sending back the people hailing from the countryside,
who had come to see him, and crossing the frontiers of Kosala, Sri
Rama reaches the bank of the holy Ganga. Alighting from the
chariot under an Ingudi tree standing on the bank, he goes
forward to meet Guha, the chief of the Nisadas, who
had come to meet him. Nay, worshipping the evening

twilight and
and drink),

taking water
the prince

only (for food
lies down on the

ground to repose for the night; while
Sumantra, Guha and Laksmana

Canto LI

Expressing his readiness to guard the Crown prince and his consort,
keeping awake the whole night, Guha importunes Laksmana to repose.
Reminding Guha of Sri Rama’s greatness, Laksmana, however, tells
him that even though the duty of guarding his princely brother and
his consort could as well be entrusted to Guha, he felt that he
did not deserve to lie down in the presence of his elder
brother and sister-in-law and preferred to remain awake.
Expressing grief for his royal father and loving mothers
he therefore spends the night talking with Guha

Canto LII

While about to step into the boat brought by Guha’s men and asked by Guha
if he could be of any further use to the prince, Sri Rama enjoins him to
obey the Emperor. Importuned by Sumantra to take him as a personal
attendant to the forest, the prince declines his loving offer and,
expostulating with him, sends him back to Ayodhya. Entangling
their locks into a thick mass with the milk of a banyan tree,
procured by Guha, Sri Rama and Laksmana with Sita
get into the boat. On reaching the middle of the
stream, Sita offers prayers to Mother Ganga
(the deity presiding over the stream) and
after crossing the river all the three halt

for the night at the foot of a tree

Canto LIII

Apprehending trouble for Kausalya and others at the hands of Kaikeyi, Sri
Rama, who was seated at the foot of a banyan tree and was a past
master in the art of persuasion, persuades Laksmana to the best
of his ability to return to Ayodhya. Laksmana, however, pleads
inability to survive in his absence and does not budge an
inch from his resolution to stay with his eldest brother.

Sri Rama, therefore, yields and sets his seal to

his continuing with him during his exile

Canto LIV

Set out on his journey for the Dandaka forest with Sita and Laksmana, Sri
Rama reaches at dusk the hermitage of Sage Bharadwaja in the vicinity
of the confluence of the holy Ganga and Yamuna rivers. Paying
due honours to Sri Rama and his party, the sage recommends
Citrakita as the fittest place for him to sojourn in.

Spending the night in discourses on various
topics with him, the sage grants him
leave early next morning to
depart for Citrakita

Canto LV

Sage Bharadwaja tells Sri Rama and Laksmana, even as they set out
on their journey to CitrakUta, the route by which they should
proceed. Accompanied by Sita, Sri Rama and Laksmana
cross the Yamuna on a raft prepared by themselves.

In the evening they halt on the bank of the
Yamuna alongwith Sita, who is rejoiced
to get fruits and blossoms of her liking

Canto LVI

Moving further next morning and rejoicing on the way to see the loveliness
of the forest, the party reaches CitrakUta and enters the hermitage of
Valmiki. Making up his mind to sojourn there with the permission
of the sage, Sri Rama gets Laksmana to erect a hut of
leaves for themselves and, worshipping the deities
presiding over the structure, they solemnly enter

their abode at a propitious hour

Canto LVII

Having been told of Sri Rama’s departure for Citrakuta by the spies of Guha
and taking leave of the latter, Sumantra drives back to Ayodhya. Entering
the royal gynaeceum, he submits to the Emperor what he reported
earlier to the citizens who followed his chariot. Dasaratha
and Kausalya fall into a swoon to hear about
Sri Rama’s departure for Citrakita and
all the inmates of the gynaeceum
burst into a wail from agony

deserve the hardships they were undergoing in the forest, the Emperor

urges Sumantra to deliver their parting message and the charioteer
proceeds to tell him what they said

Canto LX

Sumantra does not succeed in soothing the agony of Kausalya, who tossed
about on the floor due to excess of grief over her separation from
Sri Rama, even though he consoles her by telling her that
Sri Rama, being resolute and high-minded, was
living in the forest free from agony

Canto LXII

When reproached in harsh words by Kausalya, though already feeling
disconsolate through separation from his sons and daughter-in-law,
DaSgaratha falls into a swoon recalling his past sin in the form of
killing a hermit boy, which was responsible for his present
misfortune. On waking from his swoon he tries with folded
hands to conciliate Kausalya and on the latter reciprocating
his sentiments, the Emperor is lulled into a nap

Canto LXII

When reproached in harsh words by Kausalya, though already feeling
disconsolate through separation from his sons and daughter-in-law,
DaSgaratha falls into a swoon recalling his past sin in the form of
killing a hermit boy, which was responsible for his present
misfortune. On waking from his swoon he tries with folded
hands to conciliate Kausalya and on the latter reciprocating
his sentiments, the Emperor is lulled into a nap

Canto LXIII

Waking from sleep after a while and recalling his sinful deed, which spelt
death to him, Dasaratha proceeds to tell Kausalya how, while he was Prince
Regent of Ayodhya, he went out a-hunting in the forest one day and
heard during the last watch of the night the sound of a hermit boy filling
his pitcher with water by submerging into Sarayu river. Mistaking the
gurgling sound for the trumpeting of an elephant, DaSaratha hit the
boy with an arrow, which dug deep into his body and mortally
wounded him. On approaching his quarry he discovered the
fatal blunder and tendered his heartfelt apology to the hermit
boy, who asked him to extract the arrow from his body and
report the matter to his aged parents. The boy died
immediately the arrow was taken out of his body

Canto LXIV

DaSaratha continues to tell Kausalya how, having sought the presence of
the aged parents of the deceased, he apprised them of the sad incident
and escorted them to the river bank, where their son lay dead; how,
clasping the boy to their bosom, they both piteously wailed and
offered libations of water to his spirit for its benefit; how, invested
with an ethereal body, the spirit ascended to heaven while
consoling the aged couple and finally how, having cursed
the king that he too would meet his death in his agony of
separation from his son, the ascetic couple gave up
the ghost and attained the Supreme. Having thus
related the story of his imprecation and loudly
lamenting, the Emperor breathes his last with
the thought of Sri Rama foremost in his mind

Canto LXIX

Finding Bharata oppressed and sad, his friends try to beguile him by means of
music and narration of stories. But when the prince does not return to his
normal mood even then, they inquire of him as to what makes
him pensive and Bharata tells them how he saw
a bad dream the previous night

Canto LXXII

Entering his father’s apartments and not finding him there, Bharata moves to his
mother’s apartments and, having communicated to her the welfare of his
maternal grandfather and others on being questioned by her, inquires of
her the whereabouts of his father. Kaikeyi then breaks to him the
news of his father’s death, attributing it to Sri Rama’s exile and
holding herself responsible for the latter, and after comforting
him calls upon him to get himself installed on the
throne after finishing his father’s obsequies

Canto LXXVII

Having performed with the help of Satrughna all the obsequial rites relating to
his deceased father on the twelfth day after his death, Bharata gifts to the
Brahmanas abundant gold and jewels as a part of the obsequies.
Overwhelmed with grief on reaching the funeral pile in order to pick
up the principal bones of the deceased on the thirteenth day, the
prince drops to the ground. Rolling on the ground, Satrughna
for his part wails in profusion. Comforted by Vasistha and
Sumantra, the two brothers urge the ministers to

expedite the work of picking up the bones

Canto LXXVIII

While Bharata is contemplating to undertake a journey to meet Sri Rama,
Satrughna blames Laksmana for failing to restrain his father and forcibly
prevent Sri Rama’s exile. In the meantime Bharata catches sight of
Manthara standing at the door decked with ornaments and points
to her as the root of all mischief. Satrughna thereupon pounces
upon the maidservant standing in the midst of her
companions and, seizing her by her locks, drags her
on the floor and reproaches Kaikeyi too. On the
latter pleading for mercy on Manthara’s behalf,

Bharata intervenes and Satrughna lets her go

to facilitate his journey to the place of Sri Rama’s abode

The king’s bards, panegyrists and minstrels having commenced eulogizing
Bharata in the customary way in the midst of festal music at sunrise the
following morning, Bharata stops it, disclaiming sovereignty, and
complains to Satrughna of the ill—turn done to the world by his

to be present in the court; and in response to

Bharata in the customary way in the midst of festal music at sunrise the
following morning, Bharata stops it, disclaiming sovereignty, and
complains to Satrughna of the ill—turn done to the world by his
own mother. In the meantime Sage Vasistha enters the royal
court and sends messengers to Bharata urging him
to be present in the court; and in response to
his call Bharata, accompanied by Satrughna
and others, enters the assembly

Canto LXXXII

Sage Vasistha urges Bharata to accept the kingdom bequeathed to him by his
father and elder brother. Bharata, however, scornfully declines the offer and
takes a solemn pledge to depart for the forest and try his level best to
bring his elder brother back to Ayodhya. At his instance
Sumantra gets a chariot ready to take him to the
forest and brings the chariot to his presence

artisans, army and the citizens, and covering a long distance, Bharata
reaches the bank of the holy Ganga at Srigaverapura ruled
by Guha and, encamping the army, breaks journey in
order to do Sraddha and Tarpana in honour of his
deceased father on the bank of the Ganga
and also to give rest to his followers

Canto LXXXIV

Scenting mischief in the mind of Bharata, who was taking a large army
with him, Guha orders the ferrymen to guard the boats so as to prevent
Bharata’s men from crossing the Ganga, and himself seeks the
presence of Bharata in order to know his mind. Being satisfied
about his good intention, he entertains Bharata and his
men with fruits and roots etc., brought by him and,
on his having taken rest awhile and overcome
his fatigue, allows him to advance

Highly praising Guha, Bharata, while departing, inquires of him the way
to the hermitage of Bharadwaja. Promising to follow the prince as
a guide with his servants and placing his misgivings before
him, Guha seeks to know his intentions about Sri Rama
and consoles the prince, who was constantly
bewailing till dusk

Canto LXXXVI

Guha reports to Bharata how Sri Rama and his party broke their journey at
Srngaverapura and reproduces before him the dialogue that took place
between Laksmana and himself at night and further tells him
how the two brothers entangled their locks into a thick
mass, crossed the holy river with Sita and left for
the hermitage of Sage Bharadwaja

Canto LXXXVII

Overwhelmed with grief to hear from the mouth of Guha how his two brothers
got their hair entangled into a mass, Bharata falls unconscious on the
ground. On regaining consciousness, he despatches Guha to reassure
mother Kausalya, who is filled with apprehension about the safety of
Sri Rama and his party on hearing of Bharata’s swoon, and apprise
her of its real cause and also of Sri Rama’s welfare. While doing
so Guha also tells her how Sri Rama and Sita lay down on
a bed of KuSa grass prepared by Laksmana with his own
hands when the party broke journey at Srigaverapura
and how Guha with Laksmana kept watch the whole

night for the protection

Canto LXXXVIII

Showing to Kausalya and others the bed of Kuga grass on which Sri Rama
and Sita spent their night, and contrasting it with the royal splendour and
luxury in which he lived in Ayodhya, Bharata laments for him and,
holding himself responsible for this turn of events and complimenting
Laksmana and Sita for throwing their lot with Sri Rama and
sharing his privations, makes up his mind henceforth to
dwell in the forest as a representative of Sri Rama,

wearing matted locks on his head like him

Bharata proceeds to CitrakUta with his retinue

Canto XCV

In order to humour Sita, Sri Rama gives a description
of the river Mandakini to her

Canto XCVI

While recreating himself in the company of Sita on the lovely hills of Citrakuta,
Sri Rama beholds all of a sudden a large cloud of dust screening the sun
and hears a loud noise and despatches Laksmana to ascertain the
cause. Laksmana thereupon climbs up the top of a lofty tree and
catching sight of an army with the chariot of its leader distinguished
by an ensign bearing the device of a Kovidara tree, hastily
concludes that Bharata has come all the way to dispose of Sri

Rama and thereby to make himself secure on the throne of
Ayodhya. Returning apace to the presence of Sri Rama he
disburdens himself of his suspicions and speaks angrily
about Bharata, swearing that he will kill the intruder

Canto XCVII

Pointing out to Laksmana who was waxing indignant at Bharata, the impropriety
of killing the latter, Sri Rama pacifies him by assuring him of the pious
intentions of Bharata. Ashamed of himself to hear the opinion of his
brother about Bharata, and climbing down the tree, Laksmana
hides his shame by changing the topic. Commanded by
Bharata, who was anxious to avoid causing disturbance
to the hermitage occupied by Sri Rama, the army

encamps roundabout Citrakuta

Canto XCVIII

Telling off Guha and Satrughna with their followers to find out the hermitage of
Sri Rama, Bharata himself, who was keen to see Sri Rama, goes out in
search of his hermitage with the ministers, walking all the way and
envying the good fortune of Sita and Laksmana, who were living with
Sri Rama; and reaching the foot of a sala tree and concluding the
place from the presence of smoke nearby to be a human
habitation and ruling out the possibility of any other human
being living in that lonely forest, he eagerly hastens
forward in the hope of meeting Sri Rama

Canto XCIX

Requesting Vasistha to fetch his mothers and pressing forward, Bharata
reaches the hut, made of leafy twigs, in which the three exiles were
living. While attempting to hail Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana on
seeing them, he stumbles down even on an even ground,
his eyes getting bedimmed with tears at the sight of
their ascetic garb. Sri Rama hastens to lift him up
and clasps him to his bosom. Sumantra and

Guha as well meet Sri Rama and Laksmana

Canto CI

Questioned as to what made him give up the sovereignty of Ayodhya and
enter the forest in the garb of an ascetic, Bharata reproaches his
mother and implores his elder brother to accept the kingship and
return to Ayodhya. Sri Rama, however, pleads that the
command of their father is supreme and must be implicitly
obeyed by both of them, the first part of it enjoining the
exile of the elder brother being binding on him and
the second part bequeathing the kingdom to
Bharata being binding on the latter

Canto CII

Bharata tells Sri Rama that, having been deprived of the privilege of serving
his elder brother, he will have nothing to do with sovereignty and
requests him to offer water to the spirit of his deceased father, who
died with the thought of Sri Rama alone foremost in his mind,
and further implores him to get himself consecrated for the
sovereignty of Ayodhya, the obsequies of their imperial
father having been duly performed by the two

brothers, Bharata and Satrughna, at Ayodhya

Canto CIII

Fallen into a swoon to hear of his father’s ascent to heaven and brought back
to consciousness by Bharata and others, who sprinkle water on him and
employ other such devices, Sri Rama laments in various ways. Comforted
by Bharata and having in his turn consoled Sita and repairing to the
bank of the Ganga, nay, offering water and balls of cooked food to
the spirit of his departed father, he returns to his hut. Hearing
their cry of distress, the troops approach them and are
greeted by Sri Rama according to their rank

Canto CIV

Accompanied by Kausalya and others, Sage Vasistha proceeds to the hermitage
of Sri Rama. Showing to her co-wives the lumps of the pulp of the
Ingudi fruit offered to the spirit of his deceased father by Sri Rama
on blades of Kusa grass placed with their ends pointing to the
south along the bank of the Mandakini on the way, Kausalya
grieves for her deceased husband. Reaching the hermitage,
Kausalya and others see the two brothers, Sri Rama
and Laksmana, fall at their feet. Kausalya bewails
the lot of Sita when the latter falls at her feet.

Bowing down at the feet of his preceptor, Sri
Rama takes his seat. Accompanied by the

counsellors, Bharata too sits nearby

Canto CV

Bharata implores Sri Rama to oblige him by accepting the throne of Ayodhya
being offered by him to Sri Rama with the concurrence of Kaikeyi, even
though bestowed on him by their deceased father. Consoling
Bharata, who held himself responsible for Sri Rama’s exile
to the forest and was languishing with sorrow on that
score, Sri Rama, however, tries to pacify him in
various ways and urges him to shoulder the
burden of rulership

Canto CX

Pacifying Sri Rama, who had got angry with Jabali, Sage Vasistha
urges him to get himself consecrated as king since from
Ikswaku, the progenitor of his race, downward the eldest

son alone had ascended the throne of Ayodhya and
because he was not only the eldest of his
father’s sons, but the ablest too

Canto CXI

Pleading the superiority of a teacher in comparison to one’s parents, Sage
Vasistha by virtue of his superiority and as a concession to Bharata, a
dependant of Sri Rama, whose vow it has been to respect the feelings of
his dependants, urges Sri Rama to grant the prayer of Bharata. Sri
Rama, however, contends that one’s parents are more worthy of
respect even than one’s teacher and is accordingly insistent on
implementing the pledge already given by him to his father. Thus
discomfited in argument, Bharata makes up his mind to undertake
a fast unto death as a last resort to exert pressure on him.
On Sri Rama’s dubbing such a step as repugnant to the
code of conduct prescribed for a Ksatriya, Bharata
requests his elder brother to allow him to remain in
exile in the forest as the latter’s proxy. Sri Rama,

however,

rules out this proposition also as

something preposterous, saying that one’s duty
is entrusted to a proxy only on the former's
incapacity, which could not be predicated
of him, and reaffirms his decision to
enter Ayodhya only after fulfilling
the pledge given to his father

Canto CXII

Collected in the heavens to hear the dialogue of Bharata and Sri Rama,
heavenly beings applaud the two brothers. Having decided upon his course
of action, Sri Rama turns his face towards his hut; while pleading his
inability to protect the kingdom, now that his attempts to take Sri Rama
back to Ayodhya had been frustrated, Bharata prays to Sri Rama for
mercy once more. But when he comes to know that his elder
brother cannot be made to swerve an inch from his vow, he
places a new pair of wooden sandals decked with gold in
front of his brother and requests him to place his feet on
them. Readily acceding to his request, Sri Rama hands
over the sandals to Bharata, who takes a vow before
the former to enter the fire in case he did not return
immediately on the expiry of his term of exile. Sri
Rama confirms his vow and after embracing
Bharata and Satrughna and bidding good-
bye to all with due respect according to
the rank of each, enters his cottage

Canto CXIII

Departed back for Ayodhya alongwith Sage Vasistha and others, Bharata
pays his respects on the way to Sage Bharadwaja and, apprising him
of what had happened at Citrakuta and also of the pair of wooden
sandals that he had received from Sri Rama as the latter's
representative, and proceeding further, crosses the rivers
Ganga and Yamuna alongwith his followers. Departing
from Srigaverapura, he catches sight of Ayodhya
and points out to Sumantra its gloomy appearance

of a number of similes. Bharata enters his father’s palace
with a heavy heart, speaking to Sumantra about the
sorry spectacle which Ayodhya presented
at the moment

on the royal seat, himself puts on the garb of an ascetic
and carries on the rule under orders ascribed to the
sandals, holding the royal umbrella over them

Canto CXVI

Learning from outward signs that the ascetics who lived in the vicinity of
Sri Rama were anxious to shift to some other forest, the latter asks
them why they wished to leave the place. Being told in reply
that they were being molested by Raksasas under the
leadership of Khara, he gives his consent and,
sending them away with great respect,
retires to his own hermitage

Canto CXVII

Afraid of prolonging his stay at Citrakita because of many handicaps,
Sri Rama leaves that mountain and, reaching the hermitage of Sage
Atri, greets the aforesaid Rsi. Glorifying the ascetic life of his wife,
Anasuya, the sage urges Rama to send his consort, Sita, to
her. Kindly receiving the princess, who greeted the
venerable old lady on reaching her, as instructed
by her husband, Anasuya gives her instructions

on the duties of a devoted wife

Canto CXVIII
At the end of her dialogue with Sita, Anasiya requests her to ask a boon
and, highly pleased with her, the hermitess bestows heavenly flowers

and ornaments on her. Urged by Anasiya, She relates to
the hermitess the story of her marriage

Canto CXIX

Glorifying the evening shades, that had now fallen, Anasuya sends away Sita,
decked with the ornaments bestowed on her by herself, to the presence of
Sri Rama. To the latter, who was agreeably surprised to behold Sita
resplendent with heavenly jewels, she relates how she received the
jewels as a loving gift from the hermitess. Accepting the hospitality
of the hermits and staying with them for the night, Sri Rama
asks leave of them in the morning to proceed further
and, following the route shown by the hermits,
the princely party enters the Dandaka forest
